Among those crowding into the stadium that day was schoolboy Peter King, who was destined to play for Worcester and later, at the highest level, for Cardiff City. King is now 70 and living in Spain, but he will never forget that cold winter afternoon at St George's Lane. " City beat Liverpool," he told BBC Hereford & Worcester. " It sounds rather strange nowadays, doesn't it? " Liverpool were in the second division in those days, but nevertheless it was a fantastic victory. " The game couldn't be played on the Saturday as the pitch was waterlogged as per usual, so they played in the midweek and we all had time off school to go to watch it. " I can remember being there with some of my schoolmates and we thought it was an unbelievable day for Worcester City." The teenage King was already starting to attract attention from Football League clubs - Bolton Wanderers and Brentford were among those keeping a watchful eye on him - but it was Cardiff who made the first move. " Although St George's Lane is soon to be lost forever, maybe a new spirit for this great club is about to be found. " After the final whistle blows on Saturday I will leave with a lump in my throat, but also with hope in my heart that the fans will continue to support the club through their Aggborough exile and work together to bring them back to the city of Worcester soon. " As a lifelong City fan who grew up in the shadow of St George's Lane, spending the last few years commentating on every match for BBC Hereford & Worcester has been a pleasure and a privilege. " # Continuation

The connection between a football club and its home ground runs deeper than mere bricks and mortar; it is woven into the very fabric of a community's identity. For Worcester City, St George's Lane has been the stage upon which generations of families have shared moments of joy, heartbreak, and unbridled passion. The stadium has witnessed countless Sunday league players dream of one day gracing its pitch, while seasoned veterans have watched younger talents emerge from the youth academy with stars in their eyes. Moving to Aggborough, though temporary in theory, represents an uncertain chapter in the club's story, one that could easily stretch far longer than anyone currently anticipates. The challenge facing the club's hierarchy will be maintaining momentum and fan engagement while operating from a ground that, despite its facilities, will never truly feel like home. Yet history shows that football clubs possess a remarkable resilience; they adapt, they survive, and they often emerge stronger from adversity than before. The supporters who have stood by Worcester City through lean times and celebrated during golden eras will be the true measure of the club's character in the coming years. Their loyalty will be tested as they make the journey up the A449, but it may also galvanize a sense of unity that transcends the loss of a physical location. In the end, a football club is not defined by its stadium but by the people who fill it with their voices, their dreams, and their unwavering devotion to the colours they wear.

Threatened by deforestation and poaching to supply the black market trade in tiger body parts, it is one of the most endangered cats on the planet. But over the past decade or so, a new threat has emerged - a silent, insidious one - canine distemper virus. Worldwide there are an estimated 400-500 Siberian or Amur tigers. Exact numbers are hard to determine because this is a reclusive animal that is rarely observed. Yet, at the turn of the century, some of these otherwise withdrawn animals were seen wandering onto roads and into villages in the Russian Far East. They were fearless and docile, confused and dazed; the antithesis of their usual ferocious and wary demeanour. Over the next few years evidence mounted that this, the largest living felid, was suffering from an infectious agent and the indications - the nature of the illness, historical understanding of wild cat infections and the diagnosis, in a single wild tiger, of a virus belonging to the morbillivirus genus - provided a likely culprit. A study published in the journal mBio, confirmed these suspicions. Genetic analysis of several dead tiger samples showed that canine distemper virus was the cause. Canine distemper virus (CDV), a relative of the human measles virus, was first described in dogs and infection causes fatal pneumonia and encephalitis (inflammation of the brain). But this virus is incredibly promiscuous and can infect jump into a variety of different animals - usually with catastrophic effects. Two suspected CDV outbreaks, the first in 1988 and a second in 2000, killed thousands of Baikal then Caspian seals. The virus has also ripped through Africa, with fatal outbreaks in silver-backed jackals and bat-eared foxes and catastrophic die-offs in wild dog populations that continue to this day. For years, cats were thought resistant to CDV. Yes, domestic cats could be infected in the laboratory, but this was inefficient and the virus was unable to pass from one animal to another. A massive demise in 1994 of African lions living in the Serengeti national park in Tanzania showed that this was fallacy. # Continuation

The outbreak killed nearly a third of the lion population in that ecosystem, a devastating loss that fundamentally altered our understanding of the virus's reach. Researchers investigating the die-off discovered that CDV was the culprit, demonstrating for the first time that wild felids were not only susceptible to infection but could transmit the virus among themselves. This discovery sent shockwaves through the conservation community, as it became clear that cats of all sizes and species were potentially vulnerable to this pathogen. The implications for the already fragile Siberian tiger population were immediately apparent and deeply troubling. With fewer than 500 individuals scattered across a vast and fragmented landscape, any disease outbreak posed an existential threat to the species' survival. The tigers' isolated populations meant that genetic diversity was already dangerously low, leaving them with weakened immune systems and reduced ability to fight off infections. Furthermore, the virus could potentially spread through the tiger population via shared prey species or contact with infected domestic dogs in nearby villages. Conservation efforts that had slowly begun to stabilize tiger numbers in the early 2000s suddenly faced an invisible enemy that couldn't be hunted or fenced out. Scientists began urgent research into vaccination strategies and disease surveillance programs to monitor and potentially prevent further outbreaks. The challenge lay in delivering vaccines to wild animals spread across remote, inaccessible territories in the Russian Far East. International cooperation between Russian, Chinese, and Western researchers became essential to mount an effective response. This convergence of traditional threats—habitat loss, poaching, and human encroachment—with emerging infectious diseases represented a new frontier in wildlife conservation challenges. The Siberian tiger's plight had become emblematic of a larger crisis facing endangered species worldwide as our warming, increasingly fragmented world created conditions ripe for zoonotic disease spillover.

Three years after Russia annexed Crimea, a move bitterly contested by Ukraine's government, the region remains in a state of flux. It's difficult to get into, and for many people, it's difficult to know where it's going. At Kiev International Airport, I hand my passport to a border guard. " Purpose of visit?" he asks. " Journalism. I'm with the BBC." He pauses. He studies my passport. He seems to be checking a list. He goes to pick up a telephone and asks a question. He does not realise I can hear. " You remember that pro-Russian journalist from the BBC? Was his surname Rosenberg?" Another pause. " It wasn't? # Continuation

Then he turns back to me and waves me through without another word. I collect my luggage and head toward the exit, unsure whether I've just passed some kind of test or narrowly avoided something worse. Outside the terminal, the Kyiv air is cold and sharp, carrying the metallic tang of diesel fuel and winter. My fixer, Andriy, is waiting by a battered silver Lada, smoking a cigarette with the intensity of someone who needs it. He nods at me and doesn't ask about the border interaction—he's been through enough of these encounters himself to know when not to ask questions. We drive through the outskirts of the capital, past Soviet-era apartment blocks and construction sites frozen mid-project, their skeletal frames silhouetted against the grey sky. The roads are potholed and uneven, evidence of wear and neglect that seems to have accelerated since the conflict began. Andriy tells me that getting into Crimea will be more complicated than I'd anticipated. The Russian authorities have tightened security, he explains, and Western journalists are increasingly unwelcome. Still, he has contacts—people who owe him favors, people who might be willing to take risks. We need to move carefully, he warns, and we need to move fast. The window for access, he says, is closing. I ask him how long we have. He shrugs and lights another cigarette. " Days, maybe a week," he says. " After that, I don't know."

But with the lowest turnout in the constituency's history and a margin of 2,620 the narrowest ever recorded in this once proud Labour heartland, can this declaration really be believed? It's a bright and windy day in Stoke-on-Trent. The town has been at the centre of the political spotlight for weeks now, but people seem more interested in discussing the aftermath of Storm Doris than the small matter of Stoke Central's by-election result. This staunch working class community was once passionately behind Labour. In 1951, more than 60,000 went to the polls - 34,260 backing Labour. Rather than feel victorious about Gareth Snell's win, Labour voters willing to talk simply seem relieved Paul Nuttall didn't clinch it. In all, 7,853 voted for Mr Snell, compared to 5,233 who wanted UKIP to win. Wendy Wright, 51, from Newcastle-under-Lyme, thought the vote for UKIP may have been higher had it not been for its disastrous campaign. " I'm not surprised Labour won because of the way UKIP handled the campaign," she said. " The people of Stoke seemed to think he [Paul Nuttall] didn't come from the area and they didn't believe his pack of lies. " It's a relief UKIP didn't get in but [Labour retaining the seat] just means things are going to stay the same." Ms Wright said there had not been a high turnout because "people aren't interested in politics". She said: "They've kind of lost faith and lost hope. They think, what's the point, nothing's going to change. " It's a deprived area and it's a shame because the people are so welcoming and friendly." Veronica Millington, 71, from Blythe Bridge, said she and husband Anthony, 75, were "Labour through and through". # Continuation

"We've always voted Labour," she explained, settling onto a nearby bench. " My father worked down the mines, and Labour was the party that looked after working people like us. That's just how it's always been." Yet even her unwavering loyalty seemed tinged with uncertainty about what Labour's victory actually meant for the town's future. Around the corner, at a local café, opinions were more divided. A group of younger residents expressed frustration that neither major party seemed to offer real solutions to Stoke's economic struggles. " They all make promises during elections," one man remarked, "but once they're in, nothing changes for us." The narrow victory margin had clearly rattled the Labour establishment, forcing them to confront uncomfortable questions about their grip on traditional strongholds. Political analysts were already suggesting that UKIP's strong showing, despite Nuttall's missteps, reflected deeper anxieties about immigration and economic decline that Labour had failed to adequately address. Some observers wondered whether the low turnout itself was the most telling result – a silent protest from voters who felt abandoned by all parties. As the afternoon wore on and Storm Doris continued to batter the Potteries, it seemed the election had already faded from public consciousness. For many in Stoke, the storm outside felt more immediate and real than the political upheaval that had dominated the headlines. The question now was whether Labour would recognize this result as a wake-up call or simply celebrate survival.

Mr Miliband called the prime minister's refusal to take part unless the Green Party was involved a "pathetic excuse". He said he would debate with "anyone invited by the broadcasters". But Mr Cameron said the Labour leader was "chickening" out of facing the Greens and all "national parties" must be represented. The exchanges came after Mr Miliband, Lib Dem leader Nick Clegg and UKIP leader Nigel Farage urged broadcasters to press ahead with televised debates before the 7 May poll even if Mr Cameron refuses to take part. The three leaders have written to Mr Cameron saying it would be "unacceptable" for the prime minister to refuse to appear, saying an empty podium should be provided in his absence. In response, the BBC, Sky News, ITV and Channel 4 said they "remain committed" to staging debates before the poll on 7 May. Raising the issue in Parliament, Mr Miliband said Mr Cameron had argued back in 2010 that it would be "feeble" for any party leader to walk away from the debates but was now threatening to do just that. A high stakes poker game in which the ultimate prize is power - that is what the row about TV leaders election debate has now become. The players at the table are not just the nation's party leaders but also the bosses of the country's biggest broadcasters. Watching and waiting are highly paid lawyers who know that this may end up being resolve by a judge and not by the court of public opinion. The question is - will anyone blink before it gets to the court-room? Read more from Nick "It is not for him, it is not for me, it is not for any party leader to decide who is in the debate," he said. " It is up to the broadcasters, that is the country we live in. " I think he does protest too much. He has run out of excuses, he is running scared of these debates and in the words of his heroine Margaret Thatcher 'he is frit'". Mr Cameron said Mr Miliband wanted "to debate having a debate" because he did not want to talk about the economy and other issues. # Continuation

The prime minister's team insisted that Mr Cameron was simply standing firm on a matter of principle regarding which parties should be included in the debates. They argued that excluding the Green Party, while including UKIP, would be inconsistent and unfair to smaller parties seeking representation. The debate about debates had already begun to dominate the news cycle, overshadowing discussions of actual policy differences between the parties. Political analysts noted that both sides appeared to be playing a strategic game, each trying to position themselves as the reasonable party while painting their opponents as obstructionist. The broadcasters found themselves in an increasingly difficult position, caught between competing demands from powerful political figures and their own editorial standards. Some commentators suggested that the entire row was playing into Mr Cameron's hands by diverting attention from Labour's economic arguments. Others countered that Mr Miliband had successfully put the prime minister on the defensive by challenging his credibility and consistency. The stakes continued to rise as the election drew closer and the broadcasters prepared to make their final decision about the format and participants. Behind the scenes, negotiations were reportedly ongoing between the parties and media organisations, though little progress appeared to be made. The legal experts hovering in the background suggested that judicial review might become necessary if the parties could not reach agreement. Meanwhile, the public watched this spectacle with a mixture of bemusement and frustration at what many saw as political theatre obscuring the real issues. The question of who would ultimately control the debate format remained unresolved, with both Mr Cameron and Mr Miliband showing no signs of backing down. The television networks reiterated their commitment to holding debates but remained silent on exactly how they would proceed if Mr Cameron continued his refusal.

The phrase "rapid rise through the ranks" is a standard line in profiles of political leaders. But it's rarely as speedy as in Natalie Bennett's case. The Australian-born former Guardian journalist woke up on New Year's Day 2006 and decided she wanted to do something to change the world. After pondering her options (should she get involved with an NGO or the UN?) she joined the Green Party. Within six short years she had become its leader. Three years after that she was sharing a stage with the prime minister and other party leaders in a televised general election debate. Her sudden arrival on the frontline of British politics was all the more remarkable because she had never won an election, other than the one that had made her party leader. Her first aim when she took on the job was to insert the Green Party - routinely ignored or patronised by the mainstream media - into the national conversation. But when media outlets finally began calling, on the back of a surge in support for the party, she sometimes found it difficult to get her message across in a suitably snappy way. Her vision for the party was to show how "economic and environmental justice are indivisible", she explains, "the idea that tackling environmental problems isn't an add-on luxury". It was "not a easy concept to get across", she confesses. " I still don't think I've got the 12 second soundbite. But I am going to keep working on it." " It's a classic Shakespearean thing," she says of her struggles with the broadcast media. " It's both my strength and my weakness that I answer the question." # Continuation

Bennett's earnestness, while admirable, sometimes worked against her in an industry that rewards pithy zingers and carefully crafted talking points. She would launch into nuanced explanations of policy when what producers wanted were memorable quotes they could loop endlessly on the evening news. Her thoughtful, academic approach to complex issues didn't always translate into the kind of theatrical performance that television demanded. Yet this very quality—her refusal to reduce intricate problems into simplistic slogans—resonated with a growing segment of the electorate who felt alienated by conventional politics. The Green Party under her leadership began to attract younger voters and those disillusioned with the traditional left-right paradigm. Support climbed steadily through local elections and by-elections, suggesting that her message was getting through despite the media's initial dismissiveness. She became something of a polarizing figure: celebrated by supporters as a refreshingly honest voice in politics, while critics dismissed her as inarticulate and unprepared for high office. Bennett remained undeterred by the criticism, viewing it as the inevitable cost of challenging the status quo. She continued to develop her arguments, refining her delivery while maintaining her commitment to substance over spin. Her tenure as Green Party leader would ultimately be brief, but it marked a turning point in how environmental issues were discussed in mainstream British politics. The party's surge in membership during her time at the helm suggested that people were hungry for a different kind of political conversation. Whether she had found her perfect soundbite or not, Bennett had succeeded in her primary goal: making the Green Party impossible to ignore.

This is an edited version of the session. Question from Sean on Facebook: Why is #Florida always the last to announce the results? Can't they count there? Katty answers: They can count in two languages there! It's one of closest fought, most populous states so it takes longer Question from @SHSPolitics: How have Dems been able to increase their senate seats? Is it due to akin/mourdock? Katty answers: Yes. The GOP didn't do itself any favours with those two candidates Question from @errant1977: Jeb Bush for GOP run in 2016? adage says without "Bush or Nixon on the ticket" they've never won since 28? Katty answers: Hmm. In their dreams, yes. He can expand the party but his last name still isn't Smith and he's told me that's an issue Question from @alyssaweis: How do you feel the legalization of marijuana in certain states will affect the rest of the nation? Katty answers: For me, question is whether this is like gay marriage, part of a social/demographic trend - I'm not sure yet Question from @lizrob92:Where do you think Mitt will go from here? esp. considering his own people in Mass. didn't vote for him? Katty answers: He'll get some sleep, spend time with his family and then look at his future. He's got a lot of options on the table—business, potentially a return to Massachusetts politics, or staying involved in national Republican circles. The loss was painful, but he's a resilient guy and he's got the resources to figure out what's next. I think what's interesting is that he ran such a disciplined campaign, and despite that, the broader forces were just working against him. The economy was improving, incumbents have a natural advantage, and Obama's team was exceptionally well-organized. For Romney personally, I suspect he'll want to rehabilitate his image a bit after some of the controversies from the campaign trail. He's still got credibility on business and economic issues, so he might position himself as an elder statesman of sorts. Whether the Republican Party wants to listen to him is another question entirely. Right now they're going to be doing a lot of soul-searching about where they went wrong and what needs to change. The demographic shifts are real—they can't ignore the Latino vote any longer, and women's issues clearly mattered more than some GOP strategists anticipated. Moving forward, the party will need candidates who can speak to modern America while still maintaining conservative principles. It's a delicate balance, and it's not clear who in the Republican Party can pull that off right now. For Romney specifically, I'd expect him to take a step back from the spotlight for a while and let the dust settle.

She was 12 at the time and living with her parents and two siblings in northern Peru. On that night, two officials came to their home and took away her father. Mr Katsura, who owned a small general store, was arrested because he was part of Peru's prosperous Japanese community. " My father told them he hadn't done anything wrong, but they didn't listen to him," she recalls. Japanese people began migrating to Peru in considerable numbers at the end of the 19th Century, drawn by opportunities to work in the mines and on sugar plantations. By the 1940s, an estimated 25,000 people of Japanese descent lived in Peru. Many had become lawyers and doctors, or owned small businesses. Their prosperity, further fuelled by racism, soon triggered anti-Japanese sentiment in Peru, Stephanie Moore explains. Ms Moore, a scholar at the Japanese Peruvian Oral History Project, says after the outbreak of World War Two, the Japanese community in Peru became a target, and their assets were confiscated. " In May 1940, as many as 600 houses, schools and businesses belonging to citizens of Japanese descent were burned down," she says. Following Japan's 1941 attack on the US naval base at Pearl Harbor in Hawaii, the US government asked a dozen Latin American countries, among them Peru, to arrest its Japanese residents. Records from the time suggest the US authorities wanted to take them to the US and use them as bargaining chips for its nationals captured by Japanese forces in Asia. Mr Katsura was among the 2,200 Latin Americans of Japanese descent who were forcibly deported to internment camps in the US. Blanca Katsura, who is now 83 and lives in Northern California, remembers how she learned of his fate. " A month after my father was detained, he sent me a letter because it was my birthday," she recalls. " He had been taken to Panama from where they were planning to send him to the US," she adds. # Continuation

The letter arrived crumpled and worn, bearing stamps from multiple countries and the marks of countless hands. In it, her father had written words of encouragement, telling her to be strong and care for her mother and siblings. He promised that this separation would be temporary, that he would return once the war ended and the misunderstandings were cleared up. But months turned into years, and his letters became less frequent as the reality of his situation settled in. Blanca watched her mother struggle to keep the family together while managing the store alone and dealing with the suspicion that now surrounded their household. Other Japanese families in their community were experiencing similar traumas, creating an unspoken bond of shared suffering among them. The internment camps where her father was held were overcrowded and poorly managed, conditions that would haunt him for the rest of his life. He spent four years separated from his family, four years of uncertainty about whether he would ever see them again. When he was finally released in 1945, after Japan's surrender, he was a changed man—quieter, more withdrawn, bearing invisible scars that no one could fully understand. The family eventually relocated to California, seeking a fresh start away from the painful memories of Peru. Blanca's father never fully recovered from his imprisonment, though he worked hard to rebuild their life in America. She grew up determined to ensure that this dark chapter of history would not be forgotten, that the stories of the thousands who suffered would be preserved for future generations. Today, she speaks publicly about her family's experience, helping to educate others about this little-known episode of American and Latin American history. Her father passed away many years ago, but through her efforts, his memory and the memory of so many others continues to live on.

Integration with the mainland has brought Hong Kong much success as a global financial centre but it has also created problems. Those problems seem to be at their worst at any time since the handover, according to a series of surveys and opinion polls. The gap between Hong Kong's rich and poor is at a 30-year high, property rental prices have risen to record levels and residents are upset by daily news reports on corrupt lives of officials at the highest levels of Chinese government. An independent poll shows the Hong Kong public's mistrust of the Chinese government stands at a post handover high, largely because of a number of political scandals. These include the sacking of senior Communist Party official Bo Xilai, the plight of human rights activist Chen Guangcheng and, most seriously, the suspicious death of Tiananmen Square dissident Li Wangyang in June. " What happens in China very closely affects Hong Kong people, because we're all ethnic Chinese," said Frank Lee, a pollster at the University of Hong Kong's public opinion programme. " That's probably the reason why, at this moment, many people don't trust the Beijing government. In fact, anger is at the peak right now." Despite a relatively strong economy and a string of financial incentives announced by Beijing to boost popular support in the semi-autonomous city, Hong Kong is a much more restive place than it was five years ago, when Mr Hu last came to visit, in a blaze of pre-Olympic glory. Ahead of the visit, Hong Kong's census bureau publicised data that showed inequality had widened to its highest level in three decades. According to US government rankings, Hong Kong's wealth gap now outstrips all other developed nations. It ranks behind highly unequal countries such as Colombia, Haiti and Sierra Leone. Monthly incomes for Hong Kong's poorest have fallen by about 8% over the past five years, while salaries for the richest have grown by about 25%, according to the newest census data. Sze Lai Shan, a social worker with the Society for Community Organisation, said as a result of integration with the mainland, Hong Kong's economic structure had changed. " The factories have moved to mainland China, and the government believes in the trickle-down theory where everyone benefits from overall economic development. So they have done little for the poor," she said during a visit to a cramped sub-divided flat. # Continuation

The reality on the ground tells a starkly different story from official narratives of shared prosperity. In neighborhoods like Mong Kok and Sham Shui Po, families are crowded into subdivided apartments no larger than parking spaces, paying exorbitant rents that consume more than half their monthly income. These micro-units, some measuring barely 100 square feet, have become symbols of Hong Kong's housing crisis and the failure of government policies to address the needs of ordinary residents. Young people, unable to afford homes of their own, face an uncertain future and increasingly question whether Hong Kong still offers them opportunities. The government's reliance on property development as an economic engine has only enriched developers and landlords while pricing out the middle class entirely. Meanwhile, Beijing's political appointees and their allies have accumulated vast fortunes through business dealings that many Hong Kong residents view as corrupt or at least deeply troubling. The disconnect between the vision of integration promoted by officials and the lived experience of struggling families grows wider each year. Public anger is no longer confined to whispered conversations; it spills into the streets through demonstrations and protests that authorities struggle to contain. Educational institutions, once bastions of free inquiry, face increasing pressure to align with Beijing's ideological preferences, alarming parents and academics alike. The promise that "one country, two systems" would preserve Hong Kong's unique character and freedoms now rings hollow to many who witness the gradual erosion of both. Business elites, focused on mainland profits, have largely abandoned their historical role as advocates for Hong Kong's interests and autonomy. This vacuum of leadership from traditional sources has created space for younger, more radical voices demanding genuine change. Unless the government acknowledges these grievances seriously and implements meaningful reforms, Hong Kong risks becoming just another Chinese city, stripped of the very qualities that made it exceptional.

Media playback is not supported on this device Selby, 33, had trailed 10-4 but claimed nine out of 10 frames to lead 13-11. Higgins had a mini revival helped by a contentious refereeing decision, but Selby kept his composure to win. The world number one is only the fourth player after Steve Davis, Stephen Hendry and Ronnie O'Sullivan to claim back-to-back titles in the modern era. The Englishman picks up a record £375,000 in prize money, retains the top ranking spot for the 116th consecutive week and gains revenge for the defeat by Higgins in the 2007 final. No player had come back to win from a greater deficit than six frames in a World Championship final since Dennis Taylor trailed Steve Davis by 8-0 and 9-1 in their 1985 classic. " I can't believe it, I am still pinching myself now," said Selby. " From 10-4 to get to 10-7 yesterday, I was over the moon as I had nothing left. He outplayed me yesterday. Today I came back fresh and was a lot better. " When I was 10-4 down I was missing everything and had nothing left. I said 'pull something together'. If you lose, you want to at least go down fighting. " To have three world titles is unbelievable and to be one of only four players to defend it is something I could only dream of." Media playback is not supported on this device Selby was 47-0 up in the 31st frame, and leading 16-14 on frames, when he potted a red before attempting to roll up to the black ball. It was unclear whether the balls touched and referee Jan Verhaas called a foul. Selby questioned the decision and score marker Brendan Moore checked the incident on a TV, initially saying the cueball had hit. # Continuation

However, after further review, the decision stood as called. This controversial moment proved crucial as Higgins capitalised on the reprieve to narrow the gap. The tension mounted as both players fought desperately for every frame in the closing stages. Selby's mental strength was tested like never before, but he refused to buckle under the pressure. In the end, his superior consistency and match awareness prevailed over Higgins' desperate fightback. The victory represented not just a triumph of skill, but also of character and resilience under the most demanding circumstances. For Selby, the comeback from 10-4 down will be remembered as one of the greatest achievements of his career. His ability to regroup after a disastrous opening session demonstrated why he had earned the world number one ranking. The win also silenced any remaining doubters who questioned whether he could handle the pressure of defending his title. Higgins, despite the loss, had shown glimpses of the brilliance that made him a worthy opponent in the final. The controversial refereeing decision would likely be debated for years to come among snooker enthusiasts. Nevertheless, Selby's overall performance across the match was undeniably superior. He had proven once again why he belongs among the elite players who have dominated world snooker. The Englishman would now look ahead to defending his ranking and pursuing further titles in the seasons ahead.

Mr Modi coaxed the United Nations into declaring 21 June International Yoga Day and announced a grand event to be held in Delhi on that day. His government plans to get tens of thousands of people to perform yoga in the heart of the capital on Sunday. Mr Modi is an enthusiast himself - he told a biographer he tries to do yoga for an hour after waking up in the morning. So what does Sunday's mass yoga session tell us about India? Mr Modi's plan to get 35,000 people to perform yoga for 35 minutes on the stately Rajpath (King's Avenue) is aimed at setting a new Guinness World Record. The Guinness people have been invited to document the largest yoga class at a single venue. Indians already flaunt a number of yoga-related world records: the largest yoga class, longest yoga chain, longest yoga marathon and longest yoga class at multiple venues. Inspired by Mr Modi's call, even India's National Cadet Corps want to set another record on Sunday - "the largest ever demonstration of yoga by a uniformed organisation on a single day concurrently at multiple venues all across India". It says a million cadets will simultaneously do yoga at 1,900 locations. Yes, we are crazy about breaking world records. A reality check: Indians applied for some 3,000 records with Guinness World Records in 2013, just behind record-seekers from the US and Britain. The number of Indian record holders has risen an astonishing 250% in the past five years. It helps that we are the world's second-most populous nation, so we have also set records in things like the biggest blood donation drive and most number of people shaking hands. Writer Samanth Subramanian describes this as a "fevered subculture of record-setters". If a place in the Guinness World Records looks remote, no worries. We have our thriving, home-grown Limca Book of World Records and India Book of Records to take care of our achievements. # Continuation

This obsession with record-breaking reflects a deeper cultural phenomenon in modern India—a desire to assert national pride and global relevance through quantifiable achievements. When millions gather to perform yoga simultaneously, it sends a powerful message about India's soft power and its ability to mobilize its vast population for a unified purpose. The symbolism is particularly potent given yoga's ancient roots in Indian philosophy and spirituality; by internationalizing it through world records, Mr Modi positions India as the custodian of a timeless practice. Critics, however, argue that this focus on breaking records sometimes overshadows the actual substance and spiritual essence of these activities. A mass yoga session lasting only 35 minutes, they contend, may generate impressive headlines but does little to promote genuine wellness or understanding of yoga's deeper principles. Yet there's undeniable appeal in such grand spectacles—they capture public imagination and create moments of collective achievement that transcend ordinary life. The logistics alone are staggering: coordinating tens of thousands of people, ensuring proper instruction, managing the infrastructure on Rajpath, and maintaining safety standards requires meticulous planning. Social media will undoubtedly amplify the event, with images and videos spreading across the globe within minutes of the session's completion. For many participating Indians, the experience represents not just a world record attempt but a celebration of their heritage and a chance to be part of something historically significant. Whether this translates into sustained interest in yoga practice or remains a one-day phenomenon remains to be seen. Regardless, Sunday's event promises to be a spectacle that only a nation of India's scale and ambition could orchestrate, cementing yet another chapter in the country's ever-growing collection of record-breaking achievements.

The IPCC - weren't they the ones who said the Himalayan glaciers would disappear by 2035? How can we trust what they say this time? The weight given to the reports of the IPCC is a measure of the global scale of scientific involvement with the panel. Divided into three working groups that look at the physical science, the impacts and options to limit climate change, the panel involves thousands of scientists around the world. The first report, to be presented in Stockholm, has 209 lead authors and 50 review editors from 39 countries. The 30-page Summary for Policymakers that will be published after review by government officials in the Swedish capital is based on around 9,000 peer-reviewed scientific papers and 50,000 comments from the expert reviewers. But among these icebergs of data, things can and do go awry. In the last report, published in 2007, there were a handful of well publicised errors, including the claim that Himalayan glaciers would disappear by 2035. The wrong percentage was also given for the amount of land in the Netherlands under sea level. The IPCC admitted it had got it wrong and explained that, in a report running to 3,000 pages, there were bound to be some mistakes. The Himalayan claim came from the inclusion of an interview that had been published in the magazine New Scientist. In its own words, the IPCC is there "to provide the world with a clear scientific view on the current state of knowledge in climate change and its potential environmental and socio-economic impacts". The offspring of two UN bodies, the World Meteorological Organization and the United Nations Environment Programme, it has issued four heavyweight assessment reports to date on the state of the climate. These are commissioned by the governments of 195 countries, essentially the entire world. These reports are critical in informing the climate policies adopted by these governments. The IPCC itself is a small organisation, run from Geneva with a full time staff of 12. # Continuation

The organization relies heavily on the voluntary contributions of thousands of scientists who donate their time and expertise to review the latest research and synthesize findings. This distributed model, while democratizing the scientific process, also introduces vulnerabilities where non-peer-reviewed sources can occasionally slip through the cracks, as happened with the Himalayan glacier claim. Since that embarrassment, the IPCC has implemented more rigorous vetting procedures and stricter guidelines for source material inclusion. The organization now maintains a detailed errata document and has established clearer protocols for distinguishing between different levels of scientific certainty in its findings. Despite these improvements, critics argue that the IPCC remains inherently conservative in its assessments, often lagging behind the latest cutting-edge research due to the time required to compile and review thousands of studies. Conversely, some observers contend that media coverage of occasional errors has been disproportionate, overshadowing the overall accuracy and comprehensiveness of the reports. The reality is that the IPCC's work represents the most extensive consensus-building exercise in science, bringing together competing viewpoints and evidence to reach broad agreement. When thousands of scientists from dozens of countries converge on similar conclusions about climate change, the weight of that convergence carries significant meaning. The 2035 Himalayan glacier error, while regrettable, represents a tiny fraction of the vast body of work produced across thousands of pages and multiple reports. What matters most is whether the core findings—that the climate is warming and human activities are the primary cause—hold up under scrutiny. The scientific evidence supporting these conclusions has only strengthened since 2007, with more data, better models, and clearer observations from around the globe. Understanding the IPCC's limitations and occasional missteps need not undermine confidence in its fundamental conclusions about the direction and magnitude of climate change.

Andrew Davies nodded against the crossbar as County threatened early in the match. Another header, by Michael Gardyne, was kept out superbly by Accies goalkeeper Remi Matthews and the save prompted the attack that gave Hamilton the lead. The hosts countered and Imrie clipped the ball past Scott Fox. With playmaker Ali Crawford sidelined after suffering a concussion in training on Friday, more pressure was on 33-year-old Dougie Imrie to provide the attacking spark. And he did that, squeezing in his first goal in the league this season and fourth overall from a few yards out. Danny Redmond's introduction almost led to more Accies goals as he played in Eamonn Brophy and Imrie again, but County goalkeeper Scott Fox was equal to their efforts. Hamilton's midfield has been boosted by former Celtic and Bari man Massimo Donati, but there was an equally effective performance by a less glamorous grafter in the shape of ex-Clydebank player Darian MacKinnon. The 30-year-old is approaching the 150-appearance mark for Accies since joining from the junior ranks in 2012 and excels against teams with Ross County's physicality. MacKinnon defied any notion that his sole purpose is breaking up the play, though. On several occasions the midfielder - flanked by impressive youngster Greg Docherty, Gramoz Kurtaj - was a crucial cog in starting or continuing counter attacks. Each of County's main strikers - Liam Boyce, Craig Curran and Alex Schalk - had scored already this term and manager Jim McIntyre said pre-match that he expected that to continue in South Lanarkshire if the supply was right. When Davies' header from Martin Woods' corner hit the crossbar after just two minutes, it looked like finding the back of the net would not be a problem. But the supply line to the strikers appeared to be blocked as County struggled to create chances from that point forward. Gardyne's header was well saved by Hamilton keeper Matthews in what was the Dingwall men's other clear-cut opportunity. Accies player-manager Martin Canning: "Today was justification for the players in that if you continue to put that work in and be brave, then you'll win games. " Greg Docherty and Darian MacKinnon in the middle of the park were excellent - they go through some amount of work. # Continuation

The defensive solidity that Hamilton displayed was a stark contrast to some of their earlier performances this season, where they had conceded cheaply on multiple occasions. MacKinnon's experience proved invaluable in reading the game and positioning himself to intercept County's passing lanes before they could develop into genuine threats. Ross County pushed for an equalizer as the match progressed, but their frustration grew with each failed attempt to break down the resolute Accies defence. Canning's tactical setup ensured that his team remained compact and disciplined, making it difficult for McIntyre's side to find space in the final third. The introduction of Redmond had provided Hamilton with additional pace and energy in attack, allowing them to stretch County's back line on the counter. Fox made a crucial save from Brophy late in the second half that could have sealed the victory earlier and given the hosts breathing room. County's inability to convert their early dominance into goals ultimately proved costly, as they left themselves vulnerable to Hamilton's swift breaks. The three points lifted Hamilton up the table and provided them with renewed confidence heading into their next fixture. For County, it represented a missed opportunity to climb closer to the top of the division and maintain their push for promotion. Canning's men had shown character and resilience throughout, qualities that would be essential if they were to maintain this winning form in the weeks ahead. The victory was built on solid foundations rather than spectacular attacking play, but in football, results matter more than the manner in which they are achieved. As the final whistle sounded, the Hamilton players celebrated a hard-earned win that suggested their season could yet flourish despite the early season injuries.

Wenger has big problems at the back and I really fear for his side if he does not bring Laurent Koscielny back into central defence on Saturday. The way young duo Calum Chambers and Rob Holding performed there in last week's defeat by Liverpool means there is a massive question mark over them, and Wenger's decision to play them. Media playback is not supported on this device Some Gunners supporters are already unhappy and, if this game also goes the wrong way, they are going to become even more irate. That is really unhelpful for their players at this stage of the season when they are trying to gain momentum and confidence, so Wenger has a big call to make - and he has to get it right. Arsenal were the only team to beat Leicester home and away last season but I cannot see them dominating the Foxes this time. It will be end to end because the Gunners are not capable of shutting up shop. They will have a go at Claudio Ranieri's side, which of course plays into their hands. On their day, Arsenal's free-flowing football is good enough to destroy anyone and it does not matter who they play at the back. However, they looked fragile with Chambers and Holding playing together and there was definitely a lack of confidence in their defence, which is why Koscielny has to return this time. I would be amazed if he doesn't. Media playback is not supported on this device Koscielny is not injured, just being rested after returning to training late after Euro 2016 and, even if he is only 80% fit, I would play him. In his position, he does not have to cover as much ground as other outfield players and he could get through the game. As a player, if I was in his situation I would be hammering on the manager's door saying, 'you need me, I can play, stick me in'. But maybe he is saying that, and Wenger is still not picking him because he thinks he is not ready physically. If that is the case Wenger will have to take responsibility if things go wrong again. Arsenal played a high-pressing game in the first half against Liverpool but they could not sustain that tempo for 90 minutes - it is virtually impossible for any team. The intensity required for that kind of football takes a tremendous toll on the players, and you need the quality in defence to back it up when you inevitably drop deeper. Against Leicester, Arsenal will need to be smarter about when they press and when they sit off, because the Foxes have players like N'Golo Kanté and Riyad Mahrez who can punish you on the counter-attack if you are not careful. Wenger's tactical flexibility will be crucial here, and I am not entirely convinced he has shown that consistently enough this season. The manager needs to set his team up in a way that protects them while still allowing them to express themselves going forward. If Arsenal go gung-ho from the start and leave themselves exposed at the back, Leicester will have chances to hurt them, and those chances could be costly. The Gunners cannot afford another defeat, especially not at home where their supporters will lose patience completely. Winning this game could shift the narrative around the club and give everyone a much-needed boost. Conversely, a loss could see the pressure mount on Wenger in a way that becomes difficult to manage. Leicester will come to the Emirates with confidence and a clear game plan, knowing that they can exploit any weaknesses in the Arsenal defence. It will be a fascinating contest between two sides with very different trajectories this season. The outcome could have significant implications for both teams' aspirations in the title race and European football. Ultimately, it comes down to whether Wenger makes the right decisions in the next few days.

There was a time here when tribal Tibetans roamed across a vast dramatic landscape with no specific place to call home. For generation after generation they had lived as nomads, sleeping where they made camp. They kept their livestock moving, chasing the fresh pastures that became available as the seasons changed. The limits of their territory were identified by mountains and rivers, and their nomadic existence permeated all aspects of their culture. In 2016, you might expect this lifestyle to have been fully extinguished, yet it hasn't been. Not quite. However, what's left of it is now coming under considerable pressure. We set out to visit one Tibetan community in Aba region. This place came to world attention in recent years as the centre of a wave of self-immolation suicide protests. In Tibetan towns, nearly 150 people, mostly monks and nuns, set fire to themselves in protest at the impact of Beijing's rule - the largest number of them in Aba. For this reason, security has been tight in the area for years. But, as the self-immolations have slowed, we hoped to be better able to reach remote communities and speak to people. The central Chinese province of Sichuan - of which Aba is a district - is quintessential China. It's the home of pandas and spicy food. If you look at a map its virtually in the middle of the country. Yet if you drive out of the regional capital Chengdu and head west, it's not long before you enter another world. The landscape transforms dramatically as you climb higher into the mountains, the air growing thinner and colder with each passing kilometer. The cities give way to small towns, then to villages that seem to cling precariously to steep hillsides. Prayer flags begin appearing more frequently, their bright colors fluttering against the grey stone and sparse vegetation. You start to see fewer Han Chinese faces and hear Tibetan spoken more often than Mandarin in the streets. The architecture shifts too—traditional Tibetan buildings with their distinctive sloped roofs and ornate wooden decorations replace the uniform concrete blocks of the lowlands. After several hours of driving through increasingly remote territory, you begin to understand why this region has maintained such a distinct cultural identity despite centuries of external pressure. The isolation is not just geographical but also spiritual—this is a landscape that feels fundamentally different from the rest of China, a place where ancient traditions still hold genuine power. When we finally reached the nomadic settlement we had traveled so far to find, we were struck by how small and vulnerable it appeared against the vast emptiness surrounding it. The handful of traditional black yak-hair tents seemed almost insignificant beneath the towering peaks that rose on all sides. Yet within those tents lived families who represented a way of life that had endured for countless generations. Our guides explained that government policies had been gradually restricting the freedom of nomadic herders to move across the grasslands as they wished. Many families had been pressured or incentivized to settle in permanent settlements, abandoning their mobile lifestyle for fixed homes. Those who remained nomadic faced increasing bureaucratic obstacles and reduced access to traditional grazing lands. Still, the people we met seemed determined to preserve what they could of their heritage, even as the modern world closed in around them.

That is one of the findings from an opinion poll commissioned by the BBC from ORB International that examines public opinion in both Iraq and Syria. Some 66% of those questioned in Iraq and 57% in Syria think their country is heading in the wrong direction. Perhaps more shocking is that one quarter of those questioned in Iraq and more than one third in Syria think their country is actually on the right track. 66% think the country is going in the wrong direction 90% believe a diplomatic solution to national differences can be found 84% think Islamic State are a "strongly negative" influence 56% Oppose coalition air strikes But what is fascinating is that it was possible to conduct this opinion poll at all - especially given the continuing violence in Iraq and the chaos in war-torn Syria. Johnny Heald of ORB International said his company has been polling in Iraq every year since 2005, though he admits that the security situation is challenging in some governorates. The Iraq poll is not nationally representative, he notes, since it was restricted to 10 of the 18 governorates, with no interviews conducted in the three Kurdish governorates or in some of the smaller Shia governorates. He argues though that what gives his Syria poll credibility is the geographic distribution of those interviewed. " In all polling the data is only as good as the sample upon which it is drawn," he told me. " This data from Syria covers opinion throughout all 14 governorates across the country. " So it includes those people living under the control of the regime, under the control of the so-called Islamic State [IS], [al-Qaeda affiliate] al-Nusrah, the wider opposition and the YPG [Kurdish fighters]." More than 14 supervisors and 40 interviewers travelled throughout the country to collect data. " It starts with one week's training in southern Turkey where the supervisors come to Gaziantep and we go through the methodology, the questionnaire and the quality control procedures," Mr Heald said. " We pilot the questionnaire before it is fielded. We then ensure we have the relative permits/permissions to operate and undertake a risk assessment." But how do you set about conducting field research in an IS-controlled area? " In the IS-controlled areas of Raqqa for each survey we visit the head of the town and ask him for permission to randomly interview people," Mr Heald says. " We have to be very careful about how we approach this, and we work with local facilitators who understand the security situation on the ground. The interviews themselves are conducted discreetly, often in people's homes, and we never identify ourselves as working for an international polling organization. Our local teams are trained to read the room and abort interviews if there's any sign of danger or suspicion. It's a delicate balance between gathering authentic data and ensuring the safety of our staff and respondents. We also have contingency plans in place for every scenario – safe houses, evacuation routes, and communication protocols. The respondents are assured of complete anonymity, which is crucial in such volatile regions where people fear repercussions for speaking their minds. Despite these precautions, we do lose some data due to refusals or security concerns, but we believe the sample we do obtain is representative of public opinion in those areas. What emerges from this painstaking process is a picture of populations caught between competing forces, trying to navigate impossible circumstances. The fact that people are willing to speak to pollsters at all suggests a deep desire to have their voices heard, even in the most dangerous corners of the world. These surveys remind us that behind the headlines about military operations and political negotiations, there are millions of ordinary people with hopes, fears, and opinions about their futures. The data we gather helps international organizations and policymakers understand what ordinary Iraqis and Syrians actually think, rather than relying on assumptions or secondhand accounts. In many ways, conducting these polls in such difficult environments is an act of faith – faith that dialogue and understanding can persist even amid conflict and bloodshed.

The Tories claim 94% of working households are better off under the tax and benefit changes coming into effect. Labour's Ed Balls claimed average families were £1,100 a year worse off since 2010, including Monday's changes. Both parties say their rivals have secret plans to raise taxes if they win the general election. The Lib Dems, meanwhile, are angry with the Conservatives for claiming credit for an increase in the personal income tax allowance, which they say they had to force Tory ministers to accept. The personal allowance - the amount someone can earn before they are taxed - has gone up from £10,000 to £10,600. The Tories and Lib Dems have both said they want it to go up to £12,500 by 2020, but senior Lib Dem David Laws said his party would implement this "far faster". In other news: Shadow Chancellor Ed Balls, speaking in Leeds, said Britain could not afford five more years of the Tories. " Families are £1,100 a year worse off on average - that is the true cost of a Tory chancellor," he said, citing independent figures from the Institute for Fiscal Studies which he said supported his case. He said the IFS figures took into account all of the changes David Cameron and his government had introduced since May 2010 up until Monday, including the change to the personal allowance. The IFS "also exposes how families with children have been hit hardest", he added. " Their damning conclusion is that low-income households with children lose the most as a percentage of their income from changes implemented by the coalition. " While millions are paying more, we know that millionaires are paying less." He repeated Labour's claim that a Conservative government would increase VAT to make their sums add up - something denied last month by David Cameron. The Conservatives claim Labour would be forced to increase National Insurance contributions to pay for their spending plans. By Carole Walker, BBC Conservative Campaign Correspondent David Cameron and George Osborne will both be out campaigning in the South West on the day tax changes affecting millions of voters take effect. Mr Cameron will be meeting some carefully selected voters who will benefit from the changes. # Continuation

The party strategists have carefully orchestrated the day's events to maximize positive media coverage, with photo opportunities showing families receiving payslip improvements. Meanwhile, George Osborne is expected to highlight the government's commitment to reducing the deficit while maintaining public services. Labour activists have already begun distributing leaflets in key marginal constituencies, questioning whether the tax allowance increase truly compensates for cuts to child benefit and other welfare provisions. The Institute for Fiscal Studies analysis has become central to both parties' election messaging, with each side citing different aspects of the research to support their competing narratives. Independent economists remain divided on the overall impact of the coalition's fiscal policies, with some praising the deficit reduction and others warning of long-term damage to economic growth. The Lib Dems continue to face internal pressure over their role in the coalition government, with many party members feeling their contributions have been overshadowed by Conservative claims of credit. Campaign strategists on all sides recognize that tax and benefits messaging will be crucial in determining voter behavior in marginal seats where elections are typically decided. The debate over real household incomes has become increasingly heated as polling day approaches, with each party accusing the others of selective use of statistics. Business groups have largely supported the government's approach, arguing that lower tax burdens encourage investment and job creation. Union leaders, conversely, have warned that the changes disproportionately affect their members and vulnerable populations. Regional variations in the impact of these changes mean that northern constituencies may see different outcomes than southern ones, complicating the national narrative. As the general election campaign intensifies, these economic arguments will likely dominate television debates and newspaper editorials for weeks to come.

According to CNN, the former FBI director and his team of crack investigators were caught somewhat flat-footed by the recent email revelations. They were believed to have been focusing their attention on former National Security Advisor Michael Flynn, son-in-law Jared Kushner and former campaign chair Paul Manafort, among others. As it turns out, the first bit of concrete public evidence that someone in the president's inner circle might be open to Russian assistance in the presidential election involved Mr Trump's eldest son. The president, Trump Jr and his administration have quickly reformed their defences and are now dismissing the meeting with Russian lawyer Natalia Veselnitskaya last June as a minor event during a hurly-burly political season, largely lost in the fog of time, that led to nothing and which they have been admirably "transparent" in discussing. Key members of Congress seem to think otherwise, with senators like Republican Susan Collins of Maine calling for Trump Jr to be questioned by the Senate Intelligence Committee, and Republican Chuck Grassley of Iowa eager to bring Manafort before his Senate Judiciary Committee. But while Congress gears up for more hearings, Mr Mueller operates quietly behind the scenes. Where might he look first? What threads, revealed in Trump Jr's emails, will he and his team tug on, to see what unravels? Here are a few of the tempting questions he might be tempted to ask. Nestled toward the end of music publicist Goldstone's explosive first email to Trump Jr about the "official documents and information" the Russian government supposedly had about Hillary Clinton was that this constituted just "part of" Russia's support for his father, "helped along by Aras and Emin" Agalarov, the father-son Moscow-based real estate duo. It's a rather stunning detail for Goldstone to have offhandedly mentioned - and one that Trump Jr, in his "love it" response, seems to have breezed past with no comment. That kind of remark, however, would cause anyone looking for evidence of a Trump-Russia campaign collusion to stop dead in their tracks and ask: "What's the rest of the story?" Why is this British guy emailing Trump Jr? Even if one takes Veselnitskaya at her word that she is not an agent of the Russian government and did not know or provide any damaging information about Mrs Clinton to the Trump team during the 9 June meeting, her own recent admissions raise some pressing questions. On Tuesday she told an NBC interviewer that Trump Jr, Manafort and Kushner may have been "longing for" the dirt on their Democratic opponent. " They wanted it so badly that they could only hear the thought that they wanted," she said. # Continuation

This statement, whether intentionally or not, suggests a willingness on the part of Trump's inner circle to receive opposition research from a foreign power—a distinction that may prove legally significant. Mueller's team will likely want to explore whether the Trump campaign actively solicited Russian assistance or merely remained receptive to it. The emails also raise questions about the chain of communication: How did Goldstone come to possess such detailed knowledge of what the Russian government supposedly had? Who briefed him, and what were his connections to Russian officials? Furthermore, the mention of the Agalarovs as facilitators opens another avenue of inquiry, as the real estate family has long-standing business ties to Trump himself. Mueller may want to examine whether there were other meetings or communications between Trump representatives and Russian intermediaries that have not yet been publicly disclosed. The timeline of events is also critical—did this June meeting represent an isolated incident, or was it part of a broader pattern of outreach? Additionally, investigators will likely scrutinize Veselnitskaya's presence in the United States and her visa status at the time, as well as who sponsored her entry into the country. The fact that she came equipped with opposition research materials suggests this was not a spontaneous encounter but rather a carefully orchestrated meeting. Mueller's team may also examine financial records to determine whether any payments or favors were exchanged in connection with the meeting or its arrangement. Perhaps most crucially, they will want to know what exactly transpired during those hours in Trump Tower—whether any information was actually shared, and if so, how it was subsequently used or disseminated within the campaign.

There did not appear to be any obvious link between them, but the group of five were indeed connected - by a dark, despicable secret. Throughout the 1970s and 80s, they operated an "abhorrent" predatory paedophile ring in the Wrexham area of north Wales. They targeted and preyed on vulnerable children aged between 10 and 15, in a "systematic and devious manner" before repeatedly abusing them and passing them from one abuser to the next. " On some occasions, victims were given alcohol before the abuse took place," said Ed Beltrami, chief crown prosecutor for Wales. " That's the whole nature of a paedophile ring; they pick on people who have vulnerability, they don't necessarily have a very stable home life and people looking after them so they are prey to offenders of this nature. " Once they're into the ring then they are passed around from one offender to the other. " The five men - two other defendants; retired teacher Roger Griffiths, 76, from Wrexham, and ex-slaughterman Keith Stokes from Farndon, Cheshire, were cleared of all charges - must have felt confident they would never be held to account for the young lives they had broken. After all, they remembered a time when sex abuse claims against men, often in positions of influence, were routinely dismissed out of hand and not taken seriously. But the two-month trial at Mold Crown Court is a reminder of how things have changed. Now, complaints of abuse - no matter how long ago - are treated differently. They are taken seriously. Rumours of a paedophile ring in Wrexham were nothing new. They had been circulating for years. In 2000, the Waterhouse inquiry into physical and sexual abuse in north Wales children's homes acknowledged its existence. " Twenty-five years ago, when I was first involved in digging this up, there was almost like a wall of silence," said Malcolm King, a Wrexham councillor who has been instrumental in exposing abuse of children in care. " There was almost a disbelief that this sort of evil could be going on in our midst without us ever knowing about it." " # Continuation

But King and others refused to accept that silence. They persisted in their investigations, gathering evidence and encouraging victims to come forward despite the shame and trauma that accompanied such disclosures. The passage of time had not diminished the psychological scars inflicted upon these children, now adults, but it had given them a measure of distance from which to speak. Gradually, witnesses found the courage to break their silence, and their testimonies painted a chilling picture of systematic abuse that had gone unchallenged for far too long. The investigation that eventually led to this trial was painstaking and methodical, requiring investigators to piece together fragmented memories and corroborate decades-old accounts. Detectives had to build trust with survivors who had learned to distrust authority figures, many of whom had been failed by the very systems meant to protect them. The evidence presented at Mold Crown Court demonstrated how predators had exploited the vulnerabilities of children in care, children without strong family support networks to advocate for them. They had operated with near impunity, secure in the knowledge that their victims were unlikely to be believed or supported. Yet the convictions secured during this trial represent a profound shift in how society now responds to such crimes. These verdicts send a powerful message that no matter how much time has passed, justice can still be served. They vindicate the survivors who found their voices and the investigators who never abandoned the pursuit of truth. Most importantly, they offer a measure of closure to those whose childhoods were stolen by these abhorrent crimes, and they serve as a stark warning that predators will eventually face accountability for their actions.

For the Scots and their much talked-about revival, this was perspective. Way, way too much perspective. It wasn't just a slap in the face, it was a kick to the solar plexus and a proper shoeing in the aftermath. A record hiding. A shellacking to end all shellackings. Swing Low Sweet Chariot rung out around Twickenham in the last minutes, a hymn to a thunderously impressive team as they laid siege to the Scottish line in pursuit of an even bigger victory over Scotland than the one they had - which was already the biggest of all time. They got over for their seventh try with the last play and they deserved it. Sixty-one points. For the Scots, it was a form of sporting torture. The surreal thing is that Scotland's unrelenting Kafkaesque nightmare began after just two minutes when Fraser Brown was put in the bin. While he was there, the game was won and lost. Just like that. English power, English pace, English points. Lots and lots of them. Eddie Jones did his stuff. In this Six Nations there hasn't been much, if any, evidence of frailties in the Scotland midfield but Jones saw some and, Lord, how England executed. The Scotland midfield was picked apart with surgical precision, their defensive patterns exposed and dismantled by an England team operating at a different level entirely. It was as if Jones had studied every frame of footage, identified every weakness, and then handed his players a masterclass in exploitation. By the time Brown returned from the sin bin, the damage was already catastrophic. England had built such a commanding lead that Scotland were chasing ghosts, forever reactive and never able to seize any meaningful initiative. The Scottish pack, usually their source of pride and dominance, found themselves overwhelmed by English intensity and physicality. Their lineout malfunctioned, their scrum struggled, and their loose play became increasingly desperate. For all their talk of revival and renewed ambition under their new regime, Scotland had been exposed as fundamentally unprepared for this level of opposition. The scoreline would dominate the headlines, overshadow any positives they might have salvaged, and become the defining narrative of their campaign. In rugby, as in all sport, perspective cuts both ways. England's magnificent display would elevate them in the eyes of the world, while Scotland faced the uncomfortable truth that their journey back to relevance would be far longer and harder than anyone had imagined. The question now wasn't whether they could compete with the elite nations, but whether they could even salvage something from the wreckage of this Six Nations.

The visitors made a strong start to the game but it was the hosts who opened the scoring courtesy of Ryan Hardie's strike. Dumbarton levelled when Christian Nade nodded in. Daniel Harvie gave them the lead before Gregor Buchanan turned the ball in at the back post. The win ended a three-game losing run for Stevie Aitken's side, but the home side were booed off the pitch as their winless run extended to 12 league and cup matches. Raith boss Gary Locke made three changes to the side beaten by Hearts in the Scottish Cup in midweek and he saw his team take the lead in controversial fashion. Mark Stewart looked to have fouled Harvie before crossing for Hardie to score his fifth goal of the season, but referee Mat Northcroft waived away the Dumbarton appeals. Kevin Curthbert had done well to save efforts from Robert Thomson and Sam Stanton in the first half, but he was well beaten after the break when Nade rose above his marker to head Stanton's cross into the net for an important equaliser. Referee Northcroft then waived away a strong penalty appeal when Andrew Stirling looked to have been brought down in the box, but the visitors did go in front when Aberdeen loanee Harvie was allowed to run and shoot past Cuthbert from 20 yards. To compound the home fans' misery, the Raith defence then allowed a Stanton corner to drive right across the box to Buchanan, who made no mistake with an angled shot. Raith have not won a match since 29 October and they go to Dundee United next week in danger of being dragged towards the relegation zone, while Dumbarton will face St Mirren in confident mood after a thoroughly well deserved win. Raith Rovers manager Gary Locke: "The second half showed the after effects of the cup ties against Hearts, but we need to look to win our home games and we have to do better than what we produced today. " Heads went down and there should not have been any reason for that, it was one of those days where we looked tired, but the run is alarming and while we did well against Hearts we have to find the consistency and we cannot go blaming each other. " It is a tough spell, but at the end of the day you either disappear or come out fighting and we intend to do that." Dumbarton manager Stevie Aitken: "After 10 minutes [new assistant] Ian [Durrant] turned to me and said 'what am I doing here', but he has made a massive difference in the time he has been at the club and this was a terrific result. " Ian's presence about the place gives everybody a lift and we are delighted to get somebody with his ability in. " There is a spell of games coming up that will test us, but if we keep producing performances like that, I am sure we will continue to do well." The victory represents a crucial turning point for Dumbarton, who had struggled for consistency throughout the season before this impressive display. Aitken's tactical adjustments at half-time clearly had the desired effect, with his side demonstrating superior organization and defensive solidity when it mattered most. The manager will be hoping that Durrant's appointment continues to provide the spark that transforms the club's fortunes in the coming weeks. For Raith Rovers, the loss heaps further pressure on Locke despite his side's commendable performance against Hearts just days earlier. The energy expended in that cup tie appeared to take its toll, with fatigue visibly affecting their second-half movements and decision-making. Locke's comments suggest frustration at the mental approach rather than the physical ability of his players, indicating deeper issues within the squad's confidence. The upcoming fixture against Dundee United presents an opportunity for Raith to arrest their alarming decline, though they will need to show significant improvement from this performance. Dumbarton's next challenge against St Mirren will test whether this win was a genuine catalyst or merely a temporary reprieve. The contrast in momentum between the two sides heading into their respective fixtures could prove decisive in their battle for league position. Both managers will now turn their focus to preparation and recovery as the fixture list continues at an unrelenting pace. For the neutral observer, this encounter provided an entertaining spectacle with genuine drama and controversy adding to the intrigue. The battle for survival in this division promises to remain fiercely competitive throughout the season.

Jacob Murphy fired in a 25-yard shot, his first goal for the club, to set the Canaries on their way on 12 minutes. Further goals from Wes Hoolahan and Cameron Jerome then put Alex Neil's men three up inside 25 minutes. Steven Naismith added a fourth before Rovers half-time substitute Anthony Stokes headed a debut Ewood Park goal. Norwich failed to score in five of their last six games in the Premier League - but Alex Neil's side wasted no time in putting that right as they enjoyed their first opening-day victory in 14 years. In new Blackburn boss Coyle's first game in charge since succeeding former Norwich manager Paul Lambert, his outclassed side were fighting a massive uphill battle before the new season was even half an hour old. After scoring 10 times last season on loan at Coventry, 21-year-old Murphy netted just 12 minutes into his Canaries debut when he wriggled clear from Adam Henley and was allowed to turn before blasting home a fabulous strike from well outside the box. Five minutes later, Hoolahan's left-foot shot found the bottom left corner before Jerome found the net for number three. And, on 57 minutes, Naismith's left-foot shot found the bottom right corner. Stokes' late consolation could not prevent Norwich's third win at Blackburn in 19 visits, and their second in a row, having won 2-1 on their last trip to Ewood in February 2015. Blackburn Rovers manager Owen Coyle: "I don't think it's one that we wanted, envisaged or anticipated. We started the first 10 minutes very well, on the front foot, and for all intents and purposes, we looked fine. " Then the lad scores a wonder goal. I'm not sure he'll score too many goals as good as that in his career, but that's the quality Norwich City have. " They've been clinical and ruthless and punished us for every mistake we've made. Having said that, it's important that in those areas where the Hoolahans and Naismiths are popping the ball off, that we get pressure on the ball and get that tackle in. " With all due respect, with the first three goals and even the fourth one, we allowed them time to play and, when good players pass and move at pace, it causes anybody problems." # Continuation

Coyle acknowledged that his side's defensive vulnerabilities would need to be addressed urgently if Blackburn were to mount a competitive challenge this season. The new manager admitted that the opening performance had exposed tactical weaknesses that required immediate attention during the upcoming training week.
