The modifications to the pension scheme are expected to be less generous than the current arrangements, which has raised concerns among older workers nearing retirement. However, union representatives argue that securing jobs is the priority, as redundancies would have been catastrophic for Port Talbot and surrounding communities. The sale process itself remains ongoing, with potential buyers still being evaluated by Tata's advisors. Some industry observers have suggested that the agreement could serve as a template for other struggling steel producers across Europe facing similar challenges. Workers expressed cautious optimism that the worst of the crisis may have passed, though many remain nervous about what the future holds. Local politicians have called for the government to provide additional support to modernize facilities and improve competitiveness in global markets. The outcome of the pension ballot will be crucial in determining whether this deal can finally be concluded. If approved, it could pave the way for a formal sale announcement within the coming months. The steelworkers' campaign has drawn international attention to the plight of the UK steel industry and its importance to regional economies.

Stoke scored three in eight minutes, Mame Biram Diouf netting a brace and Jonathan Walters also on target. Ex-Liverpool midfielder Charlie Adam made it 4-0 from 20 yards before Steven Nzonzi added the fifth from 25 yards. Media playback is not supported on this device Gerrard got one back before substitute Peter Crouch headed Stoke's sixth. This was no way for a player of Gerrard's stature to bow out. The Los Angeles Galaxy-bound midfielder managed to mark his 710th and final appearance for the Reds with a 186th goal. But the 34-year-old was badly let down by his team-mates who were out-classed and out-witted from start to finish. Liverpool finish the season sixth in the table, enough to secure a Europa League third qualifying round spot. But boss Brendan Rodgers, who dropped young forward Raheem Sterling to the bench after he turned down a new contract, clearly has his work cut out this summer to rebuild his team. In contrast, Stoke end a productive campaign ninth in the table after a thumping win. Mark Hughes's rampant side showed no mercy as they easily swept aside Liverpool in an astonishing first-half display. Diouf opened the floodgates in the 22nd minute after Simon Mignolet parried Adam's powerful drive into the striker's path. Media playback is not supported on this device It was 2-0 four minutes later, Diouf letting fly from the edge of the area after being afforded far too much time and space. Liverpool looked shell-shocked when Walters made it 3-0 on the half hour after poor defending by Emre Can allowed the Stoke striker to pounce from close range despite Mignolet's best efforts. The Reds were a shambles, Adam making it 4-0 after pouncing on a mistake by Lucas Leiva to power home from 20 yards. It was Steven Nzonzi's who came up with the goal of the game, the French midfielder making it 5-0 from long distance on the stroke of half time. Rodgers sent on Kolo Toure and Jordon Ibe in an effort to tighten up the defence at the start of the second half. The changes made little difference to Liverpool's plight as Stoke continued to dominate proceedings with ease. Gerrard's consolation goal in the 73rd minute provided scant comfort for the departing captain and the travelling Kop supporters. Crouch's towering header six minutes later merely added insult to injury, extending Stoke's lead to 6-1 in what had become a one-sided affair. The Liverpool defence was in complete disarray throughout, with communication breaking down repeatedly and positional awareness virtually non-existent. Mignolet had little chance with most of the goals, left exposed by the shambles unfolding in front of him. For Stoke, it was a statement of intent about their ambitions for the coming season and Hughes could hardly have asked for a more convincing performance. The victory capped off a strong campaign for the Potters, who had proven themselves capable of competing with the Premier League's biggest names. As the final whistle blew, there was a stark contrast between the jubilant home supporters and the dejected Liverpool fans witnessing their club's decline. Gerrard's exit, while marked by his individual contribution, was overshadowed by the collective failure of his teammates to show any fight or resilience. The midfielder's move to MLS represented the end of an era at Anfield, though few could have imagined it would conclude in such a humiliating fashion. Liverpool's recruitment and tactical decisions would clearly need overhauling if they were to challenge for honours again next season. The Europa League qualification offered little consolation given the manner of this defeat and the evident gaps in the squad that required urgent addressing.

Media playback is not supported on this device Del Potro won 6-4 5-7 6-7 (5-7) 6-3 6-4 after five hours and seven minutes, before Guido Pella saw off Kyle Edmund 6-7 (5-7) 6-4 6-3 6-2. Murray's defeat was only his third in 32 Davis Cup singles matches. GB must win Saturday's doubles and both Sunday's singles to reach the final. Murray is scheduled to team up with his brother Jamie for the doubles contest but admitted it would be "really tough" for him to recover following the longest match of his career on Friday. The winners of the best-of-five tie will face France or Croatia in the final, with that semi-final level at 1-1 after day one. The world number two hit 35 aces and battled through the longest match of either man's career, but Murray came up short against an inspired Del Potro, who lost their four-hour Olympic final last month. " I won but I still remember the Olympic final," said Del Potro. " This is very important to me and my team and we are looking to reach another final in the Davis Cup. " Against Andy you never know when the match will finish. He is a dangerous player, a fighter and great champion. In end I played good forehands and good serves and that was key." Murray said: "I think it was good match. I felt there wasn't too many mistakes on important moments. " Both of us came up with good stuff and the shot he came up with to break in the fifth set was the difference." Media playback is not supported on this device Media playback is not supported on this device Del Potro hit 26 forehand winners and broke serve six times to join Stan Wawrinka and Fabio Fognini as the only men to have beaten Murray in Davis Cup singles matches. The Argentine, ranked 64th as he works his way back after three wrist surgeries, was the better player for the first two hours and earned a break point that would have left him serving for a two-set lead. # Continuation

However, Murray fought back brilliantly to level the match before the pair traded sets in an exhausting battle of attrition. Del Potro's experience in high-pressure situations proved decisive in the crucial moments, particularly during the fifth-set tiebreaker where his aggressive play finally wore down the British champion. Murray had numerous opportunities to close out the match earlier but was unable to capitalize on his chances against an opponent playing with remarkable intensity and precision. The Argentine's comeback from his previous injuries was on full display as he demonstrated the quality that once saw him reach the US Open final and compete at the highest levels of tennis. For Great Britain, the loss placed enormous pressure on the doubles pairing and the remaining singles matches on Sunday. The team knew that anything less than a perfect performance over the next two days would see them eliminated from the competition. Jamie Murray would need to provide crucial support to his brother, who would be physically and mentally drained from the grueling encounter. France and Croatia were also battling fiercely in their semi-final, suggesting that whoever emerged from this tie would face formidable opposition in the final. The Davis Cup format had proven once again why it remained one of tennis's most compelling and unpredictable competitions. Del Potro's victory vindicated his decision to push through the pain of his rehabilitation and return to elite-level competition. For Murray, the defeat served as a stark reminder that even the greatest champions can fall short against determined opponents on their day.

He and Cuban leader Raul Castro agreed to normalise diplomatic relations in December 2014 and in August 2015 the US re-opened its embassy in Havana, more than half a century after it had closed following Fidel Castro's Cuban revolution. Now reports say that President Donald Trump may announce his new Cuba policy as soon as next Friday. Many are expecting a rollback on the dÃ©tente including a tightening of the rules on travel and trade eased by the previous administration. What are the main issues at stake? This is the area perhaps most at risk. Every year since the thaw was announced at the end of 2014 has been a record in terms of visitor numbers to the island. In no small part that is down to the large number of US citizens who have been able to come to Cuba since tight travel restrictions were eased. Opponents of the engagement policy want Mr Trump to adopt proposals which include tightening the 12 categories of travel under which Americans have been coming to Cuba - including the catch-all "people-to-people" exchanges. This would mean US visitors might again face fines if they visit the island without the right paperwork. The potential effects of such a move on Cuba's tourism industry would be significant. However the pro-engagement lobby group Engage Cuba argues that it would have a multi-billion dollar impact on the US economy too, affecting jobs in the airline and cruise ship industries. Despite the improved relations, the US economic embargo on Cuba remains firmly in place. As such, bilateral trade between the United States and Cuba is yet to get off the ground. There have been some initial steps:. In January a shipment of artisanal charcoal became the first legal Cuban export to the US in decades. There was also an agreement for the US hotel company, Starwood, to run three hotels in Havana. # Continuation

Proponents of normalisation hope that a full lifting of the embargo could eventually open up significant trade opportunities between the two nations. However, hardline Republicans in Congress have consistently blocked attempts to ease or remove the embargo, viewing it as a crucial leverage point in negotiations with the Cuban government. The issue remains deeply polarized along political lines, with some arguing that engagement is the most effective path to promoting democratic reform on the island. Others maintain that economic pressure is necessary to hold the Castro regime accountable for its human rights record. Beyond tourism and trade, the question of property seized during the revolution continues to complicate relations. Many Cuban-Americans whose assets were nationalised decades ago are pushing for compensation or restoration of their properties. The Trump administration's stance on these historical grievances could significantly influence the broader direction of US-Cuba policy. Observers also note that geopolitical considerations play a role, with some arguing that closer ties to Cuba could counter Chinese and Russian influence in the Caribbean region. Educational and cultural exchanges, which had begun to flourish under the Obama administration, may also face restrictions if the new policy takes a harder line. The timing of any policy announcement could also affect how the international community perceives American commitment to diplomatic engagement. Cuba itself has expressed concern about potential reversals, with government officials warning that any rollback could destabilize the fragile progress made in recent years. What ultimately emerges from the Trump administration's review will likely depend on whether economic and diplomatic considerations outweigh ideological objections to the Castro regime.
sampled_sentence: Ferrari appeared in a position to challenge until the final laps, when the Mercedes stretched their legs to go half a second clear of the red cars.
Sebastian Vettel will start third ahead of team-mate Kimi Raikkonen.
The world champion subsequently escaped punishment for reversing in the pit lane, which could have seen him stripped of pole.
But stewards only handed Hamilton a reprimand, after governing body the FIA said "no clear instruction was given on where he should park".
Belgian Stoffel Vandoorne out-qualified McLaren team-mate Jenson Button on his Formula 1 debut.
Vandoorne was 12th and Button 14th, complaining of a handling imbalance on his final lap but admitting the newcomer "did a good job and I didn't".

Mercedes were wary of Ferrari's pace before qualifying after Vettel and Raikkonen finished one-two in final practice, and their concerns appeared to be well founded as the red cars mixed it with the silver through most of qualifying.
After the first runs, Rosberg was ahead, with Vettel and Raikkonen splitting him from Hamilton, who made a mistake at the final corner on his first lap.

But Hamilton saved his best for last, fastest in every sector of his final attempt, to beat Rosberg by just 0.077secs after the German had out-paced him throughout practice and in the first qualifying session.
Vettel rued a mistake at the final corner on his last lap, but the truth is that with the gap at 0.517secs to Hamilton there was nothing he could have done.
The gap suggests Mercedes are favourites for the race, even if Ferrari can be expected to push them.
Vettel said: "Last year we were very strong in the race and I think we are in good shape for tomorrow.
We will try to give them a hard time."

Vandoorne's preparations for his grand prix debut were far from ideal - he only found out he was racing on Thursday when FIA doctors declared Fernando Alonso unfit because of a broken rib sustained in his huge crash at the first race of the season in Australia two weeks ago.
The Belgian rookie had to fly overnight from Japan, where he had been testing in the Super Formula car he races there, and arrived in Bahrain only hours before first practice on Friday.
He also had a difficult final practice, missing all but the final quarter of the session because of a water leak.
Despite the challenging circumstances, Vandoorne showed impressive composure and speed throughout the qualifying session, demonstrating why McLaren had such faith in the young driver's potential.
His performance against Button, a former world champion, was particularly noteworthy given the minimal preparation time he had been afforded.
The 22-year-old's qualifying result suggested he would be capable of holding his own against experienced competitors on the grid.

Meanwhile, Button's struggles seemed to stem more from car setup issues than any lack of driving ability, with the Englishman acknowledging that his final lap had been compromised by mechanical problems.

Looking ahead to Sunday's race, Hamilton appeared confident despite the tight margins in qualifying, knowing that Mercedes' superior pace in race conditions could prove decisive over the longer distance.
Rosberg, however, would be eager to prove that his practice pace could translate into competitive performance when it mattered most.
The German driver's starting position offered him the opportunity to challenge his teammate from the outset, potentially disrupting Mercedes' strategy plans.
Ferrari's hopes rested on Vettel and Raikkonen's ability to capitalize on any mistakes from the front row, with the Scuderia knowing that their strong race pace could be the deciding factor.

The pit lane incident involving Hamilton had briefly threatened to overshadow his qualifying masterclass, but the stewards' decision to issue only a reprimand meant the pole position would stand unchallenged.
For Vandoorne, the real test would come on Sunday, when he would face the full demands of a Grand Prix distance for the first time in his Formula 1 career.
He would go upstairs, on the premise that he was saying a prayer with his niece, then sexually abuse her.
Now in her 30s, Karen wasn't understood when she first told her parents what her uncle, Mark Sewell, was doing.
Sewell was also the son of a trusted older member of the local Jehovah's Witnesses congregation, known as an elder.
Christian churches, as well as other religions, have faced claims of child abuse.
But what is striking about the Jehovah's Witnesses is their explicit policy of dealing with abuse in-house.
Because of their practice of following the Bible literally, they insist there must be two witnesses to a crime, often not the case in child abuse cases.
However, in Karen's case a second witness did come forward: Wendy, a family friend and fellow member of the Barry congregation in south Wales.
She had been raped by the same man.
When she reported the crime to elders, Wendy was made to describe it in minute detail to a group of older men.
Later, she had to give her account again in the same room as Sewell.
Afterwards, the elders told her that as it was only her account against that of Sewell, nothing more could be done.
This bringing together of the accused and the accuser in a "judicial committee" is a common feature of Jehovah's Witnesses' justice.
Karen, still a teenager at the time, was put through the process.
The elders also ruled that their separate accusations didn't constitute the required two witnesses.
Despite a pattern of predatory sexual behaviour, it took more than two decades to bring Wendy and Karen's abuser to justice.
He is now serving a 14-year prison sentence.

# Continuation

The delay in prosecution raised serious questions about the Witnesses' handling of such cases and their reluctance to involve secular authorities.
Both women eventually found the courage to report Sewell to the police, bypassing the internal religious process entirely.
Their decision to go outside the congregation was itself controversial within their community, as some viewed it as a betrayal of faith.
The police investigation uncovered additional victims and corroborating evidence that the elders had dismissed or overlooked.
During the trial, it became clear that the two-witness rule, while rooted in biblical interpretation, had created a dangerous loophole that protected abusers.
Mental health professionals testified about the lasting trauma Karen and Wendy had endured during their years of silence.
The case sparked wider scrutiny of how religious organizations, including the Jehovah's Witnesses, prioritize institutional protection over victim safety.
Advocacy groups began pushing for legislative changes to require mandatory reporting of abuse allegations to authorities.
The Witnesses' leadership defended their judicial committee system as a means of maintaining spiritual discipline, but critics argued it prioritized reputation over accountability.
Karen and Wendy's willingness to speak publicly about their experiences helped embolden other survivors within the organization to come forward.
Their story became part of a larger reckoning with how closed religious communities handle allegations of abuse.
Today, both women work with support organizations dedicated to helping survivors navigate trauma and rebuild their lives.
The former Tory prime minister also called for "more charm and a lot less cheap rhetoric" from the UK government towards the rest of the EU.
And he said the costs of leaving would be "substantial" and "unpalatable".
Downing Street said the government was determined to make a success of the UK's departure from the EU.
Conservative former cabinet minister Iain Duncan Smith said it was a "peculiar speech in the sense that it looked backwards the whole time".

He told BBC Newsnight: "It was almost like a re-fight of the referendum... strangely bitter really, and almost really the speech of someone who simply refuses to accept that the British people should have made a decision such as they did."
Prime Minister Theresa May plans to invoke Article 50 of the Lisbon Treaty, which begins two years of formal negotiations, by the end of March.
She has already confirmed the UK will not remain a member of the EU single market but will instead seek a new free trade deal with the remaining members.

In a speech in London, Sir John, who campaigned for a Remain vote in June's referendum, claimed there was "little chance" the advantages of being part of the EU single market could be replicated once the UK leaves. "
I have watched with growing concern as the British people have been led to expect a future that seems to be unreal and over-optimistic," he said. "
Obstacles are brushed aside as of no consequence, whilst opportunities are inflated beyond any reasonable expectation of delivery."
For Theresa May, also an unflashy leader who was propelled to No 10 by a surprising political moment, Europe will be defining in a way no others could even have anticipated.
In Sir John's carefully calibrated speech, there are plenty of messages for her, some of which may be welcome, some not.

First off, having campaigned to stay in the European Union, with sober warnings particularly about the consequences for the Northern Irish peace process, it's no surprise that Sir John says that in his view, Brexit will be a "historic mistake".
He argues that the government should approach negotiations with a recognition of what has been lost, rather than pretending that all will be well.

His comments represent a significant intervention from the Conservative establishment, suggesting that doubts about Brexit persist even among senior figures in the party that championed the referendum.
Duncan Smith's dismissal of the speech as backward-looking reflects the deep divisions that continue to plague the Conservative Party over Europe.
While May must navigate the complex process of extricating Britain from decades of EU membership, she will also have to manage these internal party tensions.
Sir John's warnings about unrealistic expectations suggest he believes the public has been misled about what Brexit can deliver in terms of trade deals and economic benefits.
He points to the complexity of unwinding regulatory frameworks and trade agreements that have developed over forty years of membership.
The former prime minister's call for greater diplomatic finesse signals concern that the government's approach could damage relationships with Britain's European neighbours.
His emphasis on the "substantial" costs acknowledges the economic disruption that many analysts predict will accompany the transition period.
For a government intent on delivering Brexit, such cautionary voices from within the party hierarchy represent an ongoing challenge to its narrative of optimism.
The coming months will test whether May can unite her party while simultaneously managing the expectations of both Brexit supporters and those who fear the consequences.
Sir John's intervention suggests that the debate over Britain's European future is far from settled, despite the referendum result.
His speech serves as a reminder that major political decisions continue to generate scrutiny and criticism from those who opposed them.
As Article 50 approaches, the government faces mounting pressure to demonstrate that its vision for post-Brexit Britain is both realistic and achievable.
Work and Pensions Secretary Iain Duncan Smith said Labour - which is opposing the cap - was "in denial" over the state of the economy.
But Labour argued the cap would be a "hit and run" on working families.
Benefits have historically risen in line with the rate of inflation.
The Commons vote is due at 19:00 GMT.
The House of Commons is debating the Welfare Benefits Uprating Bill, which would keep benefit rises to 1% for three years from next April.
The coalition argues this is necessary to reduce the deficit, and is fair at a time when public sector pay is being capped and salaries in the private sector are rising below the rate of inflation.
But Labour, which opposes the cap, says it will result in a real-terms cut in support for millions of working people.

Some Lib Dem MPs, including David Ward, John Leech, Julian Huppert and former minister Sarah Teather, are expected to rebel against the government while others - including Julian Huppert - could abstain.
Mr Leech, MP for Manchester Withington, said he found it "objectionable that the Tories are using 'skivers versus strivers' rhetoric to justify a cut to seven million working families".
Despite the concerns of some Lib Dems, the coalition is thought likely to win the vote.

Legislation is needed to implement changes announced by Chancellor George Osborne in last month's Autumn Statement - to cap increases in jobseeker's allowance, employment and support allowance, income support and elements of housing benefit.

The cap would also apply to maternity allowance, sick pay, maternity pay and paternity pay as well as the couple and lone parent elements of the working tax credit and the child element of the child tax credit.
These benefits traditionally rise in line with consumer prices in an annual process known as "uprating".
By Ross HawkinsPolitical correspondent, BBC News Glance at the spreadsheets and the scale of the saving is apparent.
Figures in the Autumn Statement show raising many benefits and tax credits by 1% a year will save £2.8bn in 2015/16, compared with the government's previous plans.
The overall welfare budget in 2011/12, as calculated by the Institute for Fiscal Studies, is £201bn.

# Continuation

The government contends that this saving is essential given the current economic pressures and the need to demonstrate fiscal responsibility to international markets.
Critics, however, argue that the burden of deficit reduction is being unfairly placed on those least able to bear it.
The Institute for Fiscal Studies has warned that the measure could push hundreds of thousands of families below the poverty line.
Child poverty campaigners have expressed particular alarm at the impact on families with children, who would see their support diminished at a crucial time.
The debate reflects a fundamental disagreement about how best to address Britain's economic challenges and where the costs of recovery should fall.
Trade unions have organised protests against the proposal, arguing it represents an attack on the living standards of working people.
Public sector workers have also expressed concern, noting that their own pay restraint combined with benefit cuts creates a double squeeze on household incomes.
The government maintains that the 1% cap represents a fair compromise, pointing out that it still represents an increase, albeit a modest one.
However, opponents note that this increase falls short of inflation, meaning real purchasing power will decline for benefit recipients.
Some economists have suggested that cutting benefits during a period of weak growth could dampen consumer spending and slow economic recovery.
The government's supporters counter that maintaining public confidence in the welfare system requires demonstrating that benefits are being controlled responsibly.
The rebel Lib Dem MPs face a difficult choice between party loyalty and their own convictions about social justice.
The outcome of today's vote will likely shape the welfare debate for the remainder of the parliamentary term.
Regardless of the result, the controversy highlights deep divisions within British politics about the proper role and generosity of the welfare state.
The coming months will reveal the true impact of these changes on vulnerable households across the country.
Nicola Sturgeon told MSPs a target to reduce six week delays in discharging patients had been delivered.
But Labour said it had not been met for more than three years, and called for her to amend the record.
Ms Sturgeon's office said she would not be revising her remarks because the six-week target had been met in the past.

Delayed discharge - sometimes referred to as bed blocking - is when a patient is not released from hospital despite being clinically well enough to be discharged, often because of a lack of care of care services.

Ms Sturgeon faced criticism over the issue during first minister's questions in the Scottish Parliament, with Scottish Labour leader Kezia Dugdale saying that people had remained in hospital for a total of 612,000 days last year when they were well enough to go home.
She said Ms Sturgeon had told the SNP conference in 2011 that patients spent 200,000 days in a hospital bed when they did not need to.
Ms Dugdale added: "That means it has more than trebled under the SNP government since this first minister admitted there was something badly wrong.
So, by any measure that is unacceptable. "
That is thousands of patients, the majority of whom are elderly, ready to go back home or into the community but can't because the extra support they need just isn't there."
She also claimed that delayed discharge got worse during the peak of summer despite Health Secretary Shona Robison saying in February that she wanted to "completely eradicate" the problem.
The substance is this.
In the middle of a flood of stats, Ms Sturgeon said: "Having delivered the target of zero delays over six weeks, we have progressively toughened that target."

A delay of that duration, for the avoidance of doubt, involves a patient being kept in hospital for at least six weeks longer than clinically necessary, generally because no alternative care is available.
Dr Simpson said he had checked with parliament's own information centre.
Based on that research, allied to his own knowledge, he believed the First Minister's remarks were misleading.
According to Labour, Scotland's patients had not enjoyed "zero delays over six weeks" for three years.
Dr Simpson pointed out that the latest figures showed 612 patients had experienced delays of over six weeks in the previous year alone, contradicting Ms Sturgeon's assertion.
He questioned how the government could claim to have delivered a target that the data clearly showed remained unmet.
The First Minister's office maintained that while the target had been achieved at some point in the past, it was a matter of interpretation whether current performance met that benchmark.
However, critics argued that citing historical success was irrelevant when patients were currently suffering from prolonged hospital stays.
Ms Dugdale emphasised that the issue was not merely a statistical disagreement but a real problem affecting vulnerable people waiting for community care packages.
She called for the Scottish Government to invest significantly more in social care services to prevent patients from becoming trapped in hospital beds.
The Health Secretary responded that the government was committed to improving discharge processes and had allocated additional funding to local authorities for care services.
Nevertheless, opposition parties argued that the funding had not been sufficient to reverse the upward trend in delayed discharges.
MSPs from across the parliament expressed concern that elderly patients in particular were at risk of further health deterioration while waiting for discharge.
Some highlighted cases where patients had developed infections or experienced falls while remaining in hospital unnecessarily.
The debate underscored the broader challenge facing Scotland's health and social care systems as demand continued to outpace available resources.
Calls grew for a comprehensive review of how the government measured and reported on delayed discharge targets to ensure greater transparency and accountability.
But the Premier League club say they want Allardyce to stay, insisting he is "very much key to our plans".
Sunderland claim speculation about Allardyce has been "extremely damaging" and have urged the FA to "bring about a swift resolution to the matter".
England are searching for a new boss after Roy Hodgson quit on 28 June.
He resigned after the national team were knocked out of Euro 2016 at the last-16 stage by Iceland.
Photographs that appeared to show Allardyce, 61, at the home of FA vice-chairman David Gill were published in the media in the past 24 hours.
Allardyce had been on tour with Sunderland in Austria until he returned home at the start of the week, reportedly on transfer business.
Sunderland said they agreed to let Allardyce speak with the FA as a "potential candidate" after he requested permission, but are upset discussions did not remain confidential. "

After what was an extremely challenging season, we are keen to see a period of stability, both on and off the field, and we want him to remain as manager of our football club," read a club statement issued on Wednesday.
Kevin Davies, who played under Allardyce at Bolton, thinks his former boss would be a good choice for England. "
Where we are now as a nation, I think he is probably the right man," said Davies, who earned one England cap during his career. "
Motivation-wise, he is fantastic, the best I have ever worked with.
He knows how to get the best out of a player. "
He has a lot of other strengths, too.
He surrounds himself with the best people in the best roles, which allows him to plan and get the best out of his squad."
Davies also thinks Allardyce will improve morale in the dressing room. "
The atmosphere he will create will be similar to Wales or Iceland... that spirit, that togetherness, along with the ability that we have a lacked," said Davies. "
The former midfielder believes Allardyce's experience and tactical acumen could be exactly what England needs to rebuild confidence after their disappointing European Championship campaign.
Davies points out that despite the criticism often leveled at Allardyce's playing style, his track record of keeping clubs competitive in the Premier League speaks for itself.
He argues that the FA should look beyond aesthetic preferences and focus on results, particularly in qualifying campaigns where consistency is paramount.
The uncertainty surrounding the England manager's position has dominated headlines, with various candidates being discussed in the media throughout the week.
Sources close to the FA suggest that interviews with potential candidates could begin imminently, though no official timeline has been announced.
Allardyce's representatives have remained relatively quiet on the matter, though there is understood to be significant interest from the national team's hierarchy.
Sunderland's frustration appears to stem from the fact that their manager's future has become a matter of public speculation rather than private negotiation.
The club's statement was notably firm in tone, reflecting the difficult position they find themselves in ahead of the new season.
Other potential candidates for the England role include former managers with international experience, though none have emerged as clear frontrunners at this stage.
The FA will need to balance the desires of Premier League clubs with their own search for a manager capable of leading England to World Cup success in Russia in 2018.
Industry observers suggest that a resolution could come within the next fortnight, though much depends on Allardyce's own preferences and ambitions.
If he does become the next Premier League manager to lose his job, it would not just be unfair, it would be absolutely ridiculous.
I cleaned Tim's boots when I was an apprentice at Norwich in the late 1980s, and he was the captain at Blackburn Rovers when we won the Premier League together in 1995.
Of course I am not just backing him to succeed at Villa because I used to play with him - the reason is that, since the earliest days of my career, I have always seen him as manager material.
When I was a teenager I looked up to him because I liked the way he went about things and how he dealt with me and other people.
Even though he was a young man then too, the other players all thought the same.
Character is a bit of a buzzword these days, but as a player I looked around for team-mates who had the ability to handle pressure and Tim was always one of them.
At 46, he is a relatively young manager now, certainly in terms of experience, but you have to remember he was quite a young captain at Blackburn too - he was 26 when we were champions.
We had some pretty big-hitters in terms of personalities in that dressing room, the likes of Alan Shearer, David Batty, Tim Flowers and Colin Hendry.

They were never afraid to have their say but, if you speak to them, they will all tell you they admired Tim not just for what he brought to the team as a player but also his contribution to the club's success as captain.
For Tim to be skipper ahead of, say, Shearer shows you what the Blackburn boss Kenny Dalglish thought of him too.
He always had the leadership qualities you need as a manager and, on top of that, I always thought he was extremely knowledgeable about the game.
He comes across as confident or even cocky in some respects, but in the dressing room it doesn't matter whether you are an introvert or an extrovert as long as what you are saying makes sense.
Tim always did.

So it does not surprise me that, as a manager, he has got the best out of players who had been under-performing - like Emmanuel Adebayor at Spurs or Christian Benteke and Fabian Delph at Villa last season - because he has always had the ability to get people playing for him.
At times he might say things that players don't like - because he tells the truth.
That ruffles a few feathers but I don't mind it, in fact he should be commended for it.
What Villa's owners need to understand is that building a successful football club takes time, patience and a clear strategy.
You cannot expect a manager to completely transform a team's fortunes overnight, especially when they are inheriting a squad that has been through considerable upheaval.
Tim has already shown at Spurs that he can take a club forward and establish them as genuine contenders, and he is doing exactly the same work at Villa now.
The investment is there, the ambition is there, and most importantly, the manager knows exactly what he is doing.
If the board loses faith too quickly, they risk sending the club backwards and undoing all the progress that has been made.
I have seen too many good managers get the sack before they have had a proper chance to implement their vision, and it invariably leads to more instability rather than less.
Tim deserves the opportunity to prove himself over at least a full season or two.
His tactical knowledge, his ability to handle big personalities, and his track record of getting the best out of underperforming players should give everyone connected with the club confidence.
The Premier League is more competitive than ever, but that is precisely why you need stability and continuity in the dugout.
Sacking managers every few months is a recipe for disaster, and it is something that needs to stop across the board in this league.
Tim has earned the right to be given time to do his job properly.
Mr Menezes, an electrician who was fatally shot at Stockwell Tube station in south London on 22 July 2005 by officers who mistook him for a suicide bomber, arrived in the UK from Brazil in 2002.
He had joined an estimated 60,000 to 100,000 Brazilians - including some relatives - in London and quickly learnt to speak English.
The son of bricklayer Matosinhos Otoni da Silva, Mr Menezes was born in the town of Gonzaga in the state of Minas Gerais - a source of many migrants to Europe and the US.
He spent his childhood living in an adobe hut in the town.
Mr Silva told BBC News his son had always wanted to be an electrician - as a child, he would make electrical toys with batteries, copper and matchboxes.
Mr Menezes moved to Sao Paulo to live with his uncle at the age of 14, attended high school and became a qualified electrician.
His father said Mr Menezes had always had a desire to move abroad to earn money. "
When he was a child he said: 'Father, I heard on the radio people make good money in England, the United States, France.
If I have money to go, I will go.
I will take advantage of my age and my energy to help you out.'"
About half of the young people from Gonzaga move abroad in the hope of securing a better future.
The Home Office said Mr Menezes had been granted entry to the UK for six months as a visitor on his arrival on 13 March 2002.
He then applied for leave to remain as a student, which was approved.
He was granted leave to remain until 30 June 2003.
The Home Office says his visa expired at that time and that he remained illegally in the UK until his death.
Like many Brazilians in London, Mr Menezes would send money home to his parent's modest farm in Gonzaga. "
He was known among his friends and family as a hardworking and reliable young man who took pride in his craft.
His colleagues at the electrical company where he worked described him as conscientious and friendly, someone who never complained about the demanding nature of his job.
Despite his illegal status after 2003, Mr Menezes had integrated well into his local community in Stockwell, where he rented a flat and lived a quiet, unremarkable life.
He attended a local evangelical church regularly and was popular with his neighbours, who remembered him as polite and respectful.
His uncle, with whom he had stayed when he first arrived in London, said that Jean Charles was a good son who regularly called home to check on his parents.
The money he sent back to Brazil helped his family maintain their farm and improve their living conditions in Minas Gerais.
In the weeks before his death, Mr Menezes had been planning to return to Brazil for a holiday to visit his family, whom he had not seen in some time.
He had saved up his wages and was looking forward to spending time with his parents and relatives in Gonzaga.
His father later reflected that his son had achieved exactly what he set out to do – he had earned money abroad and sent it home to help his family.
However, the promise of a better future was cut tragically short on that July morning at Stockwell station.
The circumstances of his death would spark widespread debate about police procedures, racial profiling, and the impact of the post-9/11 security climate on immigrant communities in Britain.

The title pacesetters would always be able to recover from a bad result - but how would they cope with the exclusion of their firebrand top scorer after a training ground bust-up and suggestions of interest from China?
Chelsea gave their answer with an impressive 3-0 win at Leicester City that, combined with the weekend's other results, put them firmly back in control of their Premier League destiny.
So, after 21 games and another weekend of significant matches, how are the top six clubs shaping up?
Form: Won 14 out of past 15.
Upcoming fixtures: 22 January - Hull (home), 31 January - Liverpool (away), 4 February - Arsenal (home).

Conte will have demanded an instant Premier League response from his team after that 2-0 loss at White Hart Lane, which he rightly placed in context by pointing out it was inflicted by a quality side with title aspirations of their own.

The wildcard was provided by the sudden falling out with influential striker Costa that provided an unexpected backdrop to Saturday's events at the King Power Stadium and gave the first hint of dissent in Chelsea and Conte's camp this season.
In the end, the Italian boss was given the opportunity to prove the versatility and flexibility of his squad in Costa's absence as Willian, Eden Hazard and Pedro provided the attacking threat.

In the absence of any suggestion Blues owner Roman Abramovich will bow to pressure to sell in January, the priority now is to get the combustible Spain striker back on side and ensure any unrest does not spread.
If that can be done, then Chelsea can look back at a weekend where their title position was strengthened as they now stand seven points clear.
Verdict: Back on track and clear title favourites.
Form: Won past six.
Upcoming fixtures: 21 January - Manchester City (away), 31 January - Sunderland (away), 4 February - Middlesbrough (home).
Mauricio Pochettino's side are a growing force in this title race and the 4-0 demolition of West Bromwich Albion was further evidence of their growing authority.
It was their sixth straight league win since their loss at Manchester United in December - and they have only lost two games out of 21.
Harry Kane is firing on all cylinders, shown by his hat-trick against West Brom, and with Dele Alli scoring seven goals in his past five league games Spurs are starting to look the full package.

# Continuation

The attacking prowess on display has been complemented by a tightening of their defensive vulnerabilities, with Pochettino's tactical adjustments proving instrumental in their recent resurgence.
However, their upcoming fixture list presents a stern examination of their credentials, beginning with the away trip to Manchester City that will test their mettle against another genuine contender.
Spurs' ability to maintain this momentum through a challenging period could prove decisive in determining whether they can sustain their challenge to Chelsea's lead.
The midfield balance between creativity and industry has been particularly impressive, with Christian Eriksen orchestrating play with the kind of consistency that title-winning sides require.
If Kane and Alli can continue their prolific form while the supporting cast maintains defensive solidity, Spurs could yet emerge as genuine threats to Chelsea's dominance.
Their recent performances suggest Pochettino has finally assembled a squad capable of competing at the highest level.
Verdict: Genuine title contenders with the quality to trouble Chelsea.
Media playback is not supported on this device It's 1997 and ID7 television are filming at Dunkirk rugby club in northern France.
Their subject is in his mid-30s, has a full head of hair and is strangely familiar.
He's not French, but he's fluent.
Only when the cameraman asks him to say his name does the penny drop. "
Je m'appelle Vernon Anthony Cotter."
For four and a half minutes he doesn't miss a beat - and why would he?
Cotter had been in the country for years by then.

Four seasons playing in the back row with Rumilly in the south-east, two years with Lourdes in the foothills of the Pyrenees, a year with Saint-Junien in west-central France and he would soon kick on for the small southern village of Castelnau-Riviere-Basse, where he played and coached while briefly contemplating a new life as a winemaker.
Cotter is now sitting in Murrayfield talking about the past, the present, the future.
The New Zealander has been Scotland coach for 31 Test matches - 16 victories, 15 defeats, nine of which were by seven points or less, five of them by three or less.
He's given a first cap to 26 players.
Eighteen of the squad he has just named for the Six Nations have appeared on his watch.
He has just five more Tests before France reclaims him.
Montpellier await.
Time for reflection, then.
Time to remember where Cotter came from and where he has taken Scotland in his two and a half eventful years.

# Continuation

His journey from those quiet French villages to one of rugby's most demanding international posts seems almost improbable when you trace it backwards.
Yet there's a thread of consistency running through Cotter's career - a willingness to embrace challenge, to learn languages and cultures, to build something meaningful from modest beginnings.
The French rugby community shaped him in ways that perhaps only someone who has lived there could understand.
It taught him patience, attention to detail, and how to coax the best from players who might not have world-class resources at their disposal.
When Scotland came calling in late 2022, he brought those lessons with him to Murrayfield.
The early months were chaotic - injuries, inconsistency, a squad struggling to find its identity.
But Cotter never panicked.
He had managed bigger crises in smaller towns across France.
He had learned from coaches like Philippe Saint-André and understood that building a team was like building a house: you needed solid foundations before you could add the ornaments.
His selection policy has been bold, sometimes controversial, but always purposeful.
The young players he's blooded will form the backbone of Scotland's future.
As he contemplates his final weeks in the job, there's no sense of regret, only the quiet satisfaction of someone who has given everything he had to give.
Montpellier will get a coach who understands European rugby intimately, who speaks the language, who knows how to survive and thrive in a competitive environment.
But first, there are five more Tests to play.
Head of the Army General Sir Nick Carter said the move was about trying to operate "smarter".
The 77th Brigade, made up of reservists and regular troops and based in Hermitage, Berkshire, will be formally created in April.
It has been inspired by the Chindits who fought in Burma in World War Two.
An Army spokesman said the unit would "play a key part in enabling the UK to fight in the information age" and that it "consists of more than just traditional capabilities".
He said: "77 Brigade is being created to draw together a host of existing and developing capabilities essential to meet the challenges of modern conflict and warfare. "

It recognises that the actions of others in a modern battlefield can be affected in ways that are not necessarily violent and it draws heavily on important lessons from our commitments to operations in Afghanistan amongst others."
Recruitment for the brigade, 42% of whose personnel will be reservists, will begin this spring.
Its members will come from the Royal Navy and RAF as well as from the Army.
One former commander of British forces in Afghanistan has warned the new operation should not mean fewer troops on the frontline.
Colonel Richard Kemp said: "My view is that this should not be done at the expense of combat troops.
Where are these 2,000 people going to come from?" "
They are likely to come from savings made in combat troops.
I think that's a mistake. "
I think the British forces have already been cut far too much in a very uncertain and increasingly dangerous world.
He acknowledged the need for this type of innovation, but said "it should be added to the forces, not created out of savings found elsewhere."
The creation of the new unit is part of a major restructuring of the military under the Army 2020 plan, which will see the military scaled down to around 82,000 regular troops in the next five years.

# Continuation

The restructuring represents a significant shift in military strategy, reflecting the evolving nature of modern threats that extend far beyond traditional battlefield confrontations.
The 77th Brigade will focus on capabilities such as public affairs, civil-military cooperation, and strategic communications, areas that have become increasingly vital in contemporary conflicts.
These skills are particularly relevant in countering misinformation and propaganda, which have become weapons in their own right in recent years.
The unit's creation acknowledges that winning hearts and minds is often as important as military superiority in achieving lasting strategic objectives.
By drawing personnel from across all three services, the brigade will benefit from diverse expertise and perspectives that can be applied to complex, multifaceted challenges.
The emphasis on reservists also reflects the changing nature of military recruitment and retention in the modern era.
Training and development for brigade members will emphasize adaptability and critical thinking rather than purely tactical military skills.
The Army hopes that this innovation will allow it to achieve more with the reduced budget and personnel numbers mandated by government spending constraints.
However, the debate over whether such specialized units should supplement or replace traditional combat forces remains contentious within military circles.
Defence analysts suggest that the success of the 77th Brigade will largely depend on how effectively it integrates with conventional military operations.
The unit's performance may also influence how other NATO allies structure their own information warfare and strategic communication capabilities.
As global conflicts increasingly play out in the digital realm as well as on physical terrain, the need for specialized units like the 77th Brigade will likely grow.
The coming years will reveal whether this innovative approach to modern warfare proves effective or whether concerns about reduced combat capability prove justified.

Media playback is not supported on this device But the West Midlands team have come a long way since their formation in 1991 and can make history on Saturday by reaching the first round of the FA Cup for the first time.
It will not be easy against the relative giants of FC United of Manchester but, whatever happens at the Aspray Arena, it will be another chapter in a remarkable story.
Sporting Khalsa are fifth in the Midland League Premier Division, the ninth tier of the English football pyramid.
On Tuesday, they won at Brocton.
The attendance was 87.
On Saturday, in excess of 2,000 supporters are expected for the visit of National League North side FC United, who play three tiers above Khalsa, in the FA Cup fourth qualifying round.
The winners will earn £12,500 and enter Monday's first-round draw alongside 2008 winners Portsmouth and Wigan, who lifted the trophy by beating Manchester City at Wembley in 2013. "

Preparations started on Sunday because we had to clear an access route for vans to bring in the marquees we are having to use because our bar is not big enough," said Inder Grewa, one of Sporting Khalsa's 12 owners, who also acts as treasurer. "
We have never before had to think about marquees because we normally only have 50 people here. "
There was a load of old metal and chairs that needed shifting.
Grass verges had to be dug and stone had to be put down to level it off. "
At our level we can't afford to pay people to clear the site.
We rely on volunteers. "
We sent one email out and had around 20 people turn up, including the manager and some of the players."
Prior to this FA Cup run, Sporting Khalsa had never had an attendance in three figures, but there were 750 at the Aspray Arena for the visit of Spalding in the third qualifying round.

On Saturday, a combination of local interest and FC United's healthy away following - many of whom are intent on making a trip to nearby Dudley to see the statue of legendary Manchester United and England player Duncan Edwards - could see three times that many turn up.

# Continuation

The club's modest facilities have been stretched to their limits in preparing for this unprecedented surge in interest.
Grewa explained that the ground's infrastructure, designed for a tiny regular crowd, has required significant temporary modifications to accommodate the expected influx.
Beyond the marquees for hospitality, the club has had to arrange additional parking, extra stewards, and enhanced security measures—luxuries that clubs at their level rarely need to consider.
The financial implications are substantial, yet the potential rewards make the investment worthwhile.
A victory would not only secure £12,500 in prize money but also generate considerable revenue from ticket sales and refreshment takings on the day.
More importantly, it would represent an achievement that could transform the club's profile and aspirations for the future.
For a club that started from nothing just over three decades ago, the prospect of rubbing shoulders with established Football League sides in the first round feels almost surreal.
Yet Sporting Khalsa's journey has been built on precisely this kind of determination and community spirit.
The owners and volunteers who have given their time to prepare the ground exemplify the ethos that has carried the club this far.
Manager and players alike have embraced the challenge with enthusiasm, treating the occasion as an opportunity rather than an impossible task.
FC United, despite their higher league status, will arrive knowing that complacency could prove costly against opponents playing with everything to gain.
Whatever the outcome on Saturday, Sporting Khalsa's remarkable progress will have captured the imagination of football fans who appreciate underdogs and underestimated teams.
This match represents more than just a cup tie; it is a validation of the club's existence and a testament to what can be achieved through collective effort and unwavering belief.
In her resignation letter she said her decision would enable her party to have a "real discussion" about its future.
Senior party members had "questioned my place in this new phase", she said.
Earlier, the 56-year-old said some Labour MPs were "dinosaurs" who failed to see "Scotland has changed forever".
Ms Lamont had wanted more autonomy for the party in Scotland and significant new powers for the Scottish Parliament.
But in an interview with the Daily Record she accused Westminster colleagues of treating Scotland like a "branch office".
Labour has 41 MPs in Scotland who will fight for their Westminster seats at next May's General Election.
The party also has 38 MSPs in Edinburgh's Holyrood parliament and they will seek re-election in 2016.
In her resignation letter to Scottish Labour chairman Jamie Glackin Ms Lamont said the referendum had "opened a new chapter in the debate about the future of the Scottish Labour Party".
She added: "In order that we can have the real discussion about how we take Scottish Labour forward, I believe it would be best if I took myself out of the equation and stepped down as leader."

She described Labour's challenge in Scotland as "serious", but added "I strongly believe that the Labour Party is not only our best chance of achieving a better, fairer Scotland, it's our only chance".
There are two versions of this story.

In one version, Scottish Labour's departing leader, Johann Lamont, accuses her Westminster colleagues - and, by implication, Ed Miliband - of undermining her and failing to grasp how much Scotland has changed.
She says some of her MP colleagues are "dinosaurs".

The alternative version is that Johann Lamont wasn't up to the job - that she failed to counter the SNP, that she failed to modernise the party sufficiently to cope with a new Scotland where people are no longer prepared to back Labour as a duty, that she failed to attract new talent who might freshen up the party's portrait for the electorate in 2016, when Holyrood goes to the polls.
Reacting to news of the resignation, SNP deputy leader Nicola Sturgeon said that Ms Lamont "carries my personal best wishes".
But she added: "There is no question that her shock resignation reveals Labour to be in complete meltdown in Scotland."
Ms Sturgeon went on to suggest that Labour's problems in Scotland were far deeper than simply a change of leadership.
She argued that the party had lost touch with Scottish voters and failed to offer a compelling vision for the country's future.
The SNP leader pointed to Labour's performance in the recent independence referendum campaign, where the party had aligned itself with the Conservative Party in the "Better Together" campaign.
This partnership, Ms Sturgeon contended, had damaged Labour's credibility among traditional working-class supporters who felt betrayed by the Westminster establishment.
Political analysts suggested that Ms Lamont's departure would trigger an internal power struggle within Scottish Labour as various factions competed to shape the party's direction.
Some observers noted that the party faced a crucial choice between moving further to the left to recapture lost ground or attempting to occupy the centre ground of Scottish politics.
The timing of her resignation, coming in the aftermath of the referendum, was seen by many as symbolic of Labour's broader struggles in Scotland.
Senior figures within the party were expected to consider whether a new leader could revitalise the organisation before the 2016 Holyrood elections.
Meanwhile, Westminster Labour officials expressed concern that instability in Scotland could damage the party's prospects in the upcoming General Election.
The resignation also raised questions about the relationship between the Scottish party and the wider UK Labour organisation under Ed Miliband's leadership.
Party strategists worried that a prolonged leadership vacuum in Scotland could allow the SNP to consolidate its growing dominance.
Ms Lamont's decision to step down was ultimately seen as an acknowledgment that Scottish Labour needed fundamental reform to survive and compete effectively in the transformed political landscape.
French PM Manuel Valls and US Secretary of State John Kerry said civilians were dying in Russian air strikes.
Russian PM Dmitry Medvedev said there was "no evidence of our bombing civilians, even though everyone is accusing us of this".
One observers' group says at least 1,015 civilians have been killed in Russian air strikes.
The UK-based Syrian Observatory for Human Rights said late last month that close to a quarter of those killed were under the age of 18.

How Putin is succeeding in Syria Displaced Syrians struggle to survive Syria: The story of the conflict Russia, an ally of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, has consistently denied hitting civilian targets and insists it is battling terrorists.
Mr Valls said his government "respects Russia and Russia's interests" but "that to re-discover the path to peace, to discussion, the Russian bombing of civilians has to stop".
Mr Kerry again accused Russia of using so-called 'dumb bombs' in Syria that do not hit precise targets.
Last week, he said women and children were being killed "in large numbers" by Russian raids.
Mr Medvedev said Russia was "not trying to achieve some secret goals in Syria", adding that "we are trying to protect our national interests".
Their comments were made at a security conference in Munich, days after world powers agreed a deal to push for a cessation of hostilities in Syria within a week.

Agreement to try to bring about a cessation of hostilities and allow more access for humanitarian aid was reached by world powers late on Thursday in Germany, but neither the Syrian government nor the rebels were involved.
Under the plan, efforts will be made to try to make urgent aid deliveries to besieged and hard-to-reach areas in Syria.
Steps will also be taken to work towards an eventual ceasefire and implementation of a UN-backed plan for political transition in Syria.
The halt will not apply to the battle against jihadist groups Islamic State (IS) and al-Nusra Front.
The tentative deal reached here in Munich to pause the fighting in Syria was always going to be a tough sell to the warring parties on the ground.
Now its chances of success look even slimmer - "Forty-nine per cent," said Russia's foreign minister. "

# Continuation

The cautious optimism expressed by international diplomats appeared to evaporate almost as quickly as it had formed.
Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov's bleak assessment reflected the deep skepticism that had characterized previous attempts to broker peace in Syria's brutal five-year conflict.
The fundamental disagreement over who constituted a "terrorist" organization remained a critical stumbling block in negotiations.
While Russia and the Assad government considered most opposition groups to be terrorists, Western nations and their allies drew sharper distinctions between moderate rebels and extremist factions.
This definitional dispute threatened to undermine the entire framework of the ceasefire agreement before it could even be implemented.
Humanitarian organizations warned that without rapid progress, the suffering of Syrian civilians would only intensify in the coming weeks.
Millions had already been displaced by the conflict, with many fleeing to overcrowded refugee camps in neighboring countries.
The international community faced mounting pressure to deliver tangible results, yet the political will to enforce any agreement remained questionable.
Aid workers on the ground expressed frustration at the slow pace of negotiations while people continued to die from violence and preventable diseases.
The Munich agreement represented perhaps the last genuine chance for diplomatic intervention before the conflict spiraled further into chaos.
As delegations returned to their respective capitals, the real test would come in the actions taken—or not taken—over the following days and weeks.
The world watched and waited to see whether the tentative ceasefire would hold or whether Syria would descend further into darkness.
In the press release for the new record The Systems Are Failing, the musician writes: "We're destroying the world, and we're still miserable.
Fat, sick, stupid and anxious are no ways to live. "
These systems are failing.
Let them fail.
Change or die."
What makes this different from a Moby album?

I think a lot of people think of me as making sort of down-tempo quiet, electronic living room music and this record is much more of a fast, aggressive post-punk record and, based on the reactions I've had so far, people have been a little nonplussed at that.
What reactions specifically?
Do people think you're crazy or are they applauding a brave new direction?

Both in equal measure, part of what led me to want to make a record like this, is the awareness that people don't really buy records any more and people especially don't buy records from 51-year-old musicians who are making their 15th record, so with those two things in mind there's also a sense of emancipation that comes with that.
You can either bemoan the fact that, in 2016 the audience for albums is quite small, but I see it as being really liberating because there's no external pressure at all.
I'm not trying to sell records, I'm not trying to get on the radio, I'm honestly just trying to make a record that excites me and that I think is interesting.
You've been very commercially successful and some might say that puts you in the privileged position of not having to worry about it.
Yes and it would be hard for me to argue that.

I would feel absurdly presumptuous criticising anyone else's choices, there are a lot of musicians who have children or alimony payments or sick relatives, so they need to make commercial compromises in order to pay the rent.
I'm lucky because I live a relatively simple life and I can make whatever music I want to make without any worry about any commercial ramifications.

# Continuation

That freedom is something I've earned through years of work, but I'm also aware that not everyone has that luxury.
Still, I think there's something valuable in demonstrating that there's an alternative to the relentless pursuit of commercial viability.
Making art purely because it matters to you, because it needs to exist—that's a radical act in itself.
The post-punk sound felt necessary, urgent even.
There's an anger in this record that I couldn't have made ten years ago, and I'm not sure I would have wanted to.
Age and perspective change you; you see the systems more clearly, you understand their failures more intimately.
The aggression in these songs isn't just musical—it's philosophical.
I wanted the sound to match the message, to make people uncomfortable if they were expecting the ambient soundscapes they associate with my name.
Some listeners will dismiss it as a midlife crisis or a desperate grab for relevance, and I understand that criticism even if I don't agree with it.
But the people who get it, who hear what I'm trying to say, they seem to really connect with it.
That's enough for me.
In the end, this record is a conversation with myself about what it means to create art when the world is burning and most people are too distracted to notice or care.
It's bleak, yes, but there's also hope embedded in that bleakness—the hope that articulating the problem is the first step toward change.
The biggest Commons clash is likely to be over Sunday trading - where a well organised group of Tory rebels looks set to line up with Labour, to try to strike down proposals to loosen restrictions.
The SNP look likely to be the swing vote in this particular debate.
Elsewhere, there's a new and multi-faceted crime bill, and in the Lords there's some preliminary sparring over the Trade Union Bill.
There may also be votes on the Immigration Bill.
But it looks as if the Commons-Lords "ping-pong" on the proposals to reform Employment Support Allowance for some disabled people may be petering out.
Here's my rundown of the week ahead: The Commons opens at 2.30pm for Education questions.
As usual any post weekend ministerial statements or urgent questions will follow at 3.30pm.

Then it's the launch of the Policing and Crime Bill, a wide-ranging measure which will: In the Lords (2.30pm) after the usual hour of questions to ministers, it's the latest stage of the parliamentary ping-pong over the Welfare Reform and Work Bill.

After MPs rejected a Lords amendment for the second time, it's rumoured peers may drop their resistance to the Commons over benefits for disabled people in the "Work Related Activities Group", and content themselves with a "regret" motion.

Then peers debate the Tax Credits (Income Thresholds and determination of Rates) Regulations 2016 - where the Lib Dems have put down another "regret motion" but seem unlikely to attract Labour support.
And that's followed by an International Women's Day debate on the progress of women's representation and empowerment in the UK, 150 years after the 1866 petition for women's suffrage.

There are 27 peers down to speak, including two notable maidens - the Conservative Baroness Mone and the Bishop of Gloucester, Rachel Treweek, who will become the first woman Bishop to speak in the Lords.
The Commons opens (11.30am) with Justice questions, followed at 12.30pm by a Ten Minute Rule Bill from the Conservative, Will Quince.

He's calling for a first aid component to be added to the driving test, continuing the backbench campaign for more first aid training which recently saw a private member's bill which attempted to require First Aid to be taught in schools.

He says this would substantially increase the number of Britons with knowledge of first aid - countries like Germany, Switzerland and the Czech Republic already require evidence of first aid training as a condition of having a full driving licence.
Mr Quince claims the support of the British Red Cross and St John's Ambulance.
The debate promises to be passionate, with campaigners arguing that basic life-saving skills could make the difference between life and death in emergency situations on our roads.
The Government has not yet committed to backing the proposal, though ministerial sources suggest they are "considering the evidence" presented by first aid organisations.
After the Ten Minute Rule Bill, the Commons will move on to the main business of the day - the second reading of the Crime Bill, which is expected to dominate proceedings.
This legislation covers everything from police powers to sentencing guidelines, and several backbenchers have already indicated their intention to speak.
The debate is likely to be lengthy, potentially extending well into the evening as MPs from across the chamber seek to air their concerns and support various clauses.
The Crime Bill's provisions on stop and search powers have already attracted criticism from civil liberties groups, who worry about potential discrimination and overreach.
Meanwhile, in the Lords at 2.30pm, peers will continue their examination of the Trade Union Bill with committee stage proceedings.
This remains one of the most contentious pieces of legislation before Parliament, with unions warning of severe consequences for workers' rights.
The Government maintains the Bill is necessary to ensure industrial action is used responsibly and democratically.
Amendments are expected to be tabled throughout the day, with Labour and crossbench peers likely to mount significant challenges to key provisions.
By Wednesday evening, the parliamentary timetable suggests the Commons will have made substantial progress on the Crime Bill, though completion seems unlikely before Thursday.
Northern Ireland's McIlroy, twice a winner in seven US PGA starts, bogeyed the last for a one-under 69 to finish three over and miss the cut by one.
American Walker also bogeyed the 18th for a 66 and nine-under total, matched by Streb who birdied his last for 63.
Defending champion Jason Day had eight birdies in a 65 to get to seven under.

The Australian world number one is tied third with Argentina's Emiliano Grillo (67), while Open winner Henrik Stenson of Sweden is one shot back after carding a second three-under 67 to move to six under.
Germany's Martin Kaymer (69), who won this tournament in 2010, and Americans Patrick Reed (65) and Brooks Koepka (67) are four adrift on five under.
Wales' Jamie Donaldson (67) is the highest-placed Briton at the final major of the season on four under, one ahead of world number three Jordan Spieth, who also shot 67.
With the cut mark hovering between one and two over par, McIlroy, who holed a 30-foot putt on the 17th to get to two over, thought he needed another birdie on the last.
The four-time major winner hit his second on the par-five 18th into greenside rough and took three more shots to get on the green before holing out for a bogey. "
I thought I needed to make four, so that's what I was trying to do," said the 2012 and 2014 US PGA champion. "
It was a tough lie.
I hit the first one as hard as I really could considering how close the pin was to the edge of the green."
The world number four went straight to the practice green after taking 35 putts in round one but he said his efforts on Friday, when he took 30, were "pathetic". "
Putting let me down at [the US Open in June at] Oakmont and then putting let me down here again," he said. "
My tee to green game - there's not much wrong with that.
It's pretty solid.
If you had given anyone else in this field my tee shots this week, they would have been up near the top of the leaderboard. "

# Continuation

McIlroy's frustration was palpable as he reflected on a tournament that had slipped away due to inconsistency on the greens.
Despite hitting 14 fairways and finding most of the greens in regulation, the Northern Irishman had simply been unable to convert opportunities when it mattered most.
His departure from Baltusrol marked a disappointing end to what had been a promising week off the tee.
The 27-year-old acknowledged that his short game issues would need addressing before the next major championship.
For now, his focus shifted to understanding where the mechanics of his stroke had gone awry.
McIlroy had worked extensively with his coach Michael Bannon over the previous weeks, but the results on the greens suggested more work lay ahead.
The missed cut represented only the second time in his last twelve major championship appearances that he had failed to make the weekend.
Looking ahead, McIlroy knew he would need to rebuild confidence in his putting stroke through practice and patience.
His tee-to-green game remained world-class, a fact that offered some consolation as he packed his bags and departed New Jersey.
The silver lining was that the season still offered opportunities to reassert himself among the game's elite.
With several important tournaments on the horizon, McIlroy remained optimistic about his prospects.
Still, the missed cut at a major would sting for some time, serving as a reminder that even the best players must execute across all facets of their game.
For the remaining competitors at Baltusrol, however, the weekend promised compelling golf as the leaderboard took shape.
David Anderson QC said his "central concern" about the proposals first unveiled by David Cameron in early September was: "Where are the courts?"
Temporary Exclusion Orders are one of the measures in the counter terrorism bill published by Theresa May.
It also includes tougher powers to stop people going abroad to fight.
It will include plans to stop some British citizens returning to the UK, and others from leaving the country.

In other developments on Wednesday: Analysis by Political Correspondent Robin Brant The government proposes the law, parliament passes it, then David Anderson reviews it - and sometimes makes recommendations.
That's the usual sequence of events.
But today the independent reviewer was criticising the process before the new Counter-Terror and Security Bill was published.
The QC used his customary polite, sometimes understated, tone - but this was a criticism of politicians whom he said hadn't fully thought through some of the proposals announced in early September.
Read more from Robin Mrs May's new measures to tackle terrorism come days after she said the UK faces a "greater" terror threat than ever before.

The measures include requiring airlines to pass on details of their passengers and changes to the way TPIMs - Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measures - work to monitor terror suspects who cannot be prosecuted.
But campaigners have condemned it, saying it threatens civil liberties. '

A more sensible way' Mr Anderson - appointed by the government to be its independent reviewer of terrorism legislation - said the new anti-terror legislation was "nothing like as dramatic" as David Cameron had proposed earlier this year.
He told the Joint Committee on Human Rights the original plan to block suspected British jihadists returning from Iraq and Syria as "an announcement waiting for a policy".
But he said it soon became clear such a move would "neither legally or practically" work and the current plan was now much "closer to managed return".
He argued there could be "a more sensible" way of dealing with some people suspected of being involved in terrorist activities. "
One could look at it in terms of young, possibly vulnerable people caught up with the wrong crowd in Syria - didn't really know exactly what they were doing," he said. "
"One could look at it in terms of young, possibly vulnerable people caught up with the wrong crowd in Syria - didn't really know exactly what they were doing," he said. "
There might be a case for some kind of mentoring or rehabilitation programme rather than simply locking them up."
Mr Anderson emphasized that any new legislation must maintain the proper balance between security and the protection of fundamental rights.
He warned that temporary exclusion orders, in particular, risked creating a legal vacuum where individuals could be denied access to courts to challenge decisions made against them.
The QC stressed that judicial oversight was not merely a procedural nicety but essential to the legitimacy of counter-terrorism measures.
Without courts able to scrutinize the government's actions, he argued, the entire system would lack accountability and public confidence.
He pointed out that previous counter-terrorism legislation had benefited from careful judicial review, which had actually strengthened rather than weakened security outcomes.
Mrs May's office responded by stating that the government remained committed to working with Mr Anderson throughout the legislative process.
A spokesman said the measures were carefully calibrated to address genuine security threats while respecting human rights obligations.
