The Counter-Terror and Security Bill would now go through parliamentary scrutiny, where both houses would have the opportunity to debate and amend the proposals.
Civil liberties groups have already indicated they will mount significant campaigns against several aspects of the legislation.
The coming weeks are likely to see intense debate about whether the government has struck the right balance between protecting national security and preserving individual freedoms.
In a Commons debate, the ex-Labour leader said Conservative plans to limit the voting powers of Scottish MPs on Commons laws would "rip up" hundreds of years of parliamentary procedure.
The SNP said the Conservatives wanted to create a "quasi-English Parliament".
But ministers said it was vital England was treated fairly as further powers were devolved to other parts of the UK.
At the end of the debate, Labour staged and won a vote in which the government abstained.
And Conservative MP David Davis raised a point of order to urge the government to allow more time for the matter to be considered.

The government believes bills applying exclusively to England should not become law without the explicit consent of MPs from English constituencies and it wants to change Commons rules known as standing orders to give them a "decisive say" during their passage.

Ministers say this will address the longstanding anomaly by which Scottish MPs can vote on issues such as health and education affecting England but English MPs have no say on similar matters relating to Scotland, where such policies are devolved.
Mark D'Arcy, BBC Parliamentary correspondent Panic stations?
From the point of view of the government whips this afternoon's emergency Commons debate on English Votes for English Laws was really rather alarming.

The debate, so skilfully secured by Lib Dem ex Scottish secretary Alistair Carmichael, produced a slightly pointless vote in which the Conservatives mostly abstained, but it brought all kinds of nasty tensions to the surface.

Conservative MPs are supposed to be signed up for EVEL under the terms of their manifesto, but there were clearly quite a few with doubts, some about the policy, many more about the process, which was Mr Carmichael's line of attack.
Read the article in full However, the plans came under sustained attack from Labour, SNP and Lib Dems in an urgent debate, secured by former Scottish Secretary Alistair Carmichael.

MPs are due to debate and vote on the government's plans next week but Mr Carmichael, the MP for Orkney and Shetland, said the plans required more detailed consideration, arguably through primary legislation.
If MPs representing constituencies in England had a "veto" on certain legislation, it would breach the longstanding principle that MPs, no matter who they represented, were "all equal". "
To go as far as the government wants to go in the timescale they want to go brings with it an attendant level of risk that I would consider to be irresponsible," he said. "
They are not entitled to use the UK Parliament as a proxy for an English Parliament."

# Continuation

The debate revealed deep divisions within the Conservative Party itself, with several backbenchers expressing concern that the proposed changes were being rushed through without adequate scrutiny.

Some questioned whether the government had properly consulted with Scottish, Welsh, and Northern Irish MPs before attempting to alter the constitutional arrangements that had governed Parliament for centuries.

The SNP representatives argued passionately that the changes would fundamentally undermine the principle of equal representation that underpins parliamentary democracy, creating a two-tier system of MPs with different powers depending on their constituency.
Labour's position was that while there were legitimate questions about fairness, the solution should involve genuine constitutional reform rather than ad-hoc procedural changes implemented in haste.
Alistair Carmichael's intervention proved particularly effective because it forced the government to confront the procedural irregularities in how they were attempting to implement the policy.
The abstention by Conservative ministers during the vote was widely interpreted as a tacit acknowledgment that the government's position was weaker than initially presented.

Political observers noted that the debate had exposed the tension between the government's desire to deliver on its manifesto commitment and the practical difficulties of implementing such a significant constitutional change.
The coming week would be crucial, with MPs set to return to the issue with considerably more attention from both the media and their constituents.
For the government whips, the challenge would be to shore up support among their own backbenchers while maintaining the coalition's overall parliamentary majority.

The broader question looming over the entire debate was whether EVEL could ever be properly implemented without a comprehensive constitutional settlement that addressed the asymmetries between England and the other home nations.
Opposition parties were clearly hoping to use the next week's debate to build further pressure on the government and potentially force a rethink of the timeline.
Meanwhile, constitutional experts were already preparing detailed critiques of the government's proposals, which would likely be circulated among MPs before the crucial vote.

The New South Wales premier's page was flooded with comments, almost all negative, after he mounted a defence of controversial "lockout laws" that many say have killed off Sydney's night-time economy.
Mr Baird says the laws are reducing assaults, and that a number of small bars have opened since the laws were introduced.

But questions have also been raised about why the city's Star Casino and another planned casino are exempt - the Star falls just outside the zone - leading to the premier being mocked with the hashtag #casinomike.

Since February 2014, bars and clubs within parts of Sydney have been required to shut their doors to new patrons from 1.30 in the morning and stop serving drinks at 3:00, with severe penalties for bar owners who breach the conditions.
Sales of takeaway alcohol from hotels and shops are banned statewide after 22:00.

The origin of these laws can be traced to July 2012, when 19-year-old Kieran Loveridge walked up to Thomas Kelly in the entertainment precinct of Kings Cross and, without provocation or warning, punched him in the head.
Mr Kelly, 19, fell to the ground and later died in hospital.
Kings Cross saw its second "coward punch" death on New Year's Eve in 2013, when bodybuilder Shaun McNeil, 27, killed 18-year-old Daniel Christie with a single blow in an unprovoked attack.
Both attackers were jailed.
Public outcry over these incidents was immense, and the NSW government responded by designating Sydney's most popular party zones a "CBD Entertainment Precinct".
But it had a devastating impact on businesses that depended on late-night trade, particularly around the Kings Cross area.
Some of Australia's best-known bars and clubs shut down, among them Hugos Lounge, which had operated for 15 years and was voted best nightclub in Australia six times.
Owner Dave Evans said the laws caused a 60% drop in trade, forcing him to close his doors and put 170 staff out of work.
Frustration over the laws' impact on Sydney's nightlife crystalysed last week with the publication on LinkedIn of an 8,000-word essay by businessman Matt Barrie.
Mr Barrie passionately denounced the impact of the laws and pushed a philosophy of personal responsibility that touched a nerve with many Sydneysiders. "

You've been tricked into thinking that you have done something wrong, in some way that you are genetically an idiot, or that somehow you have to feel responsible for a couple of random tragic, yet unrelated, events that occurred in the vague proximity of having fun," he wrote. "
The essay went viral, accumulating hundreds of thousands of shares and reigniting a fierce debate about whether the lockout laws had overreached in their response to isolated incidents of violence.
Critics argued that the government had essentially punished an entire industry and hundreds of thousands of law-abiding citizens for the actions of a handful of individuals.
They contended that the laws treated Sydney's adults like children incapable of making their own decisions about when and where to socialise.

Meanwhile, supporters of the legislation pointed to statistics showing reductions in assault-related hospital admissions in the affected areas, though some researchers questioned whether the laws were solely responsible for these improvements.

The debate also highlighted a broader tension between public safety and personal freedom, with many arguing that other cities managed vibrant nightlife scenes without resorting to such draconian measures.
International comparisons were drawn, with observers noting that cities like London, New York, and Melbourne had found alternative solutions that didn't require blanket closures.
Young people expressed particular frustration, feeling that their generation was being denied the cultural experiences that previous generations had enjoyed.
Business owners continued to report struggling revenues, with some establishments attempting to adapt by repositioning themselves as premium venues with higher prices to maintain profitability.
The hashtag #casinomike continued to trend, with users pointing out the apparent hypocrisy of allowing casinos to operate around the clock while restricting ordinary bars and clubs.
Local politicians faced mounting pressure from both sides—constituents demanding the laws be repealed and safety advocates insisting they be maintained or even strengthened.
The NSW government announced it would conduct a review of the laws, though many remained skeptical about whether any meaningful changes would actually be implemented.

As the controversy deepened, it became clear that Sydney's nightlife would not return to its former glory without significant policy shifts, leaving the city's entertainment industry in a state of prolonged uncertainty.
Regardless of the horse race that sprung from Lord Derby calling it right and winning that toss at Epsom in 1779, defeat would have had a much wider significance.
The event and its name are believed to be the root of the expression 'derby match', and it's worth considering that the loser was Sir Charles Bunbury.
You'll agree the 'Merseyside Bunbury' or the 'North London Bunbury' hardly have the same ring.

More than two centuries on from that coin toss to decide whose name the race should take, trainer John Gosden - who is due to have five runners in the 238th Derby, including big fancy Cracksman - marvels at what was created. "
It's a unique track," he says, staring out over the course as it snakes its way up, down and around the Surrey Downs barely 15 miles south west of central London. "
Obviously Bunbury and Derby had had a very good lunch when they came up here and pegged it out and flipped a coin. "
It's a very demanding track.
You have to stay, you have to have great dexterity, agility, balance and a turn of foot comes in very handy. "
It is a proper test of a horse - you wouldn't necessarily design it as a track for anything other than a rigorous test of a three-year-old in the first week of June, and that's what it is."
Gosden, whose ever-powerful string based at Newmarket is the biggest threat to the prevailing dominance of Ireland's Aidan O'Brien, should know.
It's 20 years since he saddled Benny The Dip to win, and two since he masterminded Golden Horn's big-race success with Frankie Dettori riding in the silks of owner-breeder Anthony Oppenheimer.
Golden Horn, who by that point had won the regularly significant Dante Stakes at York, went on to stamp his authority on proceedings that season.
After Epsom, he galloped off with the Eclipse Stakes, the Irish Champion Stakes and the Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe before suffering one of just two defeats, at the Breeders' Cup fixture in Kentucky.

Cracksman represents the same owner-trainer-jockey combination, and comes to Epsom with two wins from two starts, the second at Epsom when narrowly beating fellow Derby hopeful - and this year's Dante winner - Permian. "
Cracksman is not a Golden Horn," Gosden told BBC Sport, "but he's a superior horse to Benny The Dip. "
He's still learning a lot, and when he came here and won the trial he grew up overnight, so we're excited."
The confidence in Cracksman's potential is tempered by Gosden's characteristic realism about the task ahead.
The Derby demands more than mere trial success; it requires a combination of physical prowess and mental fortitude that separates champions from capable performers.
Permian, the Dante Stakes winner, will undoubtedly pose a serious threat, having proven his credentials over the Epsom layout in their previous encounter.
Yet the racing world knows that form lines can shift dramatically between spring and early summer, and the first week of June brings its own particular challenges.
The ground conditions, the weather, and the unpredictable nature of three-year-old thoroughbreds all conspire to make predictions hazardous at best.
What Gosden has consistently demonstrated throughout his career is an ability to have his horses primed for the major occasions, a skill honed through decades of top-level competition.
The Epsom track itself remains the great leveller, its undulating terrain and tight turns testing every aspect of a horse's athleticism.

For Cracksman to join the pantheon of Derby winners, he must overcome not just Permian and the other forty-plus runners, but also the demands of a course that has humbled many a highly fancied favourite.
The prize money and prestige attached to winning the Derby ensure that the very best three-year-olds in Europe will be represented in the field.
Gosden's five runners give him multiple chances, though concentration on one clear target is often the smarter strategy in such a competitive environment.
As the countdown to the race intensifies, all eyes will be on how Cracksman handles the mounting pressure and expectation.
The coin toss between Derby and Bunbury all those years ago set in motion events that continue to captivate racing enthusiasts worldwide.
Media playback is not supported on this device The Dons took the lead when Jonny Hayes' corner landed at the near post, with Caley Thistle's Gary Warren deflecting in Ash Taylor's shot.
Greg Tansey headed wide for the visitors, while Hayes, Kenny McLean and Niall McGinn all missed for the Dons.
Miles Storey should have made it 2-0 but somehow got the ball caught under his feet on the goal line.
The result moves Aberdeen 13 points clear of third-placed Rangers, with the two sides set to meet on Sunday at Pittodrie.

Aberdeen may have been unable to mount a serious title challenge this season, but they are still on course for a record points tally after surpassing the run of home wins set by Sir Alex Ferguson's side in 1986.
They now need 12 points from the last seven matches to do that, but an overall run of 12 victories in their last 14 Premiership matches suggests they are up to the task.

Aberdeen impressed with their pace, intensity and variety of movement going forward in the first half, but the defence remains equally effective as they have now gone 531 minutes without conceding a goal.
Of course this was another painful night for Caley Thistle manager Richie Foran in what has been a steep learning curve for the Dubliner in his first season in the job.

No team in British senior football have won fewer league matches than the Highlanders this season and, while they are not yet entirely cut adrift at the bottom, time is fast running out to turn things around.
The sight of five former Caley Thistle players in such an impressive Dons squad was compounded by the knowledge that current top league marksman Greg Tansey is moving to Pittodrie in the summer.

The midfielder is desperate to leave with the club still in the Premiership, but he missed one of their best chances of the evening when he headed a Henri Anier cross over the bar from six yards in the first half.
The real disappointment for Foran, though, will be that the goal they conceded was a gift for Aberdeen.
It was poor marking by the Caley Thistle defence as Dons pair Taylor and Andrew Considine contested a Hayes corner, the ball eventually deflected in by captain Warren off Taylor's strike.
Despite a battling performance, Caley Thistle simply could not find a way back into the match.
They did have a late let off, though, when former Inverness player Storey had an incredible miss when he failed to convert from point-blank range after a Hayes shot came off the post.

Aberdeen manager Derek McInnes: "We didn't have the same rhythm or flow to our game as we normally have, but there were a number of factors for that as we had the game in our pocket but didn't know whether to stick or twist. "
We created chances but didn't take them, which is typical of where we are at the moment in terms of our consistency.
The important thing is that we got the three points and we move on to a big game on Sunday.
Caley Thistle will feel they deserved something from the match given their work rate and commitment, but ultimately they were undone by a moment of defensive lapse.
Foran's side has shown character in recent weeks, but they need to start converting their efforts into results if they are to mount a survival bid.
For Aberdeen, the focus now shifts entirely to the Rangers clash, which represents another opportunity to extend their lead at the top of the table.
McInnes will be keen to see his side maintain their defensive solidity while improving their attacking efficiency in the final third.
The manager also has the luxury of knowing that Greg Tansey's departure in the summer won't affect their immediate prospects, given the strength in depth of his current squad.
Looking ahead, Aberdeen's pursuit of that historic points record remains very much within reach, but they cannot afford any slip-ups in the coming weeks.
The consistency they have shown over the past few months suggests they have the mentality and quality to see it through to the end of the season.
Meanwhile, Caley Thistle must regroup and prepare for their upcoming fixtures with renewed determination and focus.
Every remaining match is now crucial for their survival hopes, and Foran will be demanding a marked improvement in both defensive discipline and clinical finishing.
The contrast between these two sides' seasons could hardly be starker, yet both will be determined to make a statement when they meet again later in the campaign.

And, on a drive between the Republican Convention in Cleveland, Ohio and the Democratic Convention in Philadelphia, I gained an insight into another factor adding to the unpredictability - the growing numbers of Americans who are switching, or thinking of switching, party allegiances.
In some cases, they are changing the voting habits of a lifetime.
The first switcher was just a few blocks from the Republican convention location.
Tim - who didn't want to give his last name - is a lawyer who works in Cleveland's Key Tower, the tallest building between Chicago and Philadelphia.
A lifelong Republican, he believes that Mr Trump is not just telling tall stories, but dangerous ones. "
I am very concerned about any leader who appeals to the worst instincts of human nature.
That's very dangerous for the country and I love my country more than I love my party."
So will he vote for Hillary Clinton? "
I may.
I haven't quite got there, but I definitely won't vote for Donald Trump.
Anyone who knows me knows how ridiculous that sounds, that I would even think of voting for her."
I heard a very different view in the run-down town of Warren, 50 miles east of Cleveland.
At the 422 restaurant, the only place to eat on what local people say was once a bustling strip, I met a group of disillusioned Democrats.
They used to work at the town's steel mill, which is now nothing more than a fenced-off patch of wasteland.
As jobs, opportunities and hopes have disappeared, so too has the loyalty they felt - or were encouraged to feel by the unions - towards the Democratic Party.
During the 35 years that Joe Shrodek worked in the mill's blast furnace, he always voted for Democratic presidential candidates.

# Continuation

But this year, he is seriously considering voting for Donald Trump. "
The Democrats have done nothing for us," he said, gesturing toward the empty storefronts lining the street. "
We've been forgotten.
Trump says he'll bring back manufacturing jobs, and maybe he's telling the truth, maybe he's not, but at least he's talking about us."
His sentiment was echoed by others at the table, who spoke bitterly about trade deals they blamed for shipping their livelihoods overseas.
They felt betrayed by a party that had taken their votes for granted while their community crumbled around them.
One woman, who had worked as an administrative assistant at the mill for twenty years, said she couldn't remember the last time a Democratic politician had visited Warren. "
They come during election season and disappear," she said. "
Trump's actually been here.
He's paid attention to places like this."
What struck me most was not their anger at Democrats, but their desperation—a yearning for someone, anyone, to acknowledge their suffering and offer a path forward.
These switchers represented something the pollsters were struggling to capture: a fundamental realignment happening beneath the surface of American politics.
The old coalitions that had held for decades were fracturing.
Blue-collar workers who had inherited Democratic loyalty from their parents were walking away, while educated, suburban Republicans were questioning their party's direction.
Nobody could predict with certainty how many would actually follow through on their doubts when they stepped into the voting booth.
The play, written by Jack Thorne, is set 19 years after the seventh and final book in the series by JK Rowling.
It opens officially at the Palace Theatre, in London's West End, on Saturday.
Audiences have been urged to "keep the secrets" since the play began previews in early June.
Presented in two parts, the play - showing the stars of the wizarding saga as adults in their mid-30s as their own children head off to school - stretches over five hours.

Daily Telegraph critic Dominic Cavendish awarded the play five stars, writing: "British theatre hasn't known anything like it for decades and I haven't seen anything directly comparable in all my reviewing days."
He said "those involved can give themselves a pat on the back", adding: "It's a triumph.
Not an unqualified one - there are some quibbles - but in all key respects, it grips, it stirs, it delights."

Cavendish praises the "thrill-a-minute" stage craft which sees pupils heading to Hogwarts, at the start of the play, change into school uniforms "in the blink of an eye" as they head to the infamous Platform 9Â¾ to make their way to school.

The Stage also gives a five-star review, with Mark Shenton describing Harry Potter and the Cursed Child - co-devised by Rowling, with director John Tiffany and playwright Jack Thorne - as a "truly game-changing production and a thrilling theatrical endeavour".

It is an "entirely original" piece of work, with "Dickensian sweep and momentum to the storytelling", writes Shenton, adding: "It earns its place on the stage, feeling distinct from both the books and the screen adaptations. "
By turns playful and gripping, disturbing and detailed, poignant and powerful, it is superb family entertainment."
Shenton also praises "the theatrical wizards who've created this stunningly-realised alternative universe deliver one coup de theatre after another".
In a nod to the Kings Cross platform number, Ann Treneman gives the play four and three-quarters stars in The Times, saying the "wizardry on display" is "out of this world".
She says the plot is complex ("there are mazes that are more straightforward") but adds: "It's a raunchless Game of Thrones with heart.
Crucially, it's authentic Potter but, most importantly, it's new. "
It's not the movie of the book.

# Continuation

The production has already generated considerable excitement among Harry Potter fans, with many eager to experience the story unfold on stage rather than through film or page.
Some critics have noted that the theatrical medium allows for creative interpretations that would be impossible in other formats, making use of innovative staging techniques and special effects.
The cast, featuring established stage actors alongside some familiar faces from the film franchise, has been praised for bringing depth and nuance to characters audiences thought they knew.
The decision to present the play in two parts means viewers must commit to an extended theatrical experience, though most reviewers suggest the investment of time is entirely worthwhile.
Box office records have been broken since the play opened for previews, with demand far outstripping initial expectations.
The West End's Palace Theatre has become a destination venue, drawing audiences from across the UK and beyond who are determined to witness this theatrical phenomenon firsthand.
Industry observers are already speculating about potential international tours and productions, given the apparent universal appeal of the material.
The play serves as a reminder that theatre can still offer something unique and irreplaceable in an age dominated by streaming services and cinema.
For devoted Potter fans, it represents a chance to revisit and extend their relationship with a beloved fictional universe in a completely fresh way.
The critical consensus suggests that Jack Thorne's script successfully captures the essence of Rowling's world while establishing its own distinctive theatrical identity.
US Defence Secretary Chuck Hagel said the Americans will leave RAF Mildenhall in Suffolk and RAF Alconbury and RAF Molesworth in Cambridgeshire.
The move is part of a programme to save £320m ($500m) a year across Europe.
The USAF lease the RAF bases.
Mr Hagel said he understood it would mean job losses and thanked those who had supported the US Air Force. "
I know that this will result in a reduction of our local host nations workforces at some locations; I value the tremendous support they provided us for decades."
The USAF KC-135 tanker fleet based at Mildenhall will be moved to Germany.
RAF Lakenheath, with two squadrons of new F-35 jets (48 of them) arriving by 2020, will be the aircraft's only European base.

The Pentagon said the loss of about 2,000 US military and civilian personnel is due to relocation away from Mildenhall, but will be offset by the addition of about 1,200 people stationed permanently at Lakenheath.
Matthew Hancock, West Suffolk MP and Minister of State for Business, Enterprise and Energy, said he would support the community affected.
Mildenhall had a long and proud history of strong relations with the United States Air Force and the move "will come as a shock to many", he said. "
I have met with the defence secretary, and others in government to ensure we can work, together with the American administration, to support the community. "

We will create a Mildenhall, Alconbury and Molesworth (MAM) Working Group, which I will chair, inviting local LEPs [local enterprise partnerships], councils, the Ministry of Defence and US representatives to ensure no stone is left unturned in supporting Mildenhall and the surrounding area."
A UK Ministry of Defence spokesman said: "The US Department of Defence (DoD) has been reviewing its requirement for bases across Europe as part of (its) European infrastructure consolidation review."
RAF Mildenhall is used as a transport hub by the USAF and is home to air refuelling tankers and special operations forces.
It has about 3,200 military personnel, with 400-500 UK civilian staff employed there.
Both bases are in the Forest Heath District Council area.
The consolidation review reflects broader strategic decisions by the Pentagon to streamline its global military footprint and reduce costs in the face of budgetary constraints.
European allies have generally accepted these adjustments as part of ongoing defence partnerships, though local communities have expressed concern about the economic impact.
The relocation of the KC-135 tanker fleet to Ramstein Air Base in Germany will maintain NATO's air refuelling capabilities while reducing the overall number of US bases in the United Kingdom.
RAF Lakenheath's expansion as a primary hub for the F-35 Lightning II programme underscores its strategic importance and modern infrastructure.
The arrival of the advanced fighter jets is expected to enhance NATO's defensive posture in Eastern Europe and demonstrate American commitment to the region.
Local business leaders and councillors have begun discussions about economic diversification to mitigate the effects of the USAF withdrawal.
The closure timeline has not been immediately specified, though officials suggest the transition will occur over several years to allow adequate planning.
Community leaders have emphasized the deep historical ties between the bases and surrounding towns, dating back to World War II.
Educational institutions and local suppliers that have long benefited from the American military presence are already considering how to adapt their operations.
The UK government has pledged to work closely with affected regions to explore alternative investment and employment opportunities.
Some analysts suggest that the consolidation, while economically challenging, could position remaining bases like Lakenheath as more strategically significant installations.
Regional development agencies are exploring whether the vacated base facilities could be repurposed for civilian or commercial use.
The decision ultimately reflects shifting geopolitical priorities and the Pentagon's assessment of where military resources can be most effectively deployed.
More than 7,000 children and adolescents had their mental health referrals rejected last year.
The Scottish Association for Mental Health (SAMH) described the figure as "astonishing".
The Scottish government has already said it will commission a review into rejected referrals.
But SAMH said the issue needed to be addressed as quickly as possible because young people were "waiting now to get help and support".
The charity's Jo Anderson said 17,500 people had been turned away from receiving Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) support over the past three years.
She added: "That's an astonishing figure, and we don't know what happens to them after they've been turned away.
We don't know whether they received any other support. "
In the last few weeks of the campaign we've been running, we've been inundated with calls from parents who're in that situation and their child has been rejected from receiving a CAMHS service. "
They haven't been signposted anywhere else - that's a very worrying picture."
Caitlin Wyllie-Quinn, 20, a student from Irvine, had a difficult transition to secondary school.
Bullying left her isolated and very down, crying all the time.
She got some initial help from the local Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service but after leaving therapy she went downhill rapidly.
Her GP made another referral to CAMHS but this time it was rejected.
Caitlin was told that because she was not suicidal or self-harming, her case was not serious enough. "

If the person doing the assessment had asked me if I was suicidal or self-harming I would have answered yes to both those questions, but basically I was refused help because they didn't have the capability to assess me properly," she said. "
I was at a real low point and I felt that getting referred to CAMHS was some sort of light at the end of the tunnel and finally maybe getting some help. "

# Continuation

When that was taken away from her, Caitlin's mental health deteriorated further, and she struggled alone for months without professional support.
Her story is far from unique, as countless young people across Scotland face similar barriers to accessing the care they desperately need.
The rejection left Caitlin feeling abandoned and hopeless, questioning whether anyone understood the severity of her suffering.
She eventually found support through private therapy, but not every family has the financial resources to pursue alternative options.
Many young people in her situation simply fall through the cracks, their mental health crises going unaddressed until they reach crisis point.

The SAMH report highlights a critical gap in Scotland's mental health infrastructure, where limited funding and stretched services mean vulnerable adolescents are being turned away at the moment they need help most.
Jo Anderson emphasized that the current system is failing an entire generation of young people who are silently struggling with depression, anxiety, and other mental health conditions.
The rejection criteria appear to be dangerously narrow, focusing only on immediate risk factors like suicidal ideation rather than considering the broader picture of a young person's wellbeing.
Parents and advocacy groups are calling for urgent reform to ensure that referrals are assessed more comprehensively and compassionately.

Without immediate intervention, experts warn that more young people will experience prolonged suffering and potentially develop more severe mental health conditions that could have been prevented with timely support.
The Scottish government must prioritize this issue and allocate sufficient resources to CAMHS to eliminate these devastating rejection rates.
Media playback is not supported on this device The Black Cats' 10-season spell in the top flight ended when they lost 1-0 at home to Bournemouth on Saturday and Hull City drew 0-0 at Southampton.
Moyes, who took charge at Sunderland in July last year, has faced calls from supporters to step down. "
I think it's a question for two or three weeks' time," said the Scot. "
I think we all need to reflect and look at everything from top to bottom."
Chairman Ellis Short, who has been trying to sell the club, apologised to Sunderland fans, and admitted mistakes had been made.
Since Short bought the club in 2009 - he became chairman in 2011 - the Black Cats have had seven full-time managers, but have faced the threat of relegation in almost every season. "
We need to improve both on and off the field, and despite the bitter disappointment there is a strong determination to do so throughout the club," said the American. "
There is significant work to be done over the summer and when the season is concluded, we intend to share our plans to move forward with our supporters."

Listen as fans react to Sunderland's relegation on 606 This is the first time Moyes has been relegated as a manager, and he warned fans just two games into the the season that he thought they would struggle. "

I feel for the supporters because they're the people who pay their hard-earned cash to come and watch and we've not given them enough this season," said the former Everton and Manchester United manager. "
I've had 400-odd games in the Premier League and I've got an idea of what a good squad looks like. "
My feeling at the start of the season was it was going to be a hard graft, and I'd rather be up front with people than tell them something different."
The defeat by Bournemouth was the ninth time Sunderland have failed to score in their past 10 games, and the 18th time they have failed to do so this season.
Jermain Defoe, the team's top scorer this season with 14, has not found the net in more than 15 hours of Premier League football. "
We've needed Jermain's goals," said Moyes. "
At times we haven't given him enough quality supply.

# Continuation

The manager acknowledged that defensive vulnerabilities had also plagued the team throughout their disastrous campaign, with Sunderland conceding 64 goals—more than any other side in the division.
The statistical reality painted a bleak picture of a squad that struggled at both ends of the pitch, unable to compete with even the weakest Premier League opponents.
Moyes' comments about the lack of quality in the squad have intensified speculation about potential summer transfers and the club's financial constraints moving forward.
Several key players are expected to depart in the coming weeks, with Premier League clubs circling to take advantage of Sunderland's reduced status.
The prospect of rebuilding in the Championship presents a significant challenge for a club accustomed to top-flight football for over a decade.
Fans have expressed mixed emotions about the manager's future, with some calling for wholesale changes while others believe Moyes deserves the chance to restore the club's fortunes.
The financial implications of relegation are severe, with substantial losses in broadcasting revenue and sponsorship deals already anticipated.
Short's attempts to sell the club have made an already complicated situation even more uncertain, as potential buyers weigh the costs and challenges of a Championship rebuild.
The coming weeks will be crucial in determining whether Moyes remains in post or if the club opts for a fresh start with new management.
Whatever decisions are made, Sunderland faces a long and difficult road back to the Premier League, requiring significant investment and careful planning.
The supporters, who have endured years of underperformance and managerial upheaval, will be hoping that this moment of crisis finally prompts the structural changes the club desperately needs.
A proposed scheme would have scrapped upfront relief, worth an estimated £21bn to savers, but made pension pot withdrawals tax free.
An alternative option was to set a flat rate of tax relief, which may have been unpopular with higher earners.
Labour's shadow chancellor John McDonnell said Mr Osborne was "yet again ducking a big decision".
Campaigners said he had missed a "huge opportunity" to tackle pension inequality and help the lower paid.
But others said he was right to protect existing reliefs, and that radical reforms would have created new risks and imposed new administrative burdens on employers.
Mr Osborne had been expected to unveil changes in the Budget on 16 March, but a Treasury source said it was "not the right time" to make changes to pension tax relief.
The relief allows some of a person's earnings that would have been taken by government in tax to go into their pension instead.
Under the current system, pension savers receive tax relief at the same rate as their income tax - meaning basic rate taxpayers receive relief at 20% and higher rate taxpayers at 40 or 45%.

The proposal to introduce arrangements similar to an Isa, with no tax relief on contributions but with withdrawals free of tax, would have given a significant short-term boost to the government at the expense of lower tax revenue later.
An alternative option considered by the Treasury was for flat rate relief, which would have benefited basic rate taxpayers and cut reliefs for higher earners.
BBC business correspondent Joe Lynam So a policy which hadn't been announced will now not be announced.
And the big winners of this non-announcement will be wealthy people.
At the moment not only do they earn more, they also get a proportionately bigger tax top-up from the government when they save for their retirement.

If the chancellor had scrapped the tax relief entirely on pensions savings and created instead a new pensions Isa, that would have cost the better off (40p and 45p taxpayers) billions of pounds collectively.
The other proposal which had been reported was to create a new pension tax relief rate of 25p or even 33p.
That would have punished higher earners as well but not by as much as the Isa-style option.
The decision to shelve pension reform has been widely interpreted as a victory for the financial services industry and wealthy savers who benefit most from the current generous tax relief system.
Pension tax relief currently costs the government around £50bn annually, making it one of the most expensive tax breaks available.
Critics argue that this represents poor value for money, as much of the relief goes to those who would save for retirement anyway.
The Institute for Fiscal Studies had warned that without reform, pension tax relief would become increasingly unsustainable as the population ages and pension obligations grow.
Lower-income workers, who often struggle to save for retirement at all, receive minimal benefit from the current system due to their lower tax rates.
The government's reluctance to act suggests that the political risks of pension reform remain too high, particularly given the strength of opposition from business groups and affluent voters.
Pension specialists noted that the delay merely postpones an inevitable reckoning with the system's structural problems.
Meanwhile, employee pension contributions continue to rise across the public and private sectors, increasing the long-term cost to the Treasury.
Without intervention, pension tax relief could eventually consume an even larger share of government revenues, squeezing funds available for other priorities.
The decision also reflects broader political caution about making unpopular decisions during economically uncertain times.
Analysts suggest that any future government attempting pension reform will face similar fierce resistance from vested interests.
For now, the status quo remains intact, preserving advantages for higher earners while the debate about fairness in pension provision remains unresolved.
Last year the authorities learned of 946 victims, compared with 710 in 2010, the inter-departmental ministerial group on human trafficking said.
Trafficking gangs in China, Vietnam, Nigeria and eastern Europe now pose the biggest threat to the UK , it said.
The government said better co-ordination between its departments and with authorities abroad was key.
But anti-slavery groups warned government "failures" had led to "significant steps back" in the fight.
By Tom SymondsHome Affairs correspondent In Ilford, East London, the police moved in at 05:15 BST, smashing through the door of an end of terrace house, but without result.
It was empty.
The Met says it carries out two such raids every week, on average.
Two miles away in a second house, they found a Lithuanian family living in one room.
A stack of mail showed that a large number of people have stayed there before.
They questioned the Lithuanians who said they were being paid below minimum wage to work in a recycling depot and building firm.
The room costs Â£140 a week.
There was a CCTV camera watching the door of the house.
Are they victims of people trafficking?
It's not clear, and often those involved haven't asked themselves the same question.
But police say those who try to run are often subject to violence.
There is currently no official figure for the number of victims trafficked into the country each year.

# Continuation

Experts estimate the true figure could be many times higher than the 946 recorded cases, with some suggesting thousands are exploited annually across the UK.
The victims often come from impoverished backgrounds, promised legitimate work abroad only to find themselves trapped in debt bondage.
They may be forced to work in agriculture, construction, domestic service, or the sex industry, with their passports confiscated and movements strictly controlled.
The psychological impact of trafficking can be severe and long-lasting, leaving survivors traumatized and struggling to reintegrate into society.
Organizations working on the frontline report that victims frequently distrust authorities, fearing deportation or reprisals against their families back home.
Language barriers and cultural isolation further compound their vulnerability, making them reluctant to come forward and seek help.
The government has pledged to strengthen its National Referral Mechanism, which identifies potential victims and connects them with support services.
However, funding constraints have limited the capacity of specialist charities to provide adequate shelter and counseling.
Police forces across the country have begun specialized training to help officers recognize the signs of trafficking during routine investigations.
Despite these efforts, prosecutions of traffickers remain relatively low compared to the scale of the problem.
Campaigners argue that stronger penalties and more resources are needed to deter criminal networks.
The issue has also highlighted gaps in labor law enforcement, particularly in industries with high rates of undocumented workers.
International cooperation remains essential, as trafficking chains often span multiple countries and continents.
Without sustained commitment and investment, experts warn that the problem will only continue to grow.

Here's a look back at some of the show's most memorable moments: The birth of one of pop music's biggest beefs all started when Kanye West jumped on stage in the middle of Taylor Swift accepting her award for best female video.
When Swift's video for You Belong With Me beat Beyonce's Single Ladies, the rapper headed for the stage and took Taylor's microphone.
He said: "Taylor, I'm really happy for you, and I'ma let you finish... but BeyoncÃ© had one of the best videos of all time."
Kanye was booed by he audience, and when Beyonce herself accepted the award for video of the year later in the ceremony, she invited Swift back onstage to finish her speech.

Although Britney Spears was already a global superstar, this moment cemented her status as a pop culture icon as she performed her hit song I'm a Slave 4 U whilst draped with a seven-foot Burmese python.
Peta (People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals) and several other animal rights groups weren't too thrilled about this.
Recalling the performance last year, Britney said: "Oh my goodness, why did I do that?
That was so dumb," adding that she would "never" do it again.
Lady Gaga is no stranger to shocking the world with crazy outfits, and this one surely topped the list.
In 2010, the pop star accepted her video of the year award in a dress made from raw beef.
The dress was condemned by several animal rights groups but went on to be inducted in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011.

In an interview with American news anchor Anderson Cooper, Gaga explained that her dress was in protest against the US military's "don't ask, don't tell" policy, a rule that banned open homosexuality in the armed forces.
Beyonce announced her pregnancy with Blue Ivy in the most Beyonce way ever.

After delivering a killer performance of Love On Top, she unbuttoned her blazer, dropped her microphone on the floor (we're sure the sound engineers were big fans of that) and began rubbing her tummy.
The audience (and her fanbase) went suitably crazy.
Madonna isn't a stranger to delivering crazy performances - from hanging on a crucifix to rocking up in a ball gown as a French royal, we've seen it all.

# Continuation

One of her most talked-about moments came when she performed while suspended from the ceiling in a elaborate harness, defying gravity as she sang one of her biggest hits.
The performance left audiences speechless and sparked countless discussions about the artistry behind such daring stage productions.
Miley Cyrus shocked viewers when she performed at the awards show in a provocative outfit that sparked immediate controversy across social media.
The performance marked a significant shift in her public image and became a turning point in her career trajectory.
Rihanna has delivered some of the most visually stunning performances in the show's history, often incorporating elaborate choreography and costume changes.
Her performances are known for pushing creative boundaries while maintaining an undeniable sense of style and sophistication.
The Weeknd's dark, moody aesthetic brought a different energy to the stage, showcasing how diverse the performances at this event can truly be.
His elaborate stage design and atmospheric lighting created an immersive experience that captivated audiences worldwide.
Nicki Minaj has consistently delivered memorable moments, whether through her powerful vocal performances or her bold fashion statements on the red carpet.
Her presence at the show has become synonymous with unexpected twists and unforgettable entertainment.
These moments remind us why this awards show remains one of the most anticipated entertainment events of the year.
Viewers tune in not just for the awards themselves, but for the unpredictable nature of live performances and celebrity interactions.
The show continues to evolve, pushing artists to think creatively about how they present themselves to millions of viewers.
Whether through controversial performances, shocking announcements, or stunning visual displays, these moments have become part of pop culture history.
You try harder, you do better.
You feel the pressure and you respond to it.
You impose yourself upon rivals through physical dominance and force of personality.
In a sport like rugby union, where players have never been bigger, faster, or hit harder for longer, might is right like never before.
Which makes the continual influence of Dan Carter not just illogical but something close to miraculous.

On Saturday evening in Lyon, Carter - the leading points scorer in Test history, reigning World Rugby player of the year, World Cup winner for a second time seven months ago - will, in characteristically undemonstrative fashion, lead Racing 92's charge as they take on Saracens in the Champions Cup final.
Media playback is not supported on this device The All Blacks great is not only a physical throw-back in a game increasingly for the outsized and enormous.

At 5ft 10in and 14 stone, the 34-year-old plays an old-fashioned way: finding space where others seek contact, appearing unhurried when fly-halves have never had less time, still managing to play on instinct when set moves and patterns are everywhere you look.
It's not that Carter doesn't do the nasty stuff.
In Racing's semi-final win over Leicester Tigers he made 16 tackles.
It's how he does it.

Whereas Jonny Wilkinson, the original big-hitting number 10, stopped opposition runners like a rogue flanker and brought gasps from the stands and lungs of the victim alike, Carter tackles like he does everything else - with economy rather than anger, with perfect yet undemonstrative technique, with efficiency over ostentation.
Carter is not quite the player he was.
The outside break and sudden acceleration that defined so many of his early masterclasses have quietly been lost to the years.
It doesn't really matter.
In his swansong he appears to worry less about what he lacks than utilise what he does.

# Continuation

He has become something rarer in modern sport: a player whose influence grows as his physical capabilities diminish.
Carter's game has evolved into pure rugby intelligence, a masterclass in reading the play two phases ahead and positioning himself where the ball will be, not where it currently is.
His passing, always a weapon, has become almost telepathic in its precision—teammates seem to know where his distribution will find them before he releases it.
Against Saracens, a side built on suffocating defence and relentless pressure, this cerebral approach may prove decisive.

The English club excel at shutting down space and forcing errors through attrition, but they cannot easily defend against a player who manufactures opportunities through vision rather than athleticism.
Racing 92 have constructed their squad around Carter's particular genius, recruiting players who thrive in the spaces he creates rather than those who dominate through sheer physical presence.
At 34, with nothing left to prove and a World Cup already secured, Carter plays with the freedom of a man unburdened by expectation.
He has transcended the need to impose himself through force, instead imposing himself through thought.
In a game that increasingly rewards size and power, his persistence stands as a quiet rebuke to the notion that bigger is always better.

Perhaps that is the real miracle: not that Carter still plays at the highest level, but that he does so on his own terms, in his own way, seemingly immune to the relentless modernization happening all around him.
