After all the controversy surrounding South Africa's Caster Semenya, it is going to be great to see her put that all behind her in London.
She is definitely the woman to beat in the 800m." That athletics event remains the Games' ultimate showstopper and all eyes will be trained on Usain Bolt in the men's final on Sunday 5 August.
But athletics is not just about the track events; there are 47 medal events in all and just over half of these (24) are on the track.
The field events, such as the triple jump and javelin, are also popular and have provided Great Britain with Olympic medals in recent years.
For those unable to get tickets for the Olympic Stadium, there are five road races being held on the streets of central London and finishing at the Mall.
The women's marathon will see British world record holder Paula Radcliffe attempt to win gold at the third time of asking.
The athletics concludes with the relay races on 11 August, which should provide a fascinating finale.
The men's 4x100m has been dominated by the USA but Jamaica - led by that man Bolt - broke the world record to win gold in Beijing.
Athletics offer the widest range of choice of any Olympic sport as its various disciplines provide the opportunity to throw, run or jump.
Media playback is not supported on this device Immense core physical strength is required to throw a shot put that weighs 16 pounds for men and 8.8 pounds for women.
The test of endurance posed by the marathon sees athletes burn up to 3593 calories running a 26.2 mile course.

# Continuation

These statistics highlight just how diverse and demanding the athletic disciplines truly are.
British throwers have made significant strides in recent years, with several athletes now competing at world-class levels in the shot put and hammer throw events.
The jumpers too—both high and long—represent a tradition of excellence that stretches back decades in British Olympic history.
What makes this particular Games special is the combination of home advantage and genuine medal-winning potential across multiple events.
The energy from the crowd will undoubtedly play a role in pushing British athletes to their limits and beyond.
International competitors will face the added pressure of performing in an openly hostile environment, though the best athletes thrive under such circumstances.
The relay races, in particular, have always been crowd favorites because they encapsulate the team spirit and national pride that defines the Olympic Games.
With the men's 4x100m relay expected to be fiercely competitive, the USA, Jamaica, and Great Britain will all be eyeing that coveted gold medal.
The women's relay events have similarly become increasingly competitive, with several nations now capable of posting world-class times.
As the athletics program unfolds over these two weeks, every session will likely see packed stadiums and electrified atmospheres.
The diversity of events means that casual observers and dedicated track enthusiasts alike will find something to captivate their attention.
From the explosive power of the sprints to the grinding determination of distance running, athletics truly offers something for everyone.
This breadth of competition, combined with the quality of athletes on display, promises to make these Games memorable for years to come.
Francis used his "Urbi et Orbi" address to call for peace in Africa, Asia, the Middle East and across the globe.
He singled out "dear Syria", saying: "How much blood has been shed!
And how much suffering must there still be before a political solution is found?"
Easter is the most important festival in the Christian calendar and pilgrims have attended church across the world.

By David WilleyBBC News, Rome In past years there have been two parts to the Pope's Easter message - a heartfelt appeal for peace in the world's trouble spots, and multilingual greetings to the crowds thronging St Peter's Square.
There were 250,000 people from more than 100 countries present this morning.
But Pope Francis decided to cut the vernacular greetings.
He is, of course, most comfortable speaking his native Spanish, and he is also completely fluent in Italian as his family is from Piedmont in northern Italy.
Pope Francis was, however, almost incomprehensible when he tried out a few words in English to the crowds in St Peter's Square last week.
So he decided not to read out the "Happy Easter" greetings that had been prepared for him in 65 different languages including difficult-to-pronounce oriental tongues.
Popes John Paul II and Benedict used to struggle to pronounce even a short phrase in Burmese, Chinese or Korean, Pope Francis chose not even to try.
But his body language spoke volumes.
He was completely at ease saluting families, kissing babies and tenderly embracing a young disabled man, as the Pope was driven in an open jeep around the packed square.
Pope Francis also speaks when he remains silent.
Pope Francis, formerly Buenos Aires Archbishop Jorge Mario Bergoglio, was elected on 13 March, becoming the first non-European pope for almost 1,300 years.
He replaced Benedict XVI, who held the office for eight years and became the first pontiff in more than 700 years to resign, saying he no longer had the physical strength to continue.
The Argentine pontiff's decision to forgo the linguistic display reflected a pragmatic understanding of his limitations while simultaneously highlighting his strengths in direct human connection.
Rather than risk stumbling through unfamiliar phonetic patterns, Francis demonstrated that authenticity and warmth transcend language barriers.
The crowds seemed to appreciate this approach, responding enthusiastically to his genuine gestures and visible compassion.
His message of peace, delivered with conviction and clarity in his native tongue, resonated far more powerfully than any halting attempts at foreign pronunciation could have achieved.
This Easter address underscored a shift in papal communication style—one that prioritizes substance and sincerity over ceremonial completeness.
The young pope's willingness to acknowledge his limitations paradoxically reinforced his authority and credibility with the assembled faithful.
By focusing on what he does best—connecting with people on a human level—Francis has begun to establish his own distinct identity within the papacy.

His predecessors' struggles with linguistic performances have become something of a historical footnote, but Francis has transformed potential embarrassment into an opportunity for authentic engagement.
The Vatican observers noted that this streamlined approach may well become the template for future papal Easter messages.
For the pilgrims who traveled from distant lands to witness the celebration, the Pope's embrace and benediction proved far more meaningful than words in their native languages could ever be.
Francis's papacy, still in its earliest weeks, already suggests a leader unafraid to break tradition when doing so serves the deeper mission of his office.
The choice to remain silent in 65 languages ultimately allowed him to speak most eloquently through presence and pastoral care.
With the game pointless, England hooker Hartley lashed out at Tigers' Matt Smith with just 17 minutes gone.
Tigers registered a penalty try, but after the break Alex Waller and Ben Foden went over for the champions and Niki Goneva scored two of his own.
But with three minutes left Jamie Elliott touched down to win an epic derby at Franklin's Gardens.

It was all in spite of Saints captain Hartley's indiscretion, the latest on a growing rap sheet throughout his career which will raise questions about his credentials to represent England, regardless of whether his inevitable ban stretches into next year's Six Nations.

With two separate brawls having broken out on the pitch, replays showed that Hartley's elbow connected with Smith's chin during a scuffle, and referee JP Doyle took the advice of the television match official to send off the 28-year-old.

After missing last year's British & Irish Lions tour of Australia with an 11-week ban after being dismissed for swearing at referee Wayne Barnes in the 2013 Premiership final - also against the Tigers - Hartley, by his own admission, was in the last chance saloon when it came to his England career.

The New Zealand-born forward, who rejected a big-money move to Montpellier to sign a new Saints deal this week, can also count a 26-week ban in 2007 for eye gouging and eight-week and two-week bans in 2012, for biting and striking respectively, among his misdemeanours.
With just 17 minutes having passed when he trudged off the field, Saints, like in the 2013 final, were once again without their captain and facing an uphill battle.

Immediately after Hartley's sending off, which inevitably overshadowed a typically ferocious East Midlands derby, things got worse for Saints who, still trying to regroup, conceded a penalty try for impeding three consecutive scrums.
Owen Williams slotted the conversion and Stephen Myler put over a penalty for Saints, before Tigers' Tom Croft was sin-binned moments before the break for a high tackle.
Myler collected the points off the tee and after the interval Saints capitalised on Croft's absence, pinning Tigers back into their 22 and freeing up Salesi Ma'afu to pass on to Waller for the score.

Tom Youngs, who may be eyeing up Hartley's place in Stuart Lancaster's England side, lasted less than 50 minutes as he continued his return from injury, but Tigers were able to reassert themselves when they were back up to their full quota.

Replacement Freddie Burns's break down the middle had Saints scrambling under their posts and Leicester quickly recycled as Goneva crossed after taking a pass from Williams, who skewed his conversion wide.

Fourteen-man Saints once again validated their status as champions and Premiership leaders, though, when Luther Burrell, who was a powerhouse all afternoon, burst forward, allowing the ball to go through the hands to Foden for a superb finish from 35 metres.
It looked like it might be to no avail as Tigers, unbeaten in four Premiership games coming into the match, turned it over in midfield through Julian Salvi and put Goneva over in the corner.
But, remarkably, Saints found time for one more push and, from a line-out, swept the ball across the pitch for Elliott to clinch an unlikely and thrilling victory.

# Continuation

The result epitomized Saints' resilience and their championship credentials, proving that even depleted they possessed the quality and determination to overcome adversity.
For Leicester, it represented a missed opportunity to close the gap on the leaders and a frustrating reminder that consistency remains their Achilles heel this season.
Hartley's absence will undoubtedly be keenly felt in the coming weeks, with his leadership and experience difficult to replace in the front row.

The controversy surrounding the sending off will dominate post-match analysis, with some questioning whether the punishment fitted the offense while others argue it was a justified response to Hartley's pattern of behavior.

Stuart Lancaster faces a significant decision regarding Hartley's international future, balancing the player's undeniable talents against the recurring disciplinary issues that threaten to undermine his judgment.
The England coach has shown patience with Hartley before, but another suspension may finally force his hand on whether the hooker can be trusted at the highest level.
For Saints, the victory provides genuine momentum heading into the crucial weeks ahead, demonstrating they can win matches even when circumstances conspire against them.
Tom Youngs's limited appearance suggests Leicester's injury concerns remain a pressing issue as they seek to mount a sustained title challenge.
The East Midlands derby has once again delivered drama and intensity befitting its status as one of English rugby's fiercest rivalries.
As the dust settles on Franklin's Gardens, attention will inevitably turn to the disciplinary hearing that awaits Hartley and the length of ban he might receive.
Whether Saints can maintain their winning form with their captain sidelined will be a crucial test of their depth and character.
The incident serves as another cautionary tale about the fine line between passion and professionalism in elite sport.
Johnson confirmed his departure and the Scottish Rugby Union announced that he will be available for the June tour to Australia, Fiji and Samoa. "
I will not be looking to renew [my contract] at the end of the season," said the 49-year-old Australian.
Scotland coach Andy Robinson has been seeking an experienced coach.

Johnson added: "When I joined the region I had a remit to help set-up systems that would enable us to bring through home grown talent, coaches and players, a challenge I've thoroughly enjoyed getting my teeth into over the last three years. "
I feel that now is the right time for me to move on to other challenges but I remain 100% committed to the Ospreys between now and the end of the season."

The real legacy he [Johnson] will leave is the systems and structures he has helped to develop that will allow us to continue leading the way in Wales Johnson's role with Scotland is thought to also include some involvement with Glasgow and Edinburgh.

Ospreys chief operations officer Andrew Hore said: "It is a huge disappointment for us to be losing Scott at the end of the season, but we understand that he feels it is the right time for him to move on to other challenges having served the three years he committed himself to us for originally."
Hore added: "By informing us of his intentions at this early stage, it allows us to use this time to consider our next course of action and the options available to us as we plan for the future."

Managing director Roger Blyth said: "While he has brought silverware to the Liberty Stadium and we hope that will be the case once again this season, the real legacy he will leave is the systems and structures he has helped to develop that will allow us to continue leading the way in Wales."
Johnson joined the Ospreys ahead of the 2009-10 season, leaving the role of USA Eagles national coach to do so.
He first arrived in Wales as a skills coach during 2011 World Cup winning coach Graham Henry's reign and was assistant coach during the Steve Hansen and Mike Ruddock eras.
Wales won their first Grand Slam in 27 years under Ruddock in 2005 and many credited Johnson with playing a key role in that achievement.
Johnson became Wales caretaker coach during the 2006 Six Nations following Ruddock's controversial departure.
In March 2006 Johnson became one of John Connolly's assistant coaches and selectors ahead of the Wallabies' 2007 World Cup campaign.
In 2008 he became USA coach and left the following year to return to Wales with the Ospreys.

# Continuation

Johnson's departure marks the end of an era for the Ospreys, who have benefited enormously from his coaching expertise and strategic vision.

His track record speaks for itself, having worked alongside some of the most respected figures in international rugby, including his time developing talent systems that have proven invaluable to Welsh rugby.
The timing of his announcement allows the Ospreys adequate opportunity to recruit a suitable replacement and ensure continuity in their coaching structure.
Scotland's gain will undoubtedly be Wales's loss, as Johnson brings decades of experience at the highest levels of the sport to his new role.
His involvement with the Scottish national team, alongside potential input with their regional franchises, suggests a comprehensive restructuring of their coaching framework.
The Ospreys have already begun preliminary discussions about potential candidates to fill the void left by Johnson's departure.
Several high-profile coaches from both hemispheres have reportedly expressed interest in the position.
However, finding someone with Johnson's combination of international experience and ability to develop emerging talent will prove challenging.
His final season with the region is expected to be highly competitive, with the Ospreys aiming to add further silverware to their trophy cabinet.
Players and staff at the Liberty Stadium have expressed their appreciation for Johnson's professionalism and dedication throughout his tenure.
Looking ahead, Johnson's influence on Scottish rugby could reshape how the national team approaches player development and tactical preparation.
His Australian perspective on the game may introduce fresh methodologies that could revitalize the struggling Scottish program.
The rugby community will be watching closely to see how effectively Johnson can implement his systems in his new Scottish role.
The Dutchman was teed up on the edge of the area by the excellent Dusan Tadic and connected with the outside of his boot to drive his shot into the top corner as Saints took the lead on 25 minutes.
The Baggies pressed for an equaliser but failed to score for the fifth time in their last six games.
They were continually thwarted by England goalkeeper Fraser Forster who made three stunning saves.
The first saw him claw away a Salomon Rondon header in the first half and he then denied Craig Dawson and Jonny Evans deep into injury time as Saints held on for the win.
Tony Pulis' side have now lost four of their last six and are stuttering in their attempts to achieve their best league finish since 1981, when they came fourth.
The win leaves ninth-placed Southampton just four points behind the Baggies, who stay eighth.
Scotland winger Matt Phillips made his first start since February, but he could not inspire West Brom.
Last season they failed to win any of their last nine league games, with five defeats and four draws.
They are three games into their final nine matches of this campaign and their record reads two defeats and one draw.
While goals are clearly a problem at the moment, the Albion defence is also struggling with just three clean sheets in their last 20 league games.
Albion were not short on chances with 17 shots in the game, six of which were on target.
They had 18 attempts in the 2-0 midweek defeat by Watford and Pulis is disappointed his side are not taking their chances. "
We have had 35 shots over the last two games, we need to have more composure and steadiness in front of goal," Pulis told BBC Sport.
Still, barring a dramatic collapse, they should be able to achieve the Chinese owners' target of a top-10 finish this season.
Performances like today will put Forster back in contention to challenge Joe Hart for the England jersey.
The 29-year-old made six saves today and manager Claude Puel paid tribute.
"Fraser was outstanding," Puel said in his post-match interview, beaming with pride at his goalkeeper's display. "
These are the moments that define a season, and he showed exactly why he deserves to be considered among the best in the Premier League."
Southampton's victory was built on a foundation of defensive solidity and clinical finishing when opportunities arose, a stark contrast to West Brom's profligacy in front of goal.
The Saints had managed just 11 shots compared to their opponents' 17, yet they made theirs count when it mattered most.
Tadic's creative brilliance throughout the match had been a constant thorn in the Baggies' side, and his assist for the Dutch forward's goal exemplified his influence on the game.
For Southampton, this result provided a much-needed boost after a period of inconsistent form that had left them languishing in mid-table obscurity.
With nine games remaining, Puel's men now had genuine aspirations of pushing into the European qualification places if they could maintain this level of performance.
West Brom's frustration was evident as the final whistle approached, with several players visibly dejected at another opportunity gone begging.
The pressure would now intensify on Pulis to find a solution to his team's goalscoring drought before the window for a top-four finish closed permanently.
For Fraser Forster, this display had come at precisely the right moment in the season, with Hart's Manchester City form faltering and the national team's goalkeeper situation wide open.
His commanding presence and shot-stopping ability had been the difference between a draw and defeat today.

As both teams headed into the final stretch of the season, the contrasting trajectories were becoming increasingly apparent, with Southampton building momentum and West Brom searching desperately for answers.
Mrs May said the convention, which is separate from the EU, "can bind the hands of Parliament".
David Cameron has previously refused to rule out leaving the convention.
Ex-cabinet minister and Leave campaigner Iain Duncan Smith said her "remarkable intervention" had "utterly undermined" the government's position.

In her first major speech of the referendum campaign, Mrs May said the UK had to "stand tall and lead in Europe" rather than leaving the EU, and that membership made the UK "more secure from crime and terrorism".
She also raised concerns about more countries joining the EU.
The ECHR, which is separate from the EU institutions, was incorporated into UK law by the Human Rights Act.
The Conservatives have pledged to replace the Human Rights Act with a "British Bill of Rights" but have not proposed leaving the convention altogether.
BBC political editor The home secretary is one of the most senior politicians in the country.
Of course, her backing of the Remain campaign is important.
And it's certainly better for the prime minister and the Remain campaign to have her In, rather than Out.
But her qualified support suggests there are three, rather than two, positions at the top of the Conservative party - 'Remain', 'Leave', and perhaps now, 'Reluctant Remain'.
It was the Out campaign struggling to contain their internal differences.
Tonight, it feels like cracks might appear on the other side too.
Read more from Laura A Downing Street spokeswoman urged journalists not to "overdo the differences" between the prime minister and the home secretary on withdrawal from the ECHR.
She said Mr Cameron "rules nothing out" as he seeks reform of the convention, saying the home secretary had been "setting out a clear distinction between the ECHR and the EU".
Regardless of the outcome of the 23 June EU referendum, Mrs May said she still believed the UK should leave the ECHR which she said "adds nothing to our prosperity".

# Continuation

The home secretary's remarks have intensified the debate within Westminster over Britain's relationship with European institutions, creating an unusual dynamic within the government's official campaign message.
While Mrs May has been a consistent voice calling for withdrawal from the convention, her timing in raising the issue during the EU referendum campaign has drawn criticism from some quarters.
Senior figures within the Remain campaign have expressed concern that such discussions could distract from the central message about EU membership.
The distinction between the ECHR and the EU itself, which Mrs May sought to emphasize, is one that many voters find difficult to grasp.
This confusion has been exploited by Leave campaigners, who argue that EU membership entangles Britain in a web of European legal obligations.
Meanwhile, legal experts have warned that leaving the convention could have serious implications for the UK's international standing and its relationships with allied nations.
The government's internal divisions on this matter reflect deeper ideological splits within the Conservative Party that have been simmering for years.
Some MPs argue that the Human Rights Act itself provides sufficient protections without the need for ECHR membership, while others contend that the convention offers essential safeguards.
The debate also touches on questions of parliamentary sovereignty, a theme that resonates strongly with Leave voters.
As the referendum campaign intensifies, both sides will likely continue to invoke these broader constitutional questions to support their respective positions.

Mrs May's intervention suggests that the government's post-referendum agenda may include significant changes to Britain's relationship with European legal frameworks, regardless of the vote's outcome.
The complexity of these overlapping institutions and conventions means that many voters remain uncertain about what exactly is at stake in these discussions.
Media playback is not supported on this device On Thursday afternoon, Jackson Page was meant to be taking part in a physical education lesson at Ebbw Fawr Learning Community in Blaenau Gwent.

Instead, the 15-year-old from Ebbw Vale found himself in front of the television cameras at Cardiff's Motorpoint Arena, playing former world number one Judd Trump for a place in the last 16 of the Welsh Open.

Having beaten Jason Weston and John Astley in the first and second rounds, Page attracted a big crowd; the locals curious to see if this improbably bearded schoolboy could continue his improbable run.
However, his match against Trump - the winner of six ranking titles - proved a bridge too far for the Welsh teenager.
Page was brave in his shot selection and eager to match his opponent's attacking instincts, but was ultimately outclassed as Trump ruthlessly raced to a 4-0 win. "
It was great out there but there were a lot of nerves and pressure too," says Page, remarkably calm and level-headed for a 15-year-old. "
It's been great - I've loved every minute of it.
My friends have been proud of me and enjoyed watching me.
Now it's back to blazers and ties. "
No-one likes school really.
When I'm there I just can't wait to get into the snooker hall."
Luckily for Page, his teachers recognise his potential and are happy for him to forego as many lessons as is necessary for him to pursue his snooker ambitions.
After a week in school, Page will travel to Cyprus for the European Championships, where winning the under-21 or senior competition would give him the opportunity to turn professional.
Media playback is not supported on this device With Page in Cyprus will be his mother Clare, who along with father Andrew, has been integral to his snooker development.

Being the parents of a budding player has meant driving him from school to the local snooker hall every day, then for a whole day of practice on Saturdays and usually a tournament on Sundays - of which Page enters about 33 a year. "
We're so proud, we just can't believe it," says Clare. "

# Continuation

"He's worked so hard to get where he is, and to see him playing against someone of Judd Trump's calibre at just 15 is incredible.
We knew he had talent from a very young age, but this has exceeded all our expectations."
The commitment required from the entire family has been substantial, with weekends often consumed by travel to tournaments across Wales and beyond.
Andrew adds that watching Jackson develop not just as a player but as a person has been the most rewarding aspect of their support.
The snooker hall has become a second home, a place where Jackson's natural gifts have been honed through thousands of hours of practice and dedication.
His coach has noted that the teenager possesses a maturity and focus unusual for his age, traits that will serve him well as he progresses through the ranks.
The European Championships in Cyprus represent a crucial milestone in his career, a chance to compete against the best young talent on the continent.
A strong performance there could open doors to professional opportunities that many players spend years chasing.
Back at Ebbw Fawr Learning Community, staff members have become accustomed to Jackson's absences for snooker commitments, viewing them as investments in his future.
His teachers report that despite the disruptions to his schooling, he maintains respectable grades and a positive attitude.
The balance between education and sport is one that Jackson seems to manage with remarkable poise.
His peers at school have embraced his newfound fame, with many attending his matches at the Motorpoint Arena.
Looking ahead, Page remains grounded about his prospects, understanding that the road to professional snooker is long and competitive.
Yet with the support of his family, the backing of his school, and most importantly his own burning desire to succeed, few would bet against this determined young Welsh player achieving his dreams.
The lovelorn ballad only reached number 108, but it set the "dirt poor" Tennessee singer on the path to fame.
Since then, she's sold more than 100 million albums, topped the country chart 25 times, and won eight Grammy Awards - but she shows no signs of slowing down. "
Almost every day I come up with a few song titles or a sweet melody," says the 70-year-old, who claims to have written more than 3,000 songs in her career.
Her latest album, Pure & Simple, came out on Friday, featuring new songs with stripped-down arrangements that recall the early days of her career.
It has an overwhelmingly romantic theme, inspired by renewing her wedding vows earlier this year.
And just like her chart career, her marriage to Carl Dean turned 50 this year. "
I thought, well, this would be a good year to write an album of love songs," she says. "
I've never done that before.
I've usually had a story song or a gospel song, but this was all love stories."
The BBC caught up with the star to discuss the new record, her legendary Glastonbury set, and why she admires Adele.
First of all, congratulations on celebrating your golden wedding anniversary!
Thank you so much.
That's a long time to do anything, ain't it?
Especially to be married.
What's the secret?
We've always been good buddies.

# Continuation

We have fun together, and we respect each other's space.
I think that's really important in a marriage - you can't be in each other's pocket all the time.
Carl's always been supportive of my career, even when I was traveling constantly and he preferred to stay home.
He never tried to change me or hold me back, and I've always appreciated that about him.
When you find someone who accepts you for who you are, you hold onto that.
Over the years, we've had our ups and downs like any couple, but we've always worked through them together.
I think the key is communication and making sure you're still dating each other, not just existing as a married couple.
That's actually what inspired this whole album - remembering why we fell in love in the first place.
Writing these love songs took me back to those early days when everything felt new and magical.
Some of them are quite personal, things I've never shared publicly before.
But at this stage of my life, I felt like it was the right time to be vulnerable and honest.
I hope people connect with these songs the way I did while writing them.
Music has always been my way of expressing what's in my heart, and this album is no exception.

David Leyonhjelm, a cat-loving former veterinarian and Liberal Democratic senator for New South Wales, has twice been elected to federal parliament, and is one of a disparate group of cross-benchers who hold the balance of power in an unpredictable upper house.
He is his libertarian party's sole representative in Canberra, and has been branded "the nastiest, most sexist politician in Australia" by Sydney Morning Herald columnist Peter FitzSimons.

FitzSimons, a former rugby union international, told his readers a story about an elderly female reader who had taken exception to the senator's relaxed view on Donald Trump's crass remarks about women.
She complained and received a brusque response saying she was "not fit to use a computer". "
Apparently a constituent wrote to me and made some fairly silly remarks.
I have a fairly low tolerance for idiots," Senator Leyonhjelm told the BBC from his offices in Sydney. "
It was fairly idiotic email, so I wrote back to her and called her a bimbo.
I had no idea [but] it turns out she is in her 70s or 80s or something like that and she complained to this sports writer." "

He contacted my office and asked for a comment and one of my staff replied we had no comment for him about the particular matter butâ€¦ said 'well, usually he [the Senator] tells people like that to [profanity]-off, and he didn't in this case, so we're worried that he is mellowing. "
Apparently that enraged this guy."
It sure did.
A fired-up FitzSimons added that "with such personal abuse, the Senator disgraces the office he holds".
This is not the first time the Liberal Democratic MP has been castigated in the press.
In August, the Herald labelled him a "hate-speech apologist" and "a boorish, supercilious know-all with the empathy of a [concrete] besser block".

At issue is the senator's push to repeal section 18C of Australia's Racial Discrimination Act, which outlaws behaviour that is likely to "offend, insult, humiliate or intimidate" someone because of their race or ethnicity.
Here is where he channels memories of "Chopper" Read, who died in 2013 after serving 23 years in jail in Australia for kidnapping and malicious wounding. "

# Continuation

Leyonhjelm argues passionately that the law is too restrictive and represents an unacceptable curtailment of free speech in a democratic society.
He contends that section 18C casts too wide a net, capturing speech that may be offensive but hardly constitutes genuine discrimination or harm.
His campaign to reform the legislation has attracted support from some free speech advocates who share his concerns about legal overreach, though it has simultaneously galvanized his critics.

The senator's libertarian philosophy places individual liberty at the forefront of his political worldview, sometimes at odds with communitarian values that prioritize social cohesion and protection of vulnerable groups.
His detractors argue that his absolutist stance on free expression fails to account for the real psychological and social damage that hateful speech can inflict on marginalized communities.
Yet Leyonhjelm remains undeterred, using his position in the Senate to push for legislative change and to challenge what he sees as politically correct orthodoxy.
His willingness to court controversy and his combative responses to criticism have made him a polarizing figure in Australian politics.
Some view him as a principled defender of unpopular liberties, while others see him as a provocateur more interested in generating headlines than in substantive policy debate.
Regardless of one's assessment, his presence in the Senate ensures that debates about free speech, discrimination, and the proper limits of government regulation remain contentious and unresolved.
As a cross-bencher, he wields disproportionate influence over legislation, a position he appears determined to exploit in pursuit of his ideological agenda.
Students earlier this summer took a total of 35,537 exams.
But what do we know about how well Wales is doing?
How many students are going to university?
And what happens next for those still with big decisions to make?
MAKING THE GRADE A new top grade, A*, was introduced in 2010.
A total of 6.6% achieved this in 2016, a fall on last year and still short of the UK average of 8.1%.
The proportion of students getting A* and A also fell again from 23.1% to 22.7% and Wales continues to trail the rest of the UK and all regions of England apart from the North East of England.

When you look at individual subjects, more students in Wales have been getting top grades in maths over the last five years than in England - but in English language, Wales has been trailing, with the gap still 3.2% in the latest set of results.
In French, 31% got A* to A in Wales - this is an improvement, but 37.8% managed that in England.
The gap was as wide as 14% in 2012.
Numbers of students taking French have also gone down by 11% this year.
Wales and England are a little further apart now at the top grades for A-level physics, with Wales dropping back to 25.8% behind England on 29.6%.
It is worth looking too at how many students are only getting the lower grades.
A slightly higher proportion in Wales - 23.5% - got D and E grades, compared to 20.6% in England.
That gap has also widened slightly too.
Wales has more students getting these lower grades in English (23.2%) than who managed A* to A (15.9%) in the same subject.

# Continuation

The disparity in performance across different regions raises important questions about educational resources and support systems.
Teachers and education officials in Wales have pointed to various factors that might explain these gaps, including differences in curriculum approaches and assessment methods between nations.
Despite these challenges, there are some encouraging signs in the data.
The number of students achieving passes in core subjects has remained relatively stable, suggesting that while top-tier performance may lag, foundational education standards are being maintained.
Looking ahead, universities across the UK will be receiving applications from Welsh students with these results in hand.
Many institutions have begun adjusting their entry requirements in response to grade inflation concerns, making the context of where grades were achieved increasingly important.
For students still deciding their next steps, the results represent both opportunity and uncertainty.

Those with strong A* and A grades will have their pick of competitive university programs, while others may need to consider alternative pathways such as vocational training or further study at college level.
Employers are also paying closer attention to practical skills and experience alongside traditional qualifications.
The Welsh government has pledged to review educational standards and provide additional support to schools struggling with performance metrics.
Summer results day marks the beginning of a crucial period for students, with university clearing and other enrollment processes now underway.
Parents and students alike are advised to seek guidance from school advisors about the best options available given individual circumstances and aspirations.
Cesc Fabregas marked his 300th Premier League appearance by firing the Blues ahead, poking the ball through the legs of Jack Cork and into the net.
The hosts were stunned when Swansea equalised from their first serious attempt on target on the stroke of half-time - Fernando Llorente heading in Gylfi Sigurdsson's free-kick.
Fabregas hit the bar before Pedro's curling effort restored the lead and Diego Costa netted the third from close range.
Swansea were denied a penalty when Cesar Azpilicueta handled inside the area at 1-1.
This was far from straightforward for Antonio Conte's side and had referee Neil Swarbrick awarded Swansea a penalty shortly before Pedro made it 2-1 then the outcome might have been different.
However, in the end Chelsea's sweeping forward play earned them a 10th straight home Premier League win as they took another significant step towards a second title in three seasons.
On a weekend when the first major silverware of the season - the EFL Cup - is handed out at Wembley, the Blues look unstoppable.
They have 63 points from 26 games - three more than at the same stage in 2014-15 when last crowned champions of England.
Media playback is not supported on this device Fabregas could have ended the game with four goals on his return to the side.
The Spain midfielder had a goal-bound shot deflected behind shortly before he opened the scoring, was denied by former Arsenal team-mate Lukasz Fabianski and also rattled the bar.
With former Blues midfielder Frank Lampard watching on, Chelsea turned on the style.
While it required an error from Fabianski to restore the lead, Eden Hazard's exquisite timing and pass for Costa to make it 3-1 was a delight.

Chelsea were forced to work hard for three points thanks to a well organised and energetic Swansea side and the Swans looked a shadow of the team that was bottom of the Premier League table five weeks ago.
Their four-point safety cushion at the start of the day is down to three, but boss Paul Clement will have been pleased with the way his side frustrated the runaway leaders for long spells.
