After all, virtually everything else that sparkles, glimmers, glints or shines at Christmas comes from vast Chinese factories that work year round turning out forests of fake trees, seas of shimmering baubles, galaxies of Christmas lights and armies of ho-ho-ho-ing Santas.
The answer, says the company's chief executive Ian Newton, lies in the product itself. "
The fact is," he says, "tinsel doesn't travel well."
If you pack tinsel into a container and ship it halfway around the world, it comes out limp and flaccid, he explains.
What customers want, he says as he lifts up a thick golden rope of the stuff, is pert, bushy tinsel.
So even though labour costs are higher in south Wales than they are in the industrial parks of Southern China, it is still worth making tinsel in the UK. "
The logistics of shipping simply don't work in the company's favour when it comes to international sourcing.
Tinsel's delicate nature means that moisture, temperature fluctuations, and the sheer length of the journey all conspire to ruin the product before it ever reaches British shores.
This vulnerability has inadvertently become Festive Productions' greatest asset, transforming what could have been a weakness into a competitive advantage.
The company's location in Wales also benefits from proximity to UK customers, allowing for rapid distribution and fresher stock arriving in shops just when consumers need it most.

Ian Newton is quick to point out that this isn't merely about maintaining tradition for tradition's sake; it's about delivering a superior product that customers can actually see and feel before they buy.

The company has invested heavily in modern machinery and production techniques, ensuring that their tinsel meets contemporary quality standards while retaining the sparkle and fullness that shoppers expect.
Workers at the facility take pride in their craft, understanding that they're producing something that brings joy to millions of homes during the festive season.
The factory operates with remarkable efficiency, having refined its processes over decades to maximize output without compromising quality.
Local employment is another factor that keeps production rooted in south Wales, with the company providing stable jobs in a region where such opportunities have become increasingly scarce.
As Christmas approaches each year, Festive Productions ramps up production to meet the seasonal surge in demand, transforming the facility into a glittering hive of activity.
The company has also begun exploring sustainable alternatives and eco-friendly tinsel options, recognizing that modern consumers care about environmental impact.

Yet despite all these innovations and efficiencies, there remains something almost magical about the fact that Britain's tinsel still comes from a small corner of Wales, defying the global trend toward outsourcing.
Somewhere in the district a microphone has picked up the percussive sound of a bullet and sent a signal, via California, to the station, which is where Kim Smith hears about it.

Ms Smith, a data analyst from the University of Chicago, works at one of the city's new Strategic Decision Support Centres, where data, technology, and old-fashioned police work are being combined in an effort to control a sudden surge in gun violence.
Seconds after a ping, a large flatscreen monitor displays a Google map of the gunshot location.
Another connects to surveillance cameras activated by the shot, sometimes fast enough to see a gunman fleeing, and usually two or three minutes before the first 911 call comes in.
Sometimes someone happens to open fire while a live feed is rolling in the room. "
I've seen a lot of shootings actually happen on screen in front of me," said Ms Smith, who was new to the world of law enforcement when she joined the project. "
The first time I was really shocked.

You hear stories about people going out in the middle of the day in broad daylight, just walking the dog, and someone starts firing off rounds, but then to actually see itâ€¦" The strategic centres were established in February after more than 4,000 shootings and 762 homicides in 2016 - a massive 59% increase on the previous year and more murders than New York and LA combined.
President Trump threatened in January to "send in the Feds" if the city didn't fix "the horrible carnage".

Taking blueprints from similar operations in LA and New York, Chicago PD set up two centres in the city's two most violent districts - Englewood and Harrison, which account for 5% of the city's population but nearly a third of all shootings last year.
Eventually there will be six across the city, with initial set-up costs of about a million dollars each.

Chicago PD borrowed civilian data analysts - including Ms Smith - from the University of Chicago in an attempt to make better use of existing technologies like the Shotspotter microphones and more sense of the crime data routinely collected by the department.
The new cutting edge of anti-gun policing in Chicago had a modest start.
The Englewood district centre set up shop in a disused line-up room, the partition wall and one-way glass knocked through to make more room.
The first strategic meeting of the Harrison district centre was lit by a single lamp in a bare office.
Now there are large flatscreen monitors fixed to the walls displaying live maps and charts, while analysts track data on two or three screens in front of them.
The transformation has been remarkable, though challenges remain.

The system's real power lies not just in speed, but in prediction—algorithms now attempt to forecast where violence might erupt next based on historical patterns, recent incidents, and social network analysis.
When the data suggests a particular block or intersection is at elevated risk, officers can be positioned preemptively, sometimes preventing shootings before they happen.

Yet Ms Smith and her colleagues grapple daily with the ethical implications of their work, knowing that their algorithms could inadvertently target certain neighborhoods or communities more heavily than others.
The technology is only as good as the data feeding it, and decades of policing patterns mean that data carries historical biases baked into every query.
Still, the preliminary results have been encouraging enough that other cities are watching closely, sending delegations to observe the centres in action.
Some analysts argue that the combination of technology and human intuition—the "old-fashioned police work" alongside the data—creates a balance that pure algorithmic approaches cannot achieve.

Officers who have worked the same neighborhoods for years bring contextual knowledge that no algorithm can replicate: they know which feuds are simmering, which corners are flashpoints, which individuals are at risk.
The centres have also become a training ground for a new type of police officer, one comfortable with data visualization and statistical analysis.
For Ms Smith, the shock of witnessing that first shooting on screen has faded into something more complex—a sense of purpose mixed with the weight of responsibility.
She knows that her analysis might send an officer into a dangerous situation, or conversely, that a missed pattern in the data could mean someone dies unnecessarily.

The work is relentless, the stakes are high, and the city watches to see if Chicago's experiment in data-driven policing can finally break the cycle of violence that has plagued its streets for so long.
The two human cases are linked to nine cases of Mycobacterium bovis infection in cats in Berkshire and Hampshire last year.
Both people were responding to treatment, PHE said.
It said the risk of cat-to-human transmission of M. bovis remained "very low".
M. bovis is the bacterium that causes tuberculosis in cattle, known as bovine TB, and other species.

Transmission of M. bovis from infected animals to humans can occur by breathing in or ingesting bacteria shed by the animal or through contamination of unprotected cuts in the skin while handling infected animals or their carcasses.
The nine cases of M. bovis infection in cats in Berkshire and Hampshire were investigated by PHE and the Animal Health and Veterinary Laboratories Agency (AHVLA) during 2013.
The findings of the investigation are published in the Veterinary Record on Thursday.
Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious disease caused by a germ which usually affects the lungs.

Symptoms can take several months to appear and include •Fever and night sweats •Persistent cough •Losing weight •Blood in your phlegm or spit Almost all forms of TB are treatable and curable, but delays in detection and treatment can be damaging.
TB caused by M. bovis is diagnosed in less than 40 people in the UK each year.
The majority of these cases are in people over 65 years old.
Overall, human TB caused by M. bovis accounts for less than 1% of the 9,000 TB cases diagnosed in the UK every year.
Those working closely with livestock and/or regularly drinking unpasteurised (raw) milk have a greater risk of exposure.
Public Health England Screening was offered to people who had had contact with the infected cats.
Following further tests, a total of two cases of active TB were identified.
Molecular analysis showed that M. bovis taken from the infected cats matched the strain of TB found in the human cases, indicating that the bacterium was transmitted from an infected cat.

# Continuation

The investigation revealed that the infected cats had likely contracted M. bovis from exposure to infected cattle or contaminated environments, highlighting the importance of understanding transmission pathways across species.
Veterinarians and animal health officials emphasized that while cat-to-human transmission is rare, it underscores the need for proper hygiene practices when handling sick animals.
People who work with cats in veterinary settings or who keep infected animals at home should be particularly vigilant about hand washing and wound protection.
The cases also prompted discussions about the broader implications of bovine TB control in wildlife and domestic animal populations across the UK.
Experts noted that improving cattle testing and control measures could indirectly reduce the risk of M. bovis spreading to other species, including cats.
The investigation's findings were significant because they provided concrete evidence of feline-to-human transmission, even though such cases remain exceptionally uncommon.
Public health authorities used the incident to reinforce guidance on recognizing TB symptoms and seeking prompt medical attention if exposure to infected animals is suspected.
The two affected individuals were monitored closely throughout their treatment course, with regular follow-up appointments to ensure complete recovery.

Veterinary professionals were advised to consider M. bovis in their differential diagnosis when examining cats with respiratory or systemic symptoms, particularly in areas with known bovine TB presence.
The case also highlighted the value of collaboration between human and animal health sectors in investigating zoonotic disease outbreaks.
Moving forward, enhanced surveillance protocols were implemented to detect similar cases earlier and prevent further transmission.
The findings contributed to the growing body of evidence supporting integrated approaches to controlling tuberculosis across both human and animal populations in the United Kingdom.
Nomads, who finished as league runners-up in 2016-17, will host HJK Helsinki of Finland in the first qualifying round first leg at Bangor City's Nantporth.
Welsh Cup winners Bala will face FC Vaduz of Lichtenstein in their first leg ties at Rhyl's Belle Vue.
Bangor secured their place in Europe via the play-offs and they will be away to Danish side Lyngby BK.
Media playback is not supported on this device Bala Town will be appearing in the Europa League for the fourth time and hoping to reach the second qualifying round for the first time.
Manager Colin Caton says his side have learnt from previous campaigns, which included narrow aggregate defeats to Estonian side Levadia Tallin and Differdange of Luxembourg. "
Going into the first couple of seasons was going into the unknown really but we've got no excuses this time," Caton said. "
It's going to be a real tough test for us but we're going to give the best account we can."
Their opponents, FC Vaduz, qualified as Liechtenstein Football Cup winners but play in the Swiss leagues and were relegated from the Super League in 2016-17. "
People say that we've got the best draw and the easiest draw," Caton said. "
But we're playing against a team that's drawn with FC Basel - a Champions League qualifier - and beaten Young Boys."
Caton has added the experienced former Bangor City and Connah's Quay forward Les Davies and midfielder Eddie Toner to the squad which won the Welsh Cup in April.
And Caton says his side's 2-1 final victory over Welsh Premier champions New Saints will inspire the Lakesiders to achieve further success. "
There's still a massive buzz around the club," Caton told BBC Wales Sport. "
It's important that we all achieve again as a group and try and take it to the next step."
Connah's Quay Nomads manager Andy Morrison is under no illusions how tough a task his side face against Finnish side HJK Helsinki.
Morrison travelled to Finland to watch the Veikkausliga leaders secure their fifth successive victory with a 2-0 win over IFK Mariehamn last Thursday. "
"They're a top, top side," Morrison said. "
I've watched them play and they're very organised, very disciplined, and they move the ball around well.
It's going to be an extremely difficult tie for us, but we'll prepare properly and give it everything we've got."
HJK Helsinki are among the strongest sides in the Nordic region and have dominated Finnish football in recent years.
They will arrive in Wales as heavy favourites, but Morrison believes his Nomads squad has the quality to compete.
The manager has been impressed with his team's consistency throughout the season and feels they can replicate their league form on the European stage.
Bangor City's task against Lyngby BK is similarly daunting, with the Danish outfit having significant experience in European competitions.
However, the Welsh clubs will take heart from the fact that they have home advantage in the first legs, which could prove crucial.
The return fixtures will take place the following week, giving both teams a chance to overturn deficits if necessary.
All three Welsh representatives will be keen to progress and earn lucrative additional matches in the competition.
The prize money and exposure that comes with European football is valuable for clubs of their size.

With the first legs kicking off in the coming weeks, the Welsh football community will be watching closely to see if any of their representatives can pull off surprise results against their more fancied opponents.
The Federal Reserve made it clear that concerns over the strength of the global economy had influenced its decision not to lift rates.
Nine members of its key policymaking committee voted to hold the federal funds rate target at 0 to 0.25%.
One committee member, Jeffrey Lacker, favoured a 0.25 percentage point rise.

In a statement, the Federal Open Market Committee said: "Recent global economic and financial developments may restrain economic activity somewhat and are likely to put further downward pressure on inflation in the near term."
Signs of weaker growth and stock market turmoil in China have led to fears among investors about US economic growth. "
We've long expected to see some slowing in Chinese growth over time as they rebalance their economy.
There are no surprises there.
The question is whether or not there will be a risk of a more abrupt slowdown than most analysts expect," Fed chairwoman Janet Yellen said at a press conference.
There had been intense speculation over the Fed's decision this week, with analysts split over whether it would raise rates now or hold fire.
The Fed's long-term policy is to keep interest rates low until employment levels improve further and the main US inflation rate approaches its 2% target.
Inflation currently remains subdued in the US, kept down by cheaper oil and a strong dollar.
The central bank said that it still wanted to see more improvement in the labour market, even though recent data showed that the unemployment rate for August was 5.1%, the lowest since 2008.
The Fed also wants to be "reasonably confident" that inflation will increase. "

The committee currently anticipates that, even after employment and inflation are near mandate-consistent levels, economic conditions may, for some time, warrant keeping the target federal funds rate below levels the Committee views as normal in the longer run," it said.
The number of Fed policymakers who predicted a rate increase this year dropped to 13 from 15 in June, out of a total of 17.
When the Federal Reserve does start to raise rates, policymakers have forecast slow increases.
The decision to hold steady disappointed some market participants who had been hoping for a rate increase to signal confidence in the US economy.
However, the Fed's cautious approach reflected genuine uncertainty about global economic conditions and the potential spillover effects on American growth.
Investors responded to the announcement with mixed reactions, as some viewed it as prudent risk management while others saw it as a sign of underlying economic weakness.
The postponement of rate increases means that borrowing costs for consumers and businesses will remain near historic lows for the foreseeable future.
This should provide some support for economic activity, though it also raises questions about the Fed's ability to respond if conditions deteriorate further.
The central bank's revised projections suggested that policymakers now expected fewer rate increases over the next two years than they had anticipated just three months earlier.
Janet Yellen emphasized that the Fed was not in a rush and would take a "patient" approach to normalization once it began.
She acknowledged that global developments, particularly in China and other emerging markets, posed genuine risks to the US economy.
The Fed's statement marked a significant shift in tone from its June meeting, when officials had appeared more optimistic about the near-term outlook.
Market analysts quickly began revising their own forecasts, with many pushing back their expectations for the first rate increase to late 2015 or even 2016.
The energy sector, already battered by falling oil prices, welcomed the decision as it would help keep financing costs manageable for struggling companies.
Meanwhile, savers and retirees expressed frustration with the continued low-rate environment, which kept yields on savings accounts and bonds at minimal levels.
The Fed's emphasis on wanting to see more labour market improvement suggested that upcoming employment reports would be closely scrutinized for any signs of weakness.
As markets digested the implications of the decision, attention turned to the upcoming economic data that would inform the Fed's next policy meeting in October.
He said the bill would set a "dangerous precedent" for individuals around the world to sue the US government.
Wednesday's vote was the first time Mr Obama's veto power was overruled.
CIA Director John Brennan agreed that the bill carried "grave implications" for national security.
He added: "The downside is potentially huge."
The Justice Against Sponsors of Terrorism (JASTA) legislation opens the door for victims' families to sue any member of the Saudi government suspected of playing a role in the 9/11 attacks.
Fifteen of the 19 hijackers were Saudi nationals, but the oil-rich kingdom - a key US ally - has denied any role in the attacks, which left nearly 3,000 people dead.

While US intelligence raised suspicions about some of the hijackers' connections, the 9/11 commission found no evidence that senior Saudi officials, or the government as an institution, had funded the attackers.
Mostly this was about showing solidarity with the families of 9/11 victims.
Lawmakers across the board said they deserved their day in court, whatever the "diplomatic discomforts" involved in suing a foreign government.
It would have been difficult to be seen to be voting against them, right after the 15th anniversary of the attacks, and just before an election.
Even President Obama acknowledged that the politics were tough.
But some Congress people share the families' suspicions of a certain degree of Saudi involvement, even if not at the highest levels of government as concluded by the 9/11 Commission.
And they've become more willing to openly question this bedrock Mid-East alliance because of other strains in the relationship, which contributed to the climate in which the vote was held.

The Saudis have lobbied furiously against the bill, outlining in detail the steps they've taken since 2001 to disrupt fundraising for extremists by Islamic charities in the Kingdom, and to establish close counter terrorism cooperation with the US.
The administration is worried the latter in particular might be affected by congress' action.
Almost 3,000 people were killed when they deliberately flew planes they had seized into the World Trade Center and the Pentagon.

# Continuation

Saudi Arabia has already threatened to withdraw investments and sell off American assets worth billions of dollars if the legislation becomes law.
This economic retaliation could have serious consequences for the US economy and financial markets.
The kingdom has also hinted at scaling back military cooperation, which would weaken America's strategic position in the Middle East at a critical time.
Regional analysts worry that damaging relations with Saudi Arabia could push the country closer to other powers like Russia or China.
Furthermore, the bill creates a problematic legal framework that could expose American officials and soldiers to similar lawsuits in foreign courts.
Countries around the world are watching closely to see how this precedent unfolds, and many are already considering retaliatory legislation of their own.
European allies have expressed concerns that the bill undermines the principle of sovereign immunity that has long protected international relations.
The practical implications are equally troubling, as US diplomats may face harassment and legal jeopardy while conducting official business abroad.
Some legal experts argue the bill is poorly drafted and could lead to frivolous lawsuits that clog courts for years.
Nevertheless, supporters of JASTA maintain that justice for victims must take priority over diplomatic niceties and geopolitical calculations.
The families of 9/11 victims have waited fifteen years for answers, and they view this law as their only remaining avenue for accountability.
Moving forward, the question remains whether the administration can repair relations with Saudi Arabia while respecting Congress's decision.
Both sides will need to engage in careful diplomacy to prevent the relationship from deteriorating further.
The long-term consequences of this vote may not become apparent for months or even years.
As Christmas fast approaches, the lists of little girls and boys the world over are expected to be dominated by techy toys - including the return of some familiar favourites.

Special Report: The Technology of Business Viewpoint: Tech needs more women Hi-tech toilets could mean big business TV finds salvation in the zombie apocalypse The workplaces that build Africa's future Is teleworking driving us crazy?
The tech getting disabled people into work It was 1998 when the world first met Furby.
Cute, talkative little creatures, the Furby range featured a revolutionary feature: infrared eyes which meant the toys could communicate with each other.
It could also "listen" to conversations, and would, with a little pat-on-the-head encouragement, be taught to say a selection of words.
At the time, Furby was considered so advanced, that the US government banned the toys from its National Security Agency offices - lest it repeat top secret information to unofficial ears.
Thankfully, there were no confirmed reports of intelligence leaks, and years later, the Furby is making a comeback.

This time, its infrared eyes have been replaced with two small LCD screens, its body has more motors and sensors than before and - here's the clever bit - the Furby of 2012 comes with its own smartphone and tablet app, allowing for a much more interactive experience.
The app will even translate Furby's warblings into English. "
We've reinvented Furby with advanced technology that brings a whole new way to play to kids," the company gushed. "
The personality of each Furby appears to evolve by the way children play with it, and every unpredictable action and reaction helps make each Furby seem unique."
But Furby certainly isn't the only toy to be making use of mobile apps to enhance the play experience, says John Baulch, publisher of Toy World magazine. "
The best of these app toys, they really do significantly enhance the play value of what you get out of it," he says. "
It adds a real wow factor to toys.
It's what kids really want these days." "
Toys have always harnessed new technology - because kids are getting more technologically savvy and literate."

# Continuation

From robotic dogs that respond to voice commands to interactive dolls that connect to cloud-based platforms, the integration of digital technology into traditional toys has become increasingly sophisticated.
Manufacturers recognize that today's children have grown up surrounded by smartphones and tablets, making tech-enhanced toys feel like a natural extension of their digital lives.
However, this trend also raises important questions about screen time and the balance between physical and digital play.
Parents and educators worry that an over-reliance on app-based toys might diminish the creative, imaginative play that has long been considered crucial for child development.
Yet proponents argue that when designed thoughtfully, these toys can actually encourage learning and problem-solving skills in ways that traditional toys cannot.
The market data certainly supports the popularity of connected toys, with sales projections showing double-digit growth year after year.
Companies are investing heavily in research and development to create toys that feel intuitive and engaging to digitally-native children.
Security and privacy concerns, however, remain at the forefront of consumer consciousness, particularly given the amount of personal data these connected devices can collect.
Manufacturers are increasingly aware that they must address parental concerns about data protection and safe online interactions.
The success of a tech toy ultimately depends on whether it offers genuine added value beyond the novelty of the technology itself.
Looking ahead, experts predict that augmented reality and artificial intelligence will play even larger roles in toy design.
The line between traditional toys and digital entertainment will likely continue to blur, reshaping childhood play for generations to come.
Kim Jong-il, North Korea's "Dear Leader", was in the process of grooming Kim Jong-un as his successor when he died on 17 December 2011.
Immediately after his father's death, the younger Kim was hailed as "the great successor".
He was named head of the party, state and army within a fortnight of his father's death.
In January 2016, Mr Kim shot to global headlines again when he oversaw what the regime claimed was an underground hydrogen bomb test - its second nuclear test since he came to power.
Previously, his most high-profile act had been to purge and execute his uncle, Chang Song-thaek, who state media said had been plotting a coup, in December 2013.
Little is still known about the elusive young man who is the youngest son of Kim Jong-il and his late third wife Ko Yong-hui.
Born in 1983 or early 1984, he was initially not thought to be in the frame to take up his father's mantle.
Analysts focused their attention on his half-brother Kim Jong-nam and older full brother Kim Jong-chol.
However Kim Jong-nam's deportation from Japan in May 2001 and middle brother Kim Jong-chol's apparent "unmanliness" improved his chances.
Analysts saw him as the coming man after he was awarded a series of high-profile political posts.
Swiss-educated like his brothers, Kim Jong-un avoided Western influences, returning home when not in school and dining out with the North Korean ambassador.
After his return to Pyongyang, he is known to have attended the Kim Il-sung Military University.
His mother was thought to be Kim Jong-il's favourite wife, and she clearly doted on her son, reportedly calling him the "Morning Star King".
In his 2003 book, I Was Kim Jong-il's Chef, a Japanese man writing under the pseudonym Kenji Fujimoto also claimed that Kim Jong-un was his father's favourite.
In August 2010 Kim Jong-il visited China.
One South Korean TV station cited a South Korean official as saying Kim Jong-un had accompanied his father on the trip.

# Continuation

This visit was seen as a significant moment in Kim Jong-un's political development, exposing him to international diplomacy at a crucial juncture.
Upon returning to North Korea, he was rapidly elevated through the ranks of the military and party hierarchy.
By September 2010, just weeks after the China trip, he had been appointed to the powerful National Defence Commission, a position that typically signals preparation for supreme leadership.
His ascension was formalized at the rare Party Congress held that same month, where he was made a four-star general despite his youth and lack of military experience.
The speed of his rise surprised many observers who had expected a longer transition period.
State media began promoting him as a visionary leader who would usher North Korea into a new era of prosperity and strength.
Portraits of the young Kim Jong-un started appearing alongside those of his grandfather Kim Il-sung and father Kim Jong-il, completing the trinity of revolutionary leadership.
His appointment also signaled that the regime intended to maintain dynastic rule despite international pressure and economic hardship.
In the months following his father's death, Kim Jong-un moved swiftly to consolidate his power base and eliminate potential rivals.
He demonstrated a willingness to act decisively against those he perceived as threats to his authority.
His execution of his uncle Chang Song-thaek sent a clear message that he would tolerate no challenges to his rule, however close the family connection.
This ruthlessness, combined with his youth and relative inexperience, made him an unpredictable and potentially dangerous leader on the world stage.
A simple cut to your finger could leave you fighting for your life.
Luck will play a bigger role in your future than any doctor could.
The most basic operations - getting an appendix removed or a hip replacement - could become deadly.
Cancer treatments and organ transplants could kill you.
Childbirth could once again become a deadly moment in a woman's life.
It's a future without antibiotics.
Sir Alexander Fleming made one of the single greatest contributions to medicine when he discovered antibiotics.
He noticed that mould growing on his culture dishes had created a ring free of bacteria, he'd found penicillin.
It was the stuff of Nobel Prizes, but in 1945 the spectre of resistance was already there.

In his winner's lecture he said: "It is not difficult to make microbes resistant to penicillin in the laboratory by exposing them to concentrations not sufficient to kill them, and the same thing has occasionally happened in the body. "
The time may come when penicillin can be bought by anyone in the shops. "
Then there is the danger that the ignorant man may easily underdose himself and by exposing his microbes to non-lethal quantities of the drug make them resistant."

Sir Alexander Fleming's 1945 Nobel Prize lecture BBC History: Alexander Fleming This might read like the plot of a science fiction novel - but there is genuine fear that the world is heading into a post-antibiotic era.
The World Health Organization has warned that "many common infections will no longer have a cure and, once again, could kill unabated".
The US Centers of Disease Control has pointed to the emergence of "nightmare bacteria".
And the chief medical officer for England Prof Dame Sally Davies has evoked parallels with the "apocalypse".
Fleming's warning, issued nearly eighty years ago, has proven prophetic in ways he could scarcely have imagined.
Today, bacteria are evolving faster than new drugs can be developed, creating a dangerous gap in our medical arsenal.
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, or MRSA, stalks hospital corridors, resistant to multiple antibiotics.
Tuberculosis strains have emerged that respond to virtually no available treatment.
The misuse of antibiotics—in livestock farming, in human medicine, in agriculture—has accelerated this evolutionary arms race dramatically.
Every time we take an antibiotic unnecessarily, we give bacteria another chance to develop defenses.
Every time a patient stops taking prescribed antibiotics early because they feel better, they leave behind the strongest survivors to multiply.
In developing nations, counterfeit and substandard antibiotics flood the market, exposing bacteria to ineffective doses that breed resistance.
Even in wealthy countries, antibiotics are prescribed for viral infections where they have no effect, simply to satisfy patient expectations.

The problem is compounded by the fact that pharmaceutical companies have little financial incentive to develop new antibiotics, which are used briefly and generate less profit than chronic disease medications.
Time is running out, and the window for action is narrowing.

Without immediate and coordinated global action—stricter regulations on antibiotic use, investment in new drug development, improved infection control—we may indeed find ourselves facing a future where medicine's greatest miracle becomes powerless.
The infections that once killed millions before Fleming's discovery could reclaim that terrible role.

We stand at a crossroads, and the path we choose now will determine whether humanity enters a new dark age of infectious disease, or whether we manage to stay one step ahead of the microbes that share our world.
Foraging teacher Mark Williams reckons we should eat more weeds.
He is urging shoppers to turn their backs on "tasteless imported Spanish lettuce" and instead head into the wild to fill our salad bowls.
Even in February, he says there are dozens of alternatives to shop-bought greens growing in abundance along the coast, in woodland and even in towns and cities.
And he has drawn up a list of six plants - or are they weeds? -
which could grace our dinner plates tonight.
Mr Williams, who lives in Dumfries and Galloway and runs foraging courses, said: "None of them are in any way rare, in fact most are considered weeds. "
People expect foraging to be about looking for (possible rare) things, but it's really just about recognising abundance. "
Throughout the year, I can pick well over 300 different edible wild plants, fungi and seaweed within walking distance of my home in Gatehouse. "
Even in towns and cities, the list is huge."

The forager, who also runs a website offering advice on sourcing wild food safely and responsibly, says: "People worry about poisoning themselves by misidentifying something, but it only takes a tiny bit of effort to learn the few things to avoid. "
More people get ill from eating mass produced commodities than from eating the wrong wild plant.
Mr Williams says: "Provided you follow the first cardinal rule of foraging, nothing can go wrong: never eat anything that you can't identify with 100% confidence, and know is safe to eat."
If you have ever detected the smell of garlic as you wander through woodland in the springtime, it is likely you passed by a crop of wild garlic.
These fragrant, small green leaves are abundant in open, well-established deciduous woodland and shady hedgerows.
They have been at the forefront of a renaissance in wild food in recent years, according to Mr Williams.
He said: "It isn't hard to work out why: it is easy to find, delicious and fairly straightforward to identify. "

# Continuation

The plant's distinctive pungent aroma is virtually unmistakable, making it nearly impossible to confuse with anything dangerous.
Wild garlic can be used in virtually any dish where you would normally use cultivated garlic, from pestos to soups to simple salads.
Beyond wild garlic, Mr Williams' list includes chickweed, a delicate plant with tiny white flowers that thrives in shaded, damp areas throughout winter.
Chickweed has a mild, slightly sweet flavour and can be eaten raw in salads or lightly cooked as a nutritious green vegetable.
Another recommendation is fat hen, which despite its ungainly name is packed with vitamins and minerals, offering far greater nutritional value than many supermarket alternatives.
Dandelion leaves, though often dismissed as a nuisance by gardeners, provide a pleasantly bitter note to salads and have been consumed for centuries across Europe.
Sorrel, with its distinctive arrow-shaped leaves and lemony tang, adds a refreshing zing to any meal and grows prolifically in meadows and grassland.
Ground elder, sometimes called bishop's weed, is another abundant plant that tastes remarkably similar to spinach when young and tender.
Mr Williams emphasises that the key to successful foraging is starting with these common, easily identifiable plants before attempting anything more adventurous.
He suggests that beginners should invest time in learning to recognise plants in different seasons, as appearance can vary considerably throughout the year.
Local foraging groups and online communities can provide invaluable support and verification for anyone just starting out on their wild food journey.
The environmental benefits of foraging are also significant, as it reduces reliance on energy-intensive agricultural systems and unnecessary food miles.
By reconnecting with the natural abundance around us, we not only improve our diet but also deepen our understanding of local ecosystems.
Mr Williams believes this shift towards wild foraging represents a return to more sustainable, mindful eating practices that could benefit both individuals and the planet.
A US-led coalition of of Western and Middle Eastern countries began air strikes against the group in Iraq in August 2014 and in Syria a month later.
The UK began striking IS targets in Iraq in September 2014 and extended its involvement to Syria in December 2015.
Russia began carrying out its own air strikes in Syria in September 2015 after a request from Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, who has clung on to power despite more than four years of civil war.
Here we look at where the key countries involved in the conflict stand.
The US air force has carried out the majority of air strikes against IS targets since forming a coalition of Western and regional powers in August 2014.

President Barack Obama is extremely reluctant to send ground troops to fight IS after protracted and unpopular wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, but the US does have about 3,500 troops in Iraq to train the country's armed forces.
The US has also provided weapons and training to "moderate" Syrian rebel groups, and unconfirmed reports suggest US special forces have been fighting alongside anti-IS forces in both Iraq and Syria.
In October US Defence Secretary Ashton Carter signalled a possible shift in the US campaign against IS, telling reporters that US forces were prepared to engage in "direct action on the ground".
Mr Carter did not go into detail about the circumstances under which the US might carry out ground operations against IS, but said: "Once we locate them, no target is beyond our reach."
Regional Sunni power Saudi Arabia is part of US-led military action against IS targets in Syria.
Riyadh also agreed to a US request to provide a base to train moderate Syrian rebel forces.
The kingdom has been a key supporter of the rebels, including hardline Islamist groups, but it has rejected an Iranian accusation that it has directly supported IS.
However, wealthy Saudis have sent donations to the group and some 2,500 Saudi men have travelled to Syria to fight.
The Saudi authorities are concerned that IS will inspire Saudi jihadists to challenge the monarchy's legitimacy and seek to overthrow it.

In July 2014, Riyadh deployed 30,000 troops to beef up security along its border with Iraq, and the following month hosted Iran's deputy foreign minister as the two regional rivals agreed to co-operate.

Jordan, a staunch US ally, said it had joined the US and several Gulf Arab states in carrying out air strikes on IS militants in Syria in September 2014 to "ensure the stability and security" of its borders.
Jordan hosts thousands of Iraqi and Syrian refugees fleeing the conflict, putting significant strain on its economy and infrastructure.
The country has also experienced IS attacks on its own soil, including the bombing of a military base that killed several Jordanian soldiers.
Despite these threats, Jordan remains committed to the international coalition and continues to conduct military operations against IS targets.
Turkey, a NATO member and key strategic ally, has a complicated relationship with the anti-IS campaign.

While officially part of the coalition, Turkey's primary concern has been the Kurdish forces fighting IS in Syria and Iraq, viewing them as linked to the Kurdistan Workers Party, which it designates as a terrorist organization.
This has led to tensions with the US, which supports Kurdish fighters as effective ground forces against IS.
Turkey has also faced criticism for allegedly allowing foreign jihadists to cross its borders into Syria and for not sufficiently securing its frontier.
France intensified its involvement in the campaign following the devastating terrorist attacks in Paris in November 2015, which killed 130 people and were claimed by IS.
The French government launched its own air strikes in Syria and Iraq, vowing to increase military pressure on the militant group.
France has also deployed special forces advisors to support Kurdish and Iraqi forces on the ground.
Iraq itself remains central to the conflict, with the government in Baghdad struggling to maintain control over territory lost to IS while also managing tensions between Sunni and Shia communities.
The Iraqi military has been gradually rebuilt with international support but faces significant challenges in coordinating with various militia groups and balancing regional interests.
Iran, though not officially part of the Western-led coalition, has been actively supporting Iraqi forces and Shia militias fighting against IS, giving it considerable influence in Iraq's future.
An avid drawer of cartoons as a child, he was determined to become a professional cartoonist.
So, aged 19, Mauricio left his small home town and moved to Sao Paulo, Brazil's largest city, to pursue his dream.
Intending to produce cartoons for a newspaper, he was instead repeatedly turned down.
He was told that his work wasn't good enough.

But not a person to be deterred, the young Mauricio came up with a plan - he'd get a different job on a newspaper, while continuing to practise and improve his skills as a cartoonist in his spare time.
His thinking was that once he was more established at a paper, he'd then try again - and again - to get his cartoons accepted.
So despite the sight of blood making him feel faint, in 1954 Mauricio got a job as a crime reporter on one of Brazil's bestselling titles - Folha de Sao Paulo. "
Of course it was not the start I dreamed about, but we can not be picky when we are starting, and I had to do what they allowed me to do," says Mauricio, who is now 80 years old.
He had to wait five years before he got a cartoon accepted.
That first daily comic strip was about a dog who engaged in philosophical conversations.
It was popular enough for Mauricio to be able to quit reporting, and become a full-time cartoonist.
Almost six decades later, Mauricio has sold more than one billion comics and books.
A household name in Brazil, his work is also translated and sold around the world.

In addition, his company - Mauricio de Sousa Productions (MSP) - produces animated films and theatre productions, runs a theme park, makes computer games and cuddly toys, and licenses his characters to hundreds of consumer products.
While Mauricio doesn't like to discuss money, he is a multi-millionaire many times over.
He is also a workaholic who still leads the business every day, and has no plans to retire. "

# Continuation

"I love what I do too much to stop," Mauricio explains with a characteristic smile.
His passion for creating has never dimmed, even after decades of success.
Every morning, he arrives at his studio with the same enthusiasm he had as a struggling young cartoonist in Sao Paulo.
His team of artists and writers, numbering in the hundreds, work alongside him to bring new stories and characters to life.
The most famous of these characters, Monica, has become an iconic figure recognized by millions across generations.
Her mischievous personality and adventures have resonated with audiences far beyond Brazil's borders.
Mauricio credits much of his longevity to staying connected with his audience and understanding what they want.
He reads fan mail regularly and takes feedback seriously, constantly evolving his work to remain relevant.
Despite his vast empire, he remains humble about his achievements, often attributing his success to hard work and persistence rather than talent alone.
He frequently gives talks to young aspiring artists, encouraging them to never give up on their dreams.
His message is simple: rejection is not failure, but merely a stepping stone on the path to success.
Mauricio's journey from a rejected cartoonist to a global phenomenon serves as an inspiration to creative professionals everywhere.
He has proven that with determination, adaptability, and unwavering belief in oneself, even the smallest dreams can grow into something extraordinary.
Today, at 80, Mauricio de Sousa remains a testament to the power of perseverance and the boundless possibilities that await those willing to work for their vision.
Thousands of children were relocated to Australia and parts of the British Empire up to 1974.
Many experienced "unacceptable depravity", the first hearing in the independent inquiry into child sexual abuse in England and Wales has heard.
One migrant, David Hill, called for perpetrators to be "named and shamed".

Meanwhile the Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse (IICSA) has apologised and referred itself to the information commissioner after mistakenly sending out confidential information relating to abuse victims.
Mr Hill was 12 when he was sent with his two brothers to the Fairbridge Farm School in Western Australia.
He told the hearing: "We'll never be able to undo the great wrong that was done to these children. "
But what is important to the survivors of sexual abuse is where this inquiry is satisfied with the evidence - name the villains. "
Many of them are beyond the grave and therefore beyond the law. "
But it would bring a great deal of the comfort to the people who as children were victims of these people if they were named and shamed."
Inquiry counsel Henrietta Hill QC said claims of "systematic sexual abuse" in institutions and work environments would be heard throughout the inquiry.
The children, she said, were sent without consent of parents, wrongly told they were orphans, and denied basic details about their family backgrounds during their future lives.
For the government, Samantha Leek QC said: "Child migration is wrong.
It should not have been sanctioned or facilitated... "The lifelong consequences for those involved are a matter of deep and sincere regret."
In 2009, the Australian government apologised for the cruelty shown to the child migrants.
Britain also made an apology in 2010.
The apology contained no specific mention of sexual abuse.
The inquiry is expected to hear from dozens of survivors of child migration schemes over the coming months, many of whom are now in their seventies and eighties.
Their testimonies will document not only the physical and emotional trauma they endured, but also the lasting psychological scars that have followed them into adulthood.

Experts have noted that many survivors struggled with trust, relationships, and mental health issues throughout their lives, a direct result of the institutional abuse they suffered as vulnerable children.
The Fairbridge Farm School, where Mr Hill was sent, has become emblematic of the systemic failures that allowed such abuse to flourish unchecked for decades.
Archival records show that complaints were often ignored or dismissed by authorities, and staff members who perpetrated abuse were frequently transferred rather than prosecuted.
The inquiry aims to establish whether there was institutional knowledge of these abuses at higher levels of government and charitable organizations.
Several religious institutions that facilitated child migration schemes have already faced separate investigations and lawsuits in both Australia and Britain.
Survivors have expressed frustration that previous apologies, while acknowledged, lacked the accountability and specific naming of perpetrators that might bring closure.
The IICSA's data breach regarding confidential victim information has added another layer of concern for survivors already traumatized by breaches of trust.
The inquiry has committed to implementing stronger safeguards to protect sensitive information moving forward.
Advocates for the survivors have called for a comprehensive review of all child migration policies and the individuals who authorized them.
Some campaigners are pushing for financial compensation packages that reflect the lifelong harm caused by these schemes.
The hearing marks a significant moment in Britain's reckoning with one of its most shameful chapters of institutional child abuse.
But plans to develop wind power and export it to Britain are sparking a rural revolt, with local protest groups uniting through social media.
Some claim Ireland will become a wind farm for Britain.

Turbines up to 180m (590ft) tall - half the height of the Empire State Building - are cropping up as part of a drive to meet EU green energy targets and generate 40% of Ireland's electricity from wind.
Revenue, too, will flow by exporting power to Britain under inter-governmental plans.
More than 100 opposition groups have sprung up against new wind farms - all in rural areas - claiming the turbines will ruin the landscape, with noise and shadows blighting homes nearby.
Some have joined forces with groups opposing planned new electricity pylons to upgrade the national power grid.
Wind energy companies say they follow strict Irish planning guidelines, now under review due to new larger turbines and local opposition.
A country pub in County Laois is packed on a Monday night for a meeting of the Laois Energy Awareness Group.
Ray Conroy helped set it up, he says, after energy companies suddenly appeared, doing deals with some locals for turbine sites. "
The first we knew was when our local authorities and government announces 'wey hey - wind farms are coming, and it's a boom for everyone'.
It's nonsense," he says. "
Are we going to ruin our environment to save the UK's environment?
There's nothing in it for the citizens of this island.
The only winners are going to be multinational corporations who are sucking subsidies from the UK taxpayers. "
They're ruining our landscape.
There might be some tax revenue in it for our government but, overall, what price are we willing to pay?"

# Continuation

Conroy's frustration reflects a broader sentiment among rural Irish communities who feel excluded from decisions affecting their futures.
The protest groups argue that while corporations and governments benefit financially, ordinary residents bear the environmental and quality-of-life costs.
They point out that many turbine agreements were negotiated directly with individual landowners, bypassing broader community consultation.
Local activists have begun documenting complaints about noise disturbances, property devaluations, and the visual impact of industrial-scale structures dominating previously pristine countryside.
Some villages have become divided, with neighbours split between those receiving payments for hosting turbines and those living nearby without compensation.
The government has defended its wind expansion strategy as essential for meeting climate commitments and reducing carbon emissions.
However, critics question whether exporting renewable energy to Britain while sacrificing Irish landscapes represents a fair trade-off for the nation.

Environmental groups find themselves in an awkward position, supporting renewable energy in principle while sympathizing with locals' environmental concerns about habitat disruption and landscape preservation.
The controversy has prompted several local councils to tighten planning restrictions and demand more rigorous environmental impact assessments.
Meanwhile, energy companies argue that modern turbines are quieter and more efficient than older models, and that opposition often stems from misinformation rather than genuine environmental concern.
The debate has become increasingly polarized, with social media amplifying both sides' arguments and occasionally spreading exaggerated claims.
Industry representatives suggest that compromise solutions—such as community benefit schemes and improved setback distances from homes—could address legitimate concerns.
Yet trust between developers and rural communities appears severely damaged, making negotiations increasingly difficult.
As the standoff continues, the Irish government faces mounting pressure to balance its renewable energy ambitions against growing public discontent in the countryside.
It could bring an end to eight months of uncertainty for thousands of workers who faced losing their jobs when Tata's UK business was put up for sale.
Tata held talks with unions on Wednesday and said it was "an important step forward" for its future in the UK.
But workers still have to agree to pensions changes and will be balloted.
Almost 7,000 people are employed by Tata Steel across Wales, including more than 4,000 in Port Talbot.
A number of "significant" measures include: Steel worker Gary Keogh said: "In March we were told we were being sold off and not wanted in the family but now we're a bit more positive. "
It's very complex the pensions issue, every individual has to decide how it works out for them.
It's different for everyone financially."
Speaking after meeting with union representatives, Roy Rickhuss of Community Union said: "The past year has been incredibly difficult for steelworkers and their families. "
When Tata announced in March that they planned to sell the steelworks, no one knew if they would have a job by Christmas. "
This proposal would secure jobs for years to come and bring serious investment not just to Port Talbot but to steelworks across the UK. "

Reaching this stage of the process is a credit to the hard work of our members who never gave up the fight to 'Save Our Steel' - it was their jobs on the line and it has been their campaign that has brought Tata to this position."
But he added the pension proposal was "a serious concern" with union representatives agreeing to ballot all members on the proposal in the new year.
Analysis by Sarah Dickins, BBC Wales economics correspondent Tata's workforce may well have fallen to 6,300 in Wales but it is calculated to inject about £200m a year in wages alone.
On top of that there are thousands of other jobs dependent on the steel company and a supply chain estimated to be worth £3.3bn a year to Wales.
Economists say Tata brings "heft" and with the sort of jobs which are very hard to find elsewhere.
Unite's national officer Tony Brady described the move as "a step in the right direction for our industry" but warned there was "still a lot more that government can and must do". "
He emphasized that while this agreement represents progress, the steel industry requires sustained support beyond this single deal.
The government has been under mounting pressure to intervene and protect what remains a strategically important industry for the Welsh economy.
Union leaders are now focused on ensuring members understand the pension changes before the ballot takes place in January.
The modifications to the pension scheme are expected to be less generous than the current arrangements, which has raised concerns among older workers nearing retirement.
However, union representatives argue that securing jobs is the priority, as redundancies would have been catastrophic for Port Talbot and surrounding communities.
The sale process itself remains ongoing, with potential buyers still being evaluated by Tata's advisors.
Some industry observers have suggested that the agreement could serve as a template for other struggling steel producers across Europe facing similar challenges.
Workers expressed cautious optimism that the worst of the crisis may have passed, though many remain nervous about what the future holds.
Local politicians have called for the government to provide additional support to modernize facilities and improve competitiveness in global markets.
The outcome of the pension ballot will be crucial in determining whether this deal can finally be concluded.
If approved, it could pave the way for a formal sale announcement within the coming months.
The steelworkers' campaign has drawn international attention to the plight of the UK steel industry and its importance to regional economies.
Stoke scored three in eight minutes, Mame Biram Diouf netting a brace and Jonathan Walters also on target.
Ex-Liverpool midfielder Charlie Adam made it 4-0 from 20 yards before Steven Nzonzi added the fifth from 25 yards.
Media playback is not supported on this device Gerrard got one back before substitute Peter Crouch headed Stoke's sixth.
This was no way for a player of Gerrard's stature to bow out.
The Los Angeles Galaxy-bound midfielder managed to mark his 710th and final appearance for the Reds with a 186th goal.
But the 34-year-old was badly let down by his team-mates who were out-classed and out-witted from start to finish.
Liverpool finish the season sixth in the table, enough to secure a Europa League third qualifying round spot.
But boss Brendan Rodgers, who dropped young forward Raheem Sterling to the bench after he turned down a new contract, clearly has his work cut out this summer to rebuild his team.
In contrast, Stoke end a productive campaign ninth in the table after a thumping win.
Mark Hughes's rampant side showed no mercy as they easily swept aside Liverpool in an astonishing first-half display.
Diouf opened the floodgates in the 22nd minute after Simon Mignolet parried Adam's powerful drive into the striker's path.
Media playback is not supported on this device It was 2-0 four minutes later, Diouf letting fly from the edge of the area after being afforded far too much time and space.
Liverpool looked shell-shocked when Walters made it 3-0 on the half hour after poor defending by Emre Can allowed the Stoke striker to pounce from close range despite Mignolet's best efforts.
The Reds were a shambles, Adam making it 4-0 after pouncing on a mistake by Lucas Leiva to power home from 20 yards.
It was Steven Nzonzi's who came up with the goal of the game, the French midfielder making it 5-0 from long distance on the stroke of half time.
Rodgers sent on Kolo Toure and Jordon Ibe in an effort to tighten up the defence at the start of the second half.
The changes made little difference to Liverpool's plight as Stoke continued to dominate proceedings with ease.
Gerrard's consolation goal in the 73rd minute provided scant comfort for the departing captain and the travelling Kop supporters.
Crouch's towering header six minutes later merely added insult to injury, extending Stoke's lead to 6-1 in what had become a one-sided affair.
The Liverpool defence was in complete disarray throughout, with communication breaking down repeatedly and positional awareness virtually non-existent.
Mignolet had little chance with most of the goals, left exposed by the shambles unfolding in front of him.
For Stoke, it was a statement of intent about their ambitions for the coming season and Hughes could hardly have asked for a more convincing performance.
The victory capped off a strong campaign for the Potters, who had proven themselves capable of competing with the Premier League's biggest names.
As the final whistle blew, there was a stark contrast between the jubilant home supporters and the dejected Liverpool fans witnessing their club's decline.
Gerrard's exit, while marked by his individual contribution, was overshadowed by the collective failure of his teammates to show any fight or resilience.
The midfielder's move to MLS represented the end of an era at Anfield, though few could have imagined it would conclude in such a humiliating fashion.
Liverpool's recruitment and tactical decisions would clearly need overhauling if they were to challenge for honours again next season.
The Europa League qualification offered little consolation given the manner of this defeat and the evident gaps in the squad that required urgent addressing.

Murray's defeat was only his third in 32 Davis Cup singles matches.
GB must win Saturday's doubles and both Sunday's singles to reach the final.
Murray is scheduled to team up with his brother Jamie for the doubles contest but admitted it would be "really tough" for him to recover following the longest match of his career on Friday.
The winners of the best-of-five tie will face France or Croatia in the final, with that semi-final level at 1-1 after day one.

The world number two hit 35 aces and battled through the longest match of either man's career, but Murray came up short against an inspired Del Potro, who lost their four-hour Olympic final last month. "
I won but I still remember the Olympic final," said Del Potro. "
This is very important to me and my team and we are looking to reach another final in the Davis Cup. "
Against Andy you never know when the match will finish.
He is a dangerous player, a fighter and great champion.
In end I played good forehands and good serves and that was key."
Murray said: "I think it was good match.
I felt there wasn't too many mistakes on important moments. "
Both of us came up with good stuff and the shot he came up with to break in the fifth set was the difference."

Media playback is not supported on this device Media playback is not supported on this device Del Potro hit 26 forehand winners and broke serve six times to join Stan Wawrinka and Fabio Fognini as the only men to have beaten Murray in Davis Cup singles matches.

The Argentine, ranked 64th as he works his way back after three wrist surgeries, was the better player for the first two hours and earned a break point that would have left him serving for a two-set lead.

# Continuation

However, Murray fought back brilliantly to level the match before the pair traded sets in an exhausting battle of attrition.

Del Potro's experience in high-pressure situations proved decisive in the crucial moments, particularly during the fifth-set tiebreaker where his aggressive play finally wore down the British champion.
Murray had numerous opportunities to close out the match earlier but was unable to capitalize on his chances against an opponent playing with remarkable intensity and precision.
The Argentine's comeback from his previous injuries was on full display as he demonstrated the quality that once saw him reach the US Open final and compete at the highest levels of tennis.
For Great Britain, the loss placed enormous pressure on the doubles pairing and the remaining singles matches on Sunday.
The team knew that anything less than a perfect performance over the next two days would see them eliminated from the competition.
Jamie Murray would need to provide crucial support to his brother, who would be physically and mentally drained from the grueling encounter.
France and Croatia were also battling fiercely in their semi-final, suggesting that whoever emerged from this tie would face formidable opposition in the final.
The Davis Cup format had proven once again why it remained one of tennis's most compelling and unpredictable competitions.
Del Potro's victory vindicated his decision to push through the pain of his rehabilitation and return to elite-level competition.
For Murray, the defeat served as a stark reminder that even the greatest champions can fall short against determined opponents on their day.

He and Cuban leader Raul Castro agreed to normalise diplomatic relations in December 2014 and in August 2015 the US re-opened its embassy in Havana, more than half a century after it had closed following Fidel Castro's Cuban revolution.
Now reports say that President Donald Trump may announce his new Cuba policy as soon as next Friday.
Many are expecting a rollback on the dÃ©tente including a tightening of the rules on travel and trade eased by the previous administration.
What are the main issues at stake?
This is the area perhaps most at risk.
Every year since the thaw was announced at the end of 2014 has been a record in terms of visitor numbers to the island.
In no small part that is down to the large number of US citizens who have been able to come to Cuba since tight travel restrictions were eased.

Opponents of the engagement policy want Mr Trump to adopt proposals which include tightening the 12 categories of travel under which Americans have been coming to Cuba - including the catch-all "people-to-people" exchanges.
This would mean US visitors might again face fines if they visit the island without the right paperwork.
The potential effects of such a move on Cuba's tourism industry would be significant.
However the pro-engagement lobby group Engage Cuba argues that it would have a multi-billion dollar impact on the US economy too, affecting jobs in the airline and cruise ship industries.
Despite the improved relations, the US economic embargo on Cuba remains firmly in place.
As such, bilateral trade between the United States and Cuba is yet to get off the ground.
There have been some initial steps:.
In January a shipment of artisanal charcoal became the first legal Cuban export to the US in decades.
There was also an agreement for the US hotel company, Starwood, to run three hotels in Havana.

# Continuation

Proponents of normalisation hope that a full lifting of the embargo could eventually open up significant trade opportunities between the two nations.
However, hardline Republicans in Congress have consistently blocked attempts to ease or remove the embargo, viewing it as a crucial leverage point in negotiations with the Cuban government.
The issue remains deeply polarized along political lines, with some arguing that engagement is the most effective path to promoting democratic reform on the island.
Others maintain that economic pressure is necessary to hold the Castro regime accountable for its human rights record.
Beyond tourism and trade, the question of property seized during the revolution continues to complicate relations.
Many Cuban-Americans whose assets were nationalised decades ago are pushing for compensation or restoration of their properties.
The Trump administration's stance on these historical grievances could significantly influence the broader direction of US-Cuba policy.
Observers also note that geopolitical considerations play a role, with some arguing that closer ties to Cuba could counter Chinese and Russian influence in the Caribbean region.
Educational and cultural exchanges, which had begun to flourish under the Obama administration, may also face restrictions if the new policy takes a harder line.
The timing of any policy announcement could also affect how the international community perceives American commitment to diplomatic engagement.
Cuba itself has expressed concern about potential reversals, with government officials warning that any rollback could destabilize the fragile progress made in recent years.
What ultimately emerges from the Trump administration's review will likely depend on whether economic and diplomatic considerations outweigh ideological objections to the Castro regime.
